title: A cut to the thumb of a 22-year-old man
patientProfile: 22-year-old male orchard labourer and fruit picker, right-handed. Injured his left thumb at work an hour ago while pruning fruit trees; the pruning knife slipped and cut his left thumb through cotton gloves.
chiefComplaint: Cut to left thumb at work
candidateInfo: You are the Hospital Medical Officer (HMO) in a hospital Emergency Department. The patient injured his left thumb at work an hour ago. He is aged 22 years and works as an orchard labourer and fruit picker. He is right handed. He was pruning fruit trees today and the pruning knife slipped and he cut his left thumb. He was wearing cotton gloves. The knife cut through the glove and cut the thumb as shown in the illustration. Bleeding was minor and controlled by a pressure dressing, which has been removed for examination. The wound appears as a linear knife cut, the edges of which have sealed after the initial bleeding which has now stopped.
tasks: Examine him and assess the injury.
Explain to him the nature of the injury and your recommended management.
You may ask other questions of the patient as you proceed with the examination and explanation.
openingStatement: Will it be okay for me to go back to work tomorrow with a dressing over it now it's stopped bleeding, Doc?
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient presents with a linear knife cut to the left thumb sustained while pruning fruit trees at work. Bleeding was minor and has stopped. Patient has not yet noticed any limitation of thumb movement.
historyWithPrompting: If asked about tetanus: had a tetanus booster shot about a year ago for a leg graze and was immunised against tetanus as a child. If asked about tendon injury and normal sensation: patient is unable to extend each of the two end joints of the thumb when specifically asked; sensation is normal.
patientQuestions: Will I need an anaesthetic? (if tendon injury diagnosed and operation recommended)
Will I need another tetanus shot? (if no mention of tetanus prophylaxis or antibiotics)
physicalExaminationFindings: Linear knife cut to the left thumb at the knuckle level of the metacarpophalangeal joint. Patient is unable to actively extend either the metacarpophalangeal (MP) joint or the interphalangeal (IP) joint of the thumb. Digital cutaneous nerves have not been cut; distal sensation is normal apart from tenderness around the cut. Both extensor pollicis longus (EPL) and extensor pollicis brevis (EPB) have been severed.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to use clinical reasoning skills to diagnose and manage important injuries associated with skin wounds. In this instance, the knife cut has severed the two extensor tendons to the thumb.
expectationsOfPerformance: Cuts and stabs of various types commonly present to emergency departments. Attending doctors must be aware of the anatomy of deeper structures at risk from injuries at specific sites and the methods of diagnosing such injuries.
Diagnosis of injury to the two main long thumb tendons and recognition of the treatment requirements for primary surgical repair in this 'tidy' (clean contaminated) wound.
Explanation of treatment would optimally advise preparation for early surgery using local (field) block or general anaesthesia.
Antibiotic and tetanus prophylaxis would be appropriate.
keyIssues: Ability to identify deeper tendon injuries resulting from stabs or cuts.
Failure to appreciate that the whole thumb extensor mechanism (involving two tendons) has been damaged would comprise a fail (unsatisfactory) in the domains of examination technique and diagnosis.
Failure to name the tendons correctly would not necessarily be a fail performance, providing the presence of tendon injury was diagnosed and appropriate advice given in other areas.
Failure to mention antibiotic or tetanus prophylaxis would be unsatisfactory, but would not be considered a critical error in the presence of a 'tidy' recent wound.
criticalErrors: Failure to test and identify the injury to the extensor tendons would comprise a clear and irremediable fail for this station at a very unsatisfactory level.
commentary: The knife cut has severed the two extensor tendons to the left thumb (extensor pollicis brevis and extensor pollicis longus, from radial to ulnar side). These tendons form the margins of the anatomical snuff box. The tendons have been severed at the knuckle level of the metacarpophalangeal joint. The patient has no obvious thumb deformity but is unable actively to extend either the metacarpophalangeal (MP) joint or the interphalangeal (IP) joint of the thumb. The digital cutaneous nerves have not been cut and distal sensation is normal apart from tenderness around the cut. Extensor pollicis longus (EPL) is the prime mover and sole extensor of the terminal (interphalangeal) joint. Extensor pollicis brevis (EPB) is the prime mover in extension of the MP joint. Cuts around the knuckle of the metacarpophalangeal joint are likely to sever one or both tendons. In this patient, both EPL and EPB have been severed.
underlyingDiagnosis: Severed extensor pollicis longus (EPL) and extensor pollicis brevis (EPB) tendons to the left thumb

title: Advice on breastfeeding versus bottle-feeding for a 28-year-old pregnant woman
patientProfile: 28-year-old woman at 35 weeks of gestation, attending antenatal clinic for her first baby. She has always hoped to breastfeed but her mother recently told her that formula-fed babies grow better than breastfed babies, causing uncertainty.
candidateInfo: You are a Hospital Medical Officer (HMO) in an antenatal clinic, seeing a 28-year-old woman for her antenatal visit at 35 weeks of gestation. She wants to discuss infant feeding with you. She has heard a lot about the benefits of breastfeeding, but her mother told her recently that babies grow better with formula feeds. She is uncertain whether she should breastfeed or formula-feed her baby.
tasks: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of breast-feeding and formula-feeding with her.
Outline the steps involved in safe formula-feeding.
openingStatement: My mother feels that bottle-fed babies gain more weight than breastfed babies and therefore are more healthy. What do you think, doctor?
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient is a first-time mother at 35 weeks gestation who has always hoped to breastfeed but is now uncertain due to her mother's advice that formula-fed babies grow better.
historyWithPrompting: If asked: What are the advantages of breastfeeding? Is there anything special about breast milk? Do you have to prepare bottle feeds in any special way? Are the formula feeds safe?
patientQuestions: What are the advantages of breastfeeding? I would have thought it is easier to breastfeed.
Is there anything special about breast milk? I always thought there was.
Do you have to prepare bottle feeds in any special way?
Are the formula feeds safe? I thought they contained cow's milk and what if you are allergic to cow's milk?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to advise a young expectant mother on the advantages and disadvantages of breastfeeding and bottle-feeding. This scenario tests the candidate's ability to identify the conflict the young mother has in trying to respect what her mother has told her, while knowing that this advice is contrary to her own feelings. It also tests ability to discuss logically the advantages and disadvantages of the different feeding methods as well as testing knowledge on safe bottle-feed preparation.
expectationsOfPerformance: Be nonjudgmental, avoiding comments like 'Where on earth did your mother get such an idea?'; rather asking 'Why do you think your mother made such a recommendation?'

Discuss that while breastfeeding is the optimal method of feeding the human infant, a variety of reasons may prevent breastfeeding in practice, including illness in the mother, failure to establish lactation, possible illness in the baby, prematurity, previous extensive breast surgery, and heightened anxiety in the mother.
Explain that if for some reason breastfeeding is unsuccessful, formula-feeding is a safe and very effective alternative.

Discuss that formulas are designed to contain the same nutritional components as breast milk, but that exact reproduction is difficult as the concentration and components of breast milk change throughout each feed.
Advise that there is no advantage of formula-feeding over breastfeeding.

Discuss the specific advantages of breastfeeding: practical advantages, increased resistance of the baby to infection from immunological constituents in breast milk, and satisfaction and close relationship with the infant.
In response to her mother's comment, advise that weight gain is not the only criterion for success as excess weight gain in the first 12 months of life may in fact be detrimental in later life.

Stress the importance of optimal formula-feeding: sterility in preparing bottle feeds is essential; bottles need to be washed clean with a bottlebrush; bottles and teats need to be stored in solution (e.g. Milton®) and rinsed prior to use; fluid used should be cooled boiled water; follow explicit makeup instructions on the can; no added scoops; day's requirements best made up at one time and stored in refrigerator; only one day's feed at a time; each feed should contain approximately 30 mL more than anticipated and any excess discarded.
keyIssues: Empathic answering of this young mother-to-be's questions.
Recognition that she is uncomfortable with what her mother has told her but is seeking reassurance and support for her own view which she feels is accurate.
Satisfactory explanation of the advantages and disadvantages of the different methods of feeding.
Candidates should know how formula feeds are prepared.
commentary: Ability to discuss impartially and accurately the relative merits, indications, contraindications and techniques of infant feeding by breastfeeding and by formula-feeding is a requisite for all medical graduates as outlined and is an area where good communication skills are paramount.
underlyingDiagnosis: N/A — counselling and education scenario

title: Advice on neonatal circumcision for a couple expecting their first child
patientProfile: A young couple expecting their first child, attending a general practice. Family members have suggested circumcision if the baby is a boy. The couple have no religious beliefs dictating circumcision and are unsure about the procedure.
candidateInfo: A young couple, the wife pregnant with their first child, have come to see you in a general practice to discuss with you the place of routine neonatal circumcision if their baby is a boy.
tasks: Discuss with the couple the perceived risks and benefits of this procedure.
openingStatement: We have come to discuss with you whether to have our baby circumcised.
historyWithoutPrompting: The couple have no religious beliefs that dictate circumcision. Family members have recommended it without explanation. They are uncertain and want to understand the advantages and disadvantages before making a decision.
historyWithPrompting: If asked: What does the procedure involve? What are the complications that can happen? What are the advantages of having it done? Are there any times when it definitely should or shouldn't be done? Has anyone looked into this in detail and come to any conclusions about it?
patientQuestions: What does the procedure involve?
What are the complications that can happen?
What are the advantages of having it done?
Are there any times when it definitely should or shouldn't be done?
Has anyone looked into this in detail and come to any conclusions about it?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to give impartial advice about neonatal circumcision.
expectationsOfPerformance: Give an impartial but informed explanation to the parents on the advantages and risks involved in routine neonatal circumcision.
Indicate that when religious grounds are stated as the reason for the procedure, these are generally respected.
Realise that many are unaware of the actual process of circumcision and stress that the parents should consider the advantages and disadvantages before making a decision.

Discuss perceived advantages: reduced incidence of UTIs, possible reduction in STIs and HIV infection (controversial), circumcision indicated for phimosis if conservative methods fail, minimises risk of carcinoma of the penis (though poor penile hygiene and HPV infection are major contributors).

Discuss recognised complications and disadvantages: haemorrhage, infection including septicaemia/meningitis (rare), ulceration of the glans, inadvertent injury to the urethra, too much skin removed, anaesthetic complications, secondary phimosis. Complication rate estimated 1–5% (range 0.2–10%).

Discuss absolute contraindications: hypospadias and other congenital anomalies of the penis, chordee, buried penis, sick or jaundiced infants, family history of bleeding disorder, inadequate expertise and facilities.
Explain the procedure: usually done under local anaesthesia; if performed after six months of age, done under general anaesthesia.
Summarise that recommendations of various national and international paediatric and paediatric surgical associations do not support routine neonatal circumcision.
keyIssues: The ability to discuss in an unbiased manner the perceived advantages and disadvantages of routine neonatal circumcision.

Capacity to summarise that the recommendations of various national and international paediatric and paediatric surgical associations, who have extensively reviewed the literature on the subject, do not support routine neonatal circumcision.
commentary: This topic is one that has been controversial for many years. While circumcision for religious reasons has been done for many centuries, the issue in the scenario is related to the secular trend towards routine neonatal circumcision of all males soon after birth. This has become more of a ritual rather than for any recognised medical indication, although this tendency has reduced in recent times. In fact there are many recognised complications of circumcision, not just routine neonatal circumcision, but circumcision at any age. However there are recognised medical indications for the procedure (e.g. established phimosis in boys and men). Many young parents are unaware of the issues involved and are often ill-informed by family members who recommend that their infant should have the procedure performed without any explanation as to why. The review of the literature in relation to risks and benefits shows there is no evidence of benefit outweighing harm for neonatal circumcision as a routine procedure.
underlyingDiagnosis: N/A — counselling and education scenario

title: Suspected hearing impairment in a 10-month-old child
patientProfile: 10-month-old female infant (baby Helena), referred by local child health nurse for suspected hearing problem. Third child in a healthy family. Pregnancy and delivery were normal. Parents have never had cause to worry about her hearing. She is crawling, does not walk yet, but pulls herself up to standing. She babbles during assessment.
candidateInfo: You are working in a community health centre. Your next patient is a 10-month-old female infant, baby Helena, seen with her mother, who has been referred by the local child health nurse. The pregnancy and delivery were normal. The child presented to the nurse six weeks ago for review and general screening including hearing. The nurse was concerned that the baby has a hearing problem and wanted her checked by a doctor. The child's parents have never had cause to worry about her hearing. She is the third child in a healthy family and has been well, apart from a few upper respiratory infections. She is crawling, does not walk yet, but pulls herself up to standing beside a small table. Initial examination findings: A busy infant girl who objects to being restrained by her parent. She babbles during assessment. Otoscopic examination is normal. No abnormal physical signs are present on general examination. The parents are puzzled at the need for referral and seek information about further investigation and management.
tasks: Ask the parent for additional relevant and focused history.
Counsel the parent after you have obtained a further history.
Explain possible causes of any suspected hearing loss to the parent.
Discuss your plan of management with the parent.
historyWithoutPrompting: Parents have never had cause to worry about baby Helena's hearing. She babbles, seems to hear sounds and respond, and has been well apart from a few upper respiratory infections. The child health nurse was concerned at routine screening.
historyWithPrompting: Family history of deafness: none. Maternal problems in pregnancy especially infectious diseases: none. Perinatal problems: none. General development: satisfactory, same as other children. Does the baby respond to sounds, including loud sounds: seems to hear them and respond. Does the baby respond when called by her name: usually. Does the baby turn towards the sources of the sounds: sometimes. Does the baby respond to television: sometimes. In regard to any of the above aspects, the parents have noted no abnormalities in regards to her response to sound.
patientQuestions: Do you think further tests would help? (if referral is not suggested)
physicalExaminationFindings: Otoscopic examination is normal. No abnormal physical signs on general examination.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to deal with suspected hearing loss in an infant aged 10 months and ability to appropriately counsel the parent.
expectationsOfPerformance: History should cover: family history of deafness, maternal problems in pregnancy, perinatal problems, general development, response to sounds, response to name, turning towards sound sources, response to television.
Explain that hearing loss is common in preschoolers — most cases are mild and transient due to conductive deafness from middle ear effusions.
Explain that sensorineural hearing loss is less common but important to detect as early as possible.
Explain that distraction tests are only a screening tool and do not diagnose deafness.
Note that often it is the parents who recognise that there is something wrong; but the child health nurse is also a professional and her concerns must be followed up.
Advise that if there is a problem, the earlier the diagnosis the better.
Refer the child to a Paediatric Audiologist as soon as possible for more formal hearing assessment by an audiogram.
Review following audiogram. If normal, reassure and review hearing and early language development in about three months. If abnormal, refer to appropriate specialist for further evaluation.
Exhibit understanding for the parent's concern and provide guarded reassurance and support.
keyIssues: Appropriate history relevant to deafness.
Counselling with reference to early definitive screening for hearing.
Providing appropriate level of support and reassurance.
criticalErrors: Failure to refer for specialist assessment (audiogram) for definitive diagnosis.
commentary: The candidate is expected to proceed from the cue of 'possible hearing loss' to assess whether other evidence, such as failure to turn to sound, is present to support this diagnosis. In addition, enquiry regarding features suggesting associated developmental delay is appropriate. Given a working hypothesis of possible hearing loss, features in the history which could be causative, such as maternal illness in pregnancy, perinatal problems such as jaundice or drug administration, should be evaluated. However, the importance of early diagnosis and treatment of significant hearing deficit in this age group make referral for definitive diagnosis mandatory despite no other concerning features being present. Adequate communication skills in dealing with a concerned patient are essential, used in combination with appropriate knowledge-based clinical skills.
underlyingDiagnosis: Suspected hearing impairment — requires audiological assessment to confirm or exclude

title: Counselling a family after sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS)
patientProfile: Family of a four-month-old male infant (Andrew) who was rushed to the Emergency Department the day before and was dead on arrival. Provisional diagnosis is SIDS; Coronial autopsy is to be performed. The mother is a single mother, too distressed to speak. The spokesperson is the mother's sister (the aunt). The GP had seen the baby two months previously (thriving, normal development, commenced immunisations) and two days before death (mild upper respiratory snuffles causing minor breastfeeding difficulties). No suspicious circumstances.
chiefComplaint: Sudden infant death — family seeking answers
candidateInfo: You work in a general practice. You are counselling the family of a four-month-old male infant who was rushed to the Emergency Department of the local hospital the day before but was dead on arrival. The provisional diagnosis is sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) and the baby (Andrew) is to have a Coronial autopsy. You had seen him for the first time two months previously, with his single mother, when he was thriving and developing normally and had commenced immunisations. Two days before his death you saw him again, this time with mild upper respiratory snuffles which were causing minor difficulties with breastfeeding. However, over the next two days he apparently improved, and his mother had advised you that he appeared normal and fed well from the breast just prior to his death. You are unaware of any suspicious circumstances surrounding the death. The family members have attended to seek details of why the baby died and why an autopsy is necessary. The spokesperson for the group is the mother's sister, the aunt of the infant. The mother is also present, but is too distressed to ask any questions herself.
tasks: Answer the questions of the aunt relating to the death of the infant.
Counsel the aunt and family.
openingStatement: We can't understand why Andrew has died!
historyWithoutPrompting: The family cannot understand why the baby died. The mother is too distressed to speak. The aunt is the spokesperson.
historyWithPrompting: Questions the aunt will ask: Why do the police have to be involved? Do they think my sister killed her baby? Why does he have to have an autopsy? When will we get further information and results? When can we arrange his funeral? We feel so alone — is there anyone we can talk to about this? Should the snuffles have been treated?
patientQuestions: Why do the police have to be involved? Do they think my sister killed her baby?
Why does he have to have an autopsy?
When will we get further information and results of this?
When can we arrange his funeral?
We feel so alone. Is there anyone we can talk to about this?
Should the snuffles have been treated?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability in approach to the family and providing empathic counselling in this tragic situation of presumed sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) where there are no suspicious circumstances. Candidates should outline the statutory requirements (i.e. notifying police and coroner), in a caring and sympathetic manner.
expectationsOfPerformance: Explain the most likely cause of the child's death is SIDS. Explain what is known about SIDS: frequency has fallen from 1 in 500 live births to approximately 1 in 1000; peak incidence at about four months of age; no certain causes known.

Explain that there can be other causes of sudden infant death (e.g. overwhelming infection), but the child's history does not suggest this. Andrew's snuffles were not a warning sign and there is no suggestion that any medical treatment would have influenced the outcome.
Explain empathically that the police and the Coroner must be notified by law because Andrew's death was sudden and unexplained.
Explain that the role of the Police Officer is to assist the Coroner — police are required to interview all people concerned including the baby's general practitioner.
Explain the need for the autopsy in all cases, done by experienced pathologists to find out what causes SIDS and to exclude other possible causes of death.
Explain that tissues will be removed for further examination under the microscope.
Offer to contact the Coroner later to obtain information on the initial findings after the autopsy, or advise that the Coroner's office will contact the mother.
Offer to contact other family members for support for the mother.
The Coroner will decide if an inquest needs to be held, but with SIDS this is generally not necessary.
Offer to contact the local SIDS Support Group, if one is available.
Followup contact with family and with the Coroner/pathologist to confirm diagnosis. Liaise with support group in counselling the mother when results are available.
keyIssues: Appropriate empathic explanation.
Ability to explain the involvement of appropriate authorities and support groups.
Offering to arrange for continuing followup, contact and support with the family.
criticalErrors: Failure to display empathy in counselling.
Failure to recognise and explain need for coronial notification and autopsy.
commentary: Empathy in communication is essential in these tragic circumstances, together with accurate knowledge of legislative requirements. All deaths under these circumstances must be reported to the Coroner and the police must take statements. This is often the most distressing part of the process for young parents and should be explained carefully to the family why this process needs to happen by law. The caring practitioner will also offer to liaise with the Coroner on behalf of the parents, and in this way is often able to receive preliminary reports if the Coroner is agreeable to them being released, which many Coroners are. Several pathologists who perform these autopsies actually interview the parents themselves when the autopsy is completed. The caring practitioner will offer to keep contact with the grieving couple or parent until confident that this tragic event has been accepted.
underlyingDiagnosis: Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS) — provisional diagnosis pending Coronial autopsy
differentialDiagnosis: Other causes of sudden infant death

title: Excessive alcohol consumption in a 45-year-old man
patientProfile: A 45-year-old businessman who consulted two days ago about his drinking after seeing a TV program about the harmful effects of alcohol. He drinks at least five standard drinks every day, has problems at work, trouble with close family relationships, impaired sexual performance, two minor traffic accidents in the last year, family history of alcoholism (grandfather). On examination: overweight (BMI 28 kg/m²), hypertensive 180/90 mmHg, hepatomegaly.
chiefComplaint: Excessive drinking, returning for test results
tasks: Explain the results of the tests to the patient.
Discuss the effects of the excessive alcohol consumption.
Counsel him about his drinking.
openingStatement: (Patient is passive, contemplative, not yet decided about modifying or stopping alcohol intake.)
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient is a 45-year-old businessman who became concerned about harmful effects of alcohol after seeing a TV program. He has been a regular, now daily, drinker for many years, considered himself an 'average drinker'. He consulted the doctor two days ago; the doctor enquired about alcohol use and examined him. He has returned today to discuss results.
historyWithPrompting: Drinks excessively (at least five standard drinks every day), long-standing pattern at home, at work and socially. Problems at work. Trouble with close family relationships. Impaired sexual performance. Two minor traffic accidents in the last year. Family history (grandfather) of alcoholism. Overweight (BMI 28 kg/m²), hypertensive 180/90 mmHg, hepatomegaly on examination.
physicalExaminationFindings: Overweight (BMI 28 kg/m²), hypertensive 180/90 mmHg, hepatomegaly. (Previously established.)
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of hazardous drinking, including early presentation, sequelae, types of dependency, and skill in counselling a person who has been drinking hazardously over a long period of time.
expectationsOfPerformance: Approach to patient should be nonjudgmental and supportive.

Explain test results and their interpretation: elevated GGT and AST, elevated MCV and macrocytosis confirm clinical suspicion of liver disease due to excessive harmful drinking. Link to hypertension and excessive weight.

Discuss the effects of excessive drinking: other physical sequelae on gastrointestinal tract, cardiovascular system and central nervous system; family problems, work problems, sexual problem, minor accidents.
Apply 'CAGE' or 'AUDIT' questionnaires during discussion or counselling.

Counsel the patient about safe drinking using NHMRC Guidelines for men: low risk less than 4 standard drinks per day; hazardous 5-6 standard drinks per day; harmful (high risk) more than 6 standard drinks per day.
Explain what a 'standard drink' contains (approximately 10 grams of alcohol).
Explain types of dependency: social, psychological, physical.

Discuss significance of amount and duration of alcohol use in determining risk of physical dependency. Hallmarks of physical dependency include increasing alcohol tolerance, withdrawal symptoms, relief from withdrawal symptoms by further alcohol or agonist.
Discuss social, behavioural, emotional and cognitive sequelae of excessive alcohol use.
Explain that excessive habitual consumption/dependence can be associated with target organ damage without psychological or social disorders and vice versa.
As initial management, advise a period of abstinence to test presence and/or degree of physical dependency.

As followup management: suggest further consultation shortly; offer to see wife; mention availability of Alcoholics Anonymous; make clear patient has to come to his own decision but can expect ongoing support.
keyIssues: Interpretation of results of liver function tests (LFTs) and full blood examination (FBE).
Explanation of effects of hazardous and harmful drinking.
Advising a period of abstinence to establish type and degree of dependency.
criticalErrors: Taking a judgmental attitude and blaming patient for his condition.
commentary: As well as knowledge of hazardous drinking, this case allows assessment of the candidate's communication skills and knowledge of counselling as the principal form of management in a behavioural problem, in which the type of dependency is uncertain at this stage. The patient is likely to have true physical dependency; the next step in management is to test this assumption by asking the patient to try abstinence and see what happens. Subsequent management will depend on the result and the willingness of the patient to accept and control the type and degree of dependency on alcohol.
underlyingDiagnosis: Hazardous/harmful alcohol consumption with early liver disease (elevated AST, GGT, macrocytosis)

title: Type 1 diabetes mellitus in a 9-year-old boy
patientProfile: A 9-year-old boy, Roger, admitted to the paediatric unit for his first presentation of insulin-dependent Type 1 diabetes mellitus. His general condition is satisfactory, not requiring intravenous resuscitation; he has already commenced insulin therapy and has stabilised with good blood sugar control. His mother is seeking further information about his ongoing care.
candidateInfo:
A nine-year-old boy, Roger, is admitted to the paediatric unit to which you are the Hospital Medical Officer (HMO). This is his first presentation of insulin-dependent Type 1 diabetes mellitus. His general condition is satisfactory, not requiring intravenous resuscitation, and he has already commenced insulin therapy and has stabilised with good blood sugar control.

As the Ward HMO, his mother has asked you for further information about his ongoing care in relation to his diabetes from now on.
tasks: Answer the queries the mother has, related to the ongoing care of Roger's diabetes.
openingStatement: 'Will he need insulin injections each day from now on?'
historyWithoutPrompting: Roger is a 9-year-old boy with first presentation of Type 1 diabetes mellitus. He has stabilised on insulin therapy with good blood sugar control. His mother is asking about ongoing care.
historyWithPrompting: Questions from mother cover: need for daily insulin injections and who administers them; monitoring blood sugar levels; school liaison; school camps; sleepovers; sport participation.
patientQuestions: 'Will he need insulin injections each day from now on?'
'Who is going to be giving Roger's insulin from now on?'
'How do I assess the day to day control of his diabetes?'
'What do I need to do about his school?'
'Will he be able to go to school camps? What should I do about them?'
'He went to his first sleepover party a few weeks ago — could he still go on these now?'
'Can he play sport?'
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of practical aspects of childhood diabetic care — which is not very dissimilar to adult Type 1 diabetic care — and the candidate's ability to answer queries of a concerned parent. The questions chosen are similar to those asked by parents who are anticipating the problems that they may encounter in the months after initial diabetic education and discharge from hospital.
expectationsOfPerformance: Advise that the child will be on life-long injections of insulin, usually twice daily (short-acting and intermediate-acting combination); basal/bolus regimen generally inappropriate at this age.
Both parents and perhaps an older sibling should learn injection technique; also how and when to give glucagon.
Blood sugar levels monitored several times daily by glucometer before each meal and before bed; urine testing for ketones if blood sugar persistently high or child unwell.
Notify Roger's school of diagnosis; diabetic educator to visit school to instruct staff about hypoglycaemia detection and management; provide emergency phone numbers.
School camps: parents encouraged to attend as camp parents; supervising teachers need education and telephone support; letter to local medical practitioner if camp is distant.
Sleepovers should be encouraged; preferably at home of family who know the child well and close to home.
Sport: no reason why child cannot play most sports; insulin doses may need adjustment prior to active sport.
Counsel parent in a reassuring manner that help is but a phone call away.
keyIssues: Ability to answer the specific questions of the parent accurately and sensibly.
criticalErrors: Failure to discuss symptoms and treatment of insulin-induced hypoglycaemia.
commentary: The scenario is designed to assess both the knowledge of the topic and the candidate's ability to provide accurate information in a reassuring manner to a parent who is trying to cope with a diagnosis which to most parents is initially devastating and upsetting. The emphasis in many of the answers should be allowing the child to lead as normal a life as possible, so that school and social activities should be maintained wherever possible. The key to success in these situations is whether parents are well educated in their child's condition and can confidently instruct others accurately in the management of the child.
underlyingDiagnosis: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (first presentation)

title: Request for vasectomy from a 36-year-old man
patientProfile: A 36-year-old senior constable in the police force, married for 12 years with two children (boy aged 7, girl aged 9). No marital problems. Moderate alcohol drinker (three standard drinks a day), non-smoker. No known drug sensitivities. Family all well. Not on any medication. Normal physical examination including scrotum, testes and penis. BP 120/70 mmHg, urinalysis normal.
tasks: Explain the sterilisation procedure and its consequences to the patient.
Answer the patient's questions and provide counselling accordingly.
openingStatement: (Patient wishes to discuss vasectomy; wife wants to cease oral contraception because of weight gain.)
historyWithoutPrompting: Wife aged 34 years works as a secretary and wishes to cease oral contraception because of weight gain. She has taken the pill since the birth of their son now aged seven. Patient also has a daughter aged nine. Wife would prefer not to have a tubal ligation. Happily married with mutually satisfactory sexual relationship; neither wishes to have another child. No extramarital relationships.
historyWithPrompting: Patient has reservations about the operation because of possible complications, reliability, absence from work, and any adverse effect on sexual performance.
patientQuestions: 'Is it 100% effective?'
'How do you know it has worked?'
'Can the operation be reversed?'
'Are there any complications?'
'Will there be any change in my sexual performance?'
'How long afterwards can we resume normal sex?'
'Does my wife have to sign anything?'
'What is actually done?'
'Will my wife notice any difference?'
'What happens to the semen?'
'Can it be done as a day procedure?'
'Will I have a general anaesthetic?'
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to explain the surgical procedure of vasectomy, its complications, effectiveness, reversibility and effects on sexual performance.
expectationsOfPerformance: Give reassurance about result of checkup (good health); be supportive and give honest advice about the procedure.
Explain surgical procedure of vasectomy — how it is done.
Discuss inconvenience and complications.
Explain postoperative sperm counts.
Discuss effectiveness and reversibility — although reversal is sometimes possible, vasectomy should effectively be regarded as a method of permanent sterilisation.
Discuss timing and effect on sexual activity/performance.
Use of diagram, patient education material (e.g. Royal Australasian College of Surgeons patient information brochure).
Recommend involvement of patient's wife — offer to discuss procedure with her; advise she should preferably also give written permission.
Discuss alternatives (i.e. condom or diaphragm with spermicidal).
Clarify that they need to use contraception until seminal analysis is negative.
keyIssues: Adequate explanation to the patient of the procedure and its followup.
criticalErrors: False reassurance that the procedure is 100% effective before at least two negative sperm counts.
False reassurance that a vasectomy is easily reversed.
Refusal to discuss sterilisation.
commentary: Appropriate consent for vasectomy is required. Permission of the patient's wife is not obligatory but is strongly advised by medical defence organisations. The candidate may have religious or cultural objections to contraception in general or vasectomy in particular. If so, candidates have been advised to inform the patient of their position at the beginning of the consultation. However, medical knowledge of the procedure is still expected together with the communication skills required in explanation. This recognises the patient's right to seek the procedure and the doctor's right not to remain involved beyond this consultation, but to suggest appropriate referral after initial discussion.
underlyingDiagnosis: Request for vasectomy (elective sterilisation)

title: Case Presentations to Examiner — Headache
subcategory: 1-B Case presentations and summaries to Examiner
patientProfile: Example case presentation scenario — headache.
candidateInfo:
Case Presentations and Discharge Summary scenarios 022-029 cover: Headache, Neck lump, Previous shoulder dislocation, Dysphagia, Low back pain, Knee pain, Abdominal discomfort, Gastric ulcer with haemorrhage.

Verbal Case presentation to an observing examiner or senior colleague is a fundamental part of clinical medicine. It may consist of an informal exchange of information between colleagues on a ward round or it may be a more formal presentation to an examiner or to a group in examination circumstances.
tasks: Present a succinct summary of the clinical problem to the examiner.
Provide a provisional diagnosis and differential diagnoses.
Advise the patient of the likely diagnosis and management plans when directed by the examiner.
openingStatement: Examiner: 'What do you believe is the most likely diagnosis from the information you have found so far?'
historyWithoutPrompting: Example: Patient presenting with intermittent headaches for at least five years, attacks occurring irregularly every few months, lasting for a few days. Recently occurring more often (every few days) and lasting longer. Feels like a tight band or pressure around or on top of the head. Affects forehead and both temples, radiates to back of head. Usually starts in the morning and lasts all day, getting worse by evening. Reduces after Panadol®, evening meal, rest and local heat. Not accompanied by nausea, vomiting, visual disturbance, photophobia. Patient very busy, perfectionist by nature, finding it more difficult to cope. Recently saw a TV documentary about cerebral tumour.
historyWithPrompting: Headache not increased in intensity — only in frequency and duration. Throbbing quality as it progresses. Pain rates about 3-4 out of 10. Not related to posture, exercise or position of head or neck. Can continue to work and do household duties during attacks. No loss of time from work.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to present a concise, accurate and logically organised verbal case summary to an observing examiner, covering history, examination findings, diagnostic and investigational plans and management.
expectationsOfPerformance: Present a succinct, accurate and logically organised verbal case summary.
Identify the most likely diagnosis (tension headache) and relevant differential diagnoses.
Communicate clearly and accurately to the examiner.
Advise the patient of the likely diagnosis and management plans when directed.
Use appropriate language — avoid jargon when speaking to the patient.
keyIssues: Ability to present a focused, logical and accurate verbal case summary.
Correct identification of most likely diagnosis and differential diagnoses.
Clear communication with both examiner and patient.
commentary: Verbal Case presentation to an observing examiner or senior colleague is a fundamental part of clinical medicine. Relevant data should be given in a systematic and concise form highlighting the presenting problem, and evolving into a coherent whole. To summarise a patient's problem fully, begin with patient identification (age is essential) and reason for presentation. Proceed to a narrative which may sequentially summarise examination findings, investigation findings, systems review, risk factors, and diagnostic and treatment plans.
underlyingDiagnosis: Tension headache (muscle contraction headache)

title: Case Presentations to Examiner — Neck lump
subcategory: 1-B Case presentations and summaries to Examiner
patientProfile: Example case presentation scenario — neck lump.
candidateInfo:
Case Presentations and Discharge Summary scenarios 022-029 cover: Headache, Neck lump, Previous shoulder dislocation, Dysphagia, Low back pain, Knee pain, Abdominal discomfort, Gastric ulcer with haemorrhage.

Verbal Case presentation to an observing examiner or senior colleague is a fundamental part of clinical medicine. Practice in delivering summarised case presentations to an observing examiner is well worthwhile as an aid to improving communication skills, and to organising and presenting clinical reasoning skills in data acquisition, assimilation and interpretation.
tasks: Describe examination findings to the examiner as you proceed.
Present a case summary and provisional diagnosis to the examiner.
Indicate further assessment steps.
openingStatement: Candidate: 'The patient has a lump in the right anterior triangle of the midneck which feels like a lymph node enlargement. I shall proceed to examine the skin of face and scalp, the oropharynx and nasopharynx, and the external ear for any primary pathology.'
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient presents with a lump in the right anterior triangle of the midneck. Examination reveals a swelling consistent with lymph node enlargement.
historyWithPrompting: Examination of skin of face and scalp, oropharynx, nasopharynx, and external ear for any primary pathology. Consideration of lymphatic spread from areas outside head and neck (chest, lungs, abdomen, genitals) and focal presentation of systemic lymphoid pathology.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to perform a focused examination of a neck lump, describe findings accurately to the examiner, and present a logical case summary with differential diagnosis.
expectationsOfPerformance: Describe examination findings to the examiner as proceeding — both describe and perform.
Examine accessible nasopharynx and oropharynx.
Consider lymphatic spread from areas outside head and neck.
Consider focal presentation of systemic lymphoid pathology.
Present a succinct, accurate case summary.
Indicate appropriate further assessment (e.g. cytologic aspirate, endoscopic evaluation for occult primary neoplasms).
keyIssues: Ability to perform and describe a focused examination of a neck lump.
Logical case summary with appropriate differential diagnosis.
Awareness of need to examine for primary pathology in relevant sites.
commentary: One of the most common errors is failing to observe appropriately. The candidate must actually look for what they say they are looking for. With neck lumps it is particularly important to observe the effects of movement: swallowing, coughing, protruding the tongue, and tensing underlying muscles. Remember to examine the accessible nasopharynx and oropharynx. Cytologic aspirate will often clarify the diagnosis and point the way for further diagnostic tests.
underlyingDiagnosis: Lymph node enlargement (neck lump — aetiology to be determined)

title: Case Presentations to Examiner — Previous shoulder dislocation
subcategory: 1-B Case presentations and summaries to Examiner
patientProfile: Example case presentation scenario — patient with past history of dislocated shoulder of the dominant right arm.
chiefComplaint: Previous shoulder dislocation — current status assessment
candidateInfo:
Case Presentations and Discharge Summary scenarios 022-029 cover: Headache, Neck lump, Previous shoulder dislocation, Dysphagia, Low back pain, Knee pain, Abdominal discomfort, Gastric ulcer with haemorrhage.

Verbal Case presentation to an observing examiner or senior colleague is a fundamental part of clinical medicine. Practice in delivering summarised case presentations to an observing examiner is well worthwhile as an aid to improving communication skills, and to organising and presenting clinical reasoning skills.
tasks: Perform a focused and relevant physical examination of the shoulder.
Describe findings to the observing examiner as you proceed.
Assess current status of the shoulder.
openingStatement: Candidate (to the examiner): 'The patient has had a past history of dislocated shoulder of his dominant right arm and my task is to assess the current status. I shall begin by inspection of contour looking in particular for wasting or deformity. None is apparent. I shall now test the range of active movements, comparing these to the opposite normal side.'
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient has a past history of dislocated shoulder of the dominant right arm. Task is to assess the current status of the shoulder.
historyWithPrompting: Range of active movements compared to opposite normal side. Inspection for wasting or deformity. Testing power of movement. Sensory and motor assessment relevant to nerves at risk from shoulder dislocation.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to perform a focused examination of the shoulder joint, describe findings accurately to the examiner, and present a logical case summary.
expectationsOfPerformance: Begin with inspection of contour looking for wasting or deformity.
Test range of active movements, comparing to the opposite normal side.
Describe and perform examination simultaneously — do not just describe without doing.
Test power of movement.
Assess for nerve injury relevant to shoulder dislocation (axillary nerve).
Present findings to examiner in a succinct and accurate manner.
keyIssues: Ability to perform and describe a focused shoulder examination.
Comparison with the normal contralateral side.
Assessment of neurovascular status relevant to previous dislocation.
commentary: One of the most common errors is failing to observe appropriately — the candidate must actually look for what they say they are looking for. When asked to describe what is being done as proceeding with an examination, make sure you both describe and perform. The shoulder joint is the most mobile synovial joint in the human body. The axillary nerve runs between the muscles immediately below the capsule and is at hazard from stretching injury in shoulder dislocations.
underlyingDiagnosis: Previous anterior shoulder dislocation — assessment of current status

title: Dysphagia in a 60-year-old man (Case Presentation Example)
subcategory: 1-B Case presentations and summaries to Examiner
patientProfile: A 60-year-old man with a 6-month history of progressive painless dysphagia associated with weight loss. Physical findings are noncontributory in the chest, neck and abdomen.
candidateInfo: This is an example case presentation to an observing examiner. The candidate has taken a history from a 60-year-old man with progressive painless dysphagia for 6 months associated with weight loss. Physical examination findings are noncontributory.
tasks: Provide a provisional diagnosis and reasons for this to the examiner.
Indicate which investigations you would choose and why.
Advise the patient of your recommendations.
openingStatement: Examiner: 'What is your provisional diagnosis?'
historyWithoutPrompting: The patient is aged 60 years and has a history of progressive painless dysphagia for the past 6 months associated with weight loss.
historyWithPrompting: Physical findings are noncontributory in the chest, neck and abdomen.
physicalExaminationFindings: Physical findings are noncontributory in the chest, neck and abdomen.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to present a case summary, provide a provisional diagnosis, select appropriate investigations, and advise the patient of recommendations.
expectationsOfPerformance: Candidate states the most likely diagnosis is an oesophageal lesion, probably a neoplasm.
Candidate notes the patient needs further investigation by diagnostic imaging or endoscopy.
When asked which investigation to choose, candidate selects endoscopy as most definitive but also orders a contrast swallow to identify the site of a stricture and extralumenal aspects.
Candidate advises the patient of recommendations clearly.
keyIssues: Ability to provide a succinct and logical case summary.
Correct provisional diagnosis of probable oesophageal neoplasm.
Appropriate selection and justification of investigations.
commentary: This example illustrates case presentation to an observing examiner. The candidate is expected to summarise history, examination findings, diagnostic and investigational plans and management in a logical manner. Endoscopy is usually most definitive; preliminary contrast imaging may help by identifying the site of a stricture and can show extralumenal aspects like extrinsic compression.
underlyingDiagnosis: Probable oesophageal neoplasm (carcinoma of the oesophagus)

title: Low back pain and sciatica — Case Presentation Example
subcategory: 1-B Case presentations and summaries to Examiner
patientProfile: A 25-year-old man with low back pain and sciatic radiation after a lifting strain at work two weeks ago.
chiefComplaint: Low back pain with leg radiation
candidateInfo: This is an example case presentation to an observing examiner. The candidate has taken a history and examined a 25-year-old man with low back pain and sciatic radiation after a lifting strain at work two weeks ago.
tasks: Summarise the history and findings to the examiner.
Provide a provisional diagnosis and reasons for this.
openingStatement: Examiner: 'Please summarise your history and findings and your provisional diagnosis.'
historyWithoutPrompting: The patient is aged 25 years and has had low back pain with sciatic radiation after a lifting strain at work two weeks ago.
historyWithPrompting: He has painful limitation of back movements and symptoms and signs suggesting lower lumbar nerve root impingement.
physicalExaminationFindings: Painful limitation of back movements and symptoms and signs suggesting lower lumbar nerve root impingement.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to summarise a history and examination findings and provide a provisional diagnosis in a case of low back pain and sciatica.
expectationsOfPerformance: Candidate provides a succinct summary identifying the key features: age, mechanism of injury, duration, radiation of pain.
Candidate states the most likely diagnosis is a lumbar disc prolapse with L5 radiculopathy.
Summary is logical, clear and well organised.
keyIssues: Ability to provide a concise and accurate clinical summary.
Correct provisional diagnosis of lumbar disc prolapse with radiculopathy.
commentary: This example illustrates a succinct case summary to an observing examiner. The candidate is expected to identify the key features of the history and examination and arrive at a logical provisional diagnosis. The most likely diagnosis is a lumbar disc prolapse with L5 radiculopathy.
underlyingDiagnosis: Lumbar disc prolapse with L5 radiculopathy

title: Knee pain with recurrent swelling after injury — Case Presentation Example
subcategory: 1-B Case presentations and summaries to Examiner
patientProfile: A young man with a past history of twisting strain to his right knee and persisting pain on its inner side.
chiefComplaint: Knee pain and swelling after injury
candidateInfo: This is an example case presentation to an observing examiner. The candidate has examined the right knee of a young man with a past history of twisting strain and persisting pain on the inner side.
tasks: Summarise the findings on physical examination to the examiner.
Provide a provisional diagnosis.
openingStatement: Examiner: 'Please summarise your findings on physical examination so far.'
historyWithoutPrompting: The patient has a past history of twisting strain to his knee and persisting pain on its inner side. He walks with a painful limp favouring the right leg and finds it difficult to bear weight on that side, and painful to kneel or squat.
historyWithPrompting: He cannot fully extend the right knee, the deficiency is 15°, and flexion range is normal. No joint effusion, no local swelling. Joint stability is stable testing the collateral and cruciate ligaments. Patellofemoral mobility and tracking appears normal; patellofemoral friction is not painful. He has localised tenderness over the joint line anteriorly on its inner side. No muscle wasting. The other knee appears normal.
physicalExaminationFindings: Painful limp favouring right leg. Cannot fully extend right knee (deficit of 15°). Flexion range normal. No joint effusion, no local swelling. Joint stability stable. Localised tenderness over the joint line anteriorly on the inner side. No muscle wasting. Other knee normal.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to summarise physical examination findings and provide a provisional diagnosis in a case of knee injury.
expectationsOfPerformance: Candidate provides a systematic and accurate summary of examination findings.
Candidate identifies the key positive finding of localised medial joint line tenderness and restricted extension.
Candidate states the provisional diagnosis of injury to the medial intra-articular cartilage.
keyIssues: Accurate and systematic summary of examination findings.
Correct provisional diagnosis of medial meniscal injury.
commentary: This example illustrates a focused examination summary to an observing examiner. The candidate is expected to identify the key findings and arrive at a logical provisional diagnosis. The most likely diagnosis is injury to the medial intra-articular cartilage (medial meniscus).
underlyingDiagnosis: Injury to the medial intra-articular cartilage (medial meniscus)
differentialDiagnosis: Traumatic osteochondritis/synovitis of the right knee

title: Abdominal discomfort in a 65-year-old woman — Case Presentation Example
subcategory: 1-B Case presentations and summaries to Examiner
patientProfile: Mrs S, a 65-year-old widowed pensioner with multiple comorbidities presenting with increasing abdominal discomfort and bloating over the past four weeks.
candidateInfo: This is an example case presentation to an observing examiner. The candidate has taken a history from a 65-year-old woman with a recent history of increasing abdominal discomfort and bloating related to meals over the past four weeks, with episodes of greater discomfort waking her from sleep. She has multiple comorbidities including rheumatoid arthritis, Type 2 diabetes, previous myocardial infarction, and hypertension.
tasks: Summarise the history and provisional diagnosis to the examiner.
Outline how you would proceed with further assessment.
openingStatement: Examiner: 'Please summarise the history you have obtained so far and your provisional diagnosis from the history.'
historyWithoutPrompting: Mrs S is a 65-year-old widowed pensioner with a recent history of increasing abdominal discomfort and bloating related to meals over the past four weeks, with episodes of greater discomfort which have woken her from sleep intermittently. The pain is epigastric and diffuse without radiation. She has occasional reflux of bitter fluid, has not vomited nor passed blood. She gets some relief from a glass of milk. She neither smokes nor drinks alcohol.
historyWithPrompting: She has rheumatoid arthritis affecting predominantly the hands for 15 years, treated with anti-inflammatory agents and steroids, currently on 5 mg prednisolone and Celebrex. Type 2 diabetes diagnosed 5 years ago, on diet and oral hypoglycaemics with good control. Myocardial infarction 10 years ago with no sequelae, remains on low dose aspirin. Hypertension controlled by an ACE-inhibitor. Always been overweight despite dieting. Family history of diabetes and coronary artery disease. Widowed 5 years ago, lives alone, three grown children interstate. Rheumatoid arthritis progressively worsened causing increasing difficulties in activities of daily living.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to provide a problem-based case summary incorporating multiple comorbidities and to outline a logical plan for further assessment.
expectationsOfPerformance: Candidate provides a succinct, problem-based summary identifying the presenting complaint and relevant comorbidities.
Candidate identifies multiple risk factors for peptic ulcer disease (NSAIDs, steroids, aspirin).
Candidate proposes upper gastrointestinal endoscopy to check for peptic ulcer, gastritis or reflux oesophagitis.
Candidate also suggests excluding gallstones by ultrasound.
Summary is logical, clear and well organised.
keyIssues: Ability to provide a comprehensive yet concise problem-based case summary.
Recognition of multiple risk factors for peptic ulcer disease.
Appropriate investigative plan.
commentary: This example illustrates a problem-based summary to an observing examiner. The candidate is expected to identify the presenting problem in the context of multiple comorbidities and risk factors, and propose an appropriate investigative plan. She has multiple risk factors for peptic ulcer disease. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy and abdominal ultrasound are the key investigations.
underlyingDiagnosis: Probable peptic ulcer disease / gastritis / reflux oesophagitis (in context of NSAID and steroid use)

title: Gastric ulcer with haemorrhage — Discharge Summary Example
subcategory: 1-B Case presentations and summaries to Examiner
patientProfile: Mr B, aged 66 years, admitted with haematemesis and melaena secondary to a gastric ulcer, with background of rheumatoid arthritis and previous myocardial infarction.
candidateInfo: This is an example of a written discharge summary to a local medical officer. Mr B, a 66-year-old pensioner, has been taking anti-inflammatory drugs for rheumatoid arthritis. He presented with haematemesis on two occasions and a tarry melaena stool. Urgent endoscopy showed a shallow gastric ulcer in the prepyloric region with a bleeding point which was fulgurated. He was begun on a proton pump inhibitor and his steroids and NSAID were discontinued.
tasks: Prepare a written discharge summary to the local medical officer covering the principal problems, management and follow-up arrangements.
openingStatement: Copy of Discharge Summary to local medical officer.
historyWithoutPrompting: Mr B, a 66-year-old pensioner, has been taking anti-inflammatory drugs (Celebrex with intermittent steroid courses) for rheumatoid arthritis diagnosed in 1988. At time of admission had been on prednisolone for past two weeks. He had a past history of myocardial infarction in 1992 with no sequelae. He presented with a history of having vomited blood on two occasions, and passed a tarry melaena stool earlier that day.
historyWithPrompting: Rheumatoid arthritis since 1988 affecting mainly the hands and wrist. Significantly disabled by pain, stiffness and deformity causing loss of function. Referred to rheumatology clinic for advice on continuing management.
physicalExaminationFindings: He was haemodynamically stable on admission.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to write a clear, accurate and comprehensive discharge summary covering the principal problems, investigations, management and follow-up arrangements.
expectationsOfPerformance: Discharge summary includes patient identification details, inpatient dates, and problem list.

Summary of gastric ulcer with haemorrhage: mechanism (NSAID/steroid use), presentation (haematemesis and melaena), investigation (endoscopy showing prepyloric ulcer with bleeding point), treatment (fulguration, omeprazole, cessation of NSAID and steroids).
Summary of rheumatoid arthritis and referral to rheumatology.
Medications on discharge listed.
Follow-up arrangements clearly stated.
Prognosis noted as guarded given risk factors.
keyIssues: Ability to write a clear and comprehensive discharge summary.
Identification of NSAID/steroid use as causative factors.
Appropriate management plan and follow-up arrangements.
commentary: This example illustrates a written discharge summary to a local medical officer. The summary should cover the principal problems, investigations, management and follow-up arrangements in a clear and concise manner. Prognosis is guarded in view of associated risk factors of arthritis likely to require resumption of anti-inflammatory and steroid treatment, necessitating long-term acid inhibition medication.
underlyingDiagnosis: Gastric ulcer with haemorrhage (haematemesis and melaena) secondary to NSAID and steroid use

title: Jaundice in a breastfed infant
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: Baby Helen, a two-week-old female infant born at term by easy vaginal delivery weighing 3.7 kg, presenting with persisting jaundice since day three of life. Currently breastfeeding well, weight 3.9 kg. Previous investigations showed no blood group incompatibility (both mother and baby group O positive), no red blood cell abnormality, and phototherapy was given for two days. Discharged at eight days.
candidateInfo:
Baby Helen is brought to see you in a general practice setting, as her mother is concerned about her continuing jaundice. Helen is now two weeks old and was born at term by easy vaginal delivery weighing 3.7 kg. Apgar scores were 9 and 10 (at 1 and 5 minutes respectively). She became jaundiced in the neonatal period starting on day three. Investigations then revealed no blood group incompatibility, both mother and baby being group O positive and no red blood cell (including enzymes) abnormality. The infant was treated with phototherapy for two days. Since discharge from hospital at eight days of age the jaundice has persisted and the mother is concerned. Baby is feeding well from the breast. Current weight is 3.9 kg. The baby was active and clinically normal apart from the jaundice when you saw her yesterday. You arranged investigations as set out below. The mother has now returned with the baby to discuss the results and your advice about treatment.

Investigation results:
• Serum bilirubin: Total 250 umol/L; Conjugated: less than 10 umol/L
• Neonatal thyroid screening: normal
• Urine culture: sterile
• Full Blood Examination (FBE): normal
tasks: Obtain any further necessary history you require. You should not take more than 2-3 minutes to do this.
Discuss the results of investigations with the mother.
Explain the diagnosis to her and advise about future management.
openingStatement: The mother is concerned about her baby's continuing jaundice since discharge from hospital.
historyWithoutPrompting: This is the mother's first baby. She has always intended to breastfeed, and established breastfeeding successfully. Baby Helen has been normal in all respects since birth. She has gained weight and is having normal bowel actions — motions and urine are both normally coloured. The mother is a healthy, breastfeeding mother, nonsmoker, on no medication.
historyWithPrompting: Urine and stools are of normal colour (key question to establish). Mother is not on any medications. Baby is feeding well and gaining weight.
patientQuestions: Does Helen need to go under those lights again?
Is there something wrong with my milk, should I stop breastfeeding?
Will this affect my capacity to continue breastfeeding? (if temporary cessation of breastfeeding is suggested)
physicalExaminationFindings: The baby was active and clinically normal apart from the jaundice.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of the causes of persisting neonatal jaundice, ability to make an appropriate diagnosis and to convey this to the patient.
expectationsOfPerformance: Candidate establishes that urine and stools are of normal colour — virtually clinching the diagnosis of breast milk jaundice.
Candidate correctly interprets the significance of the predominantly unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia.
Candidate explains that the most likely diagnosis is breast milk jaundice.
Candidate advises that the condition is self-limiting and requires no treatment.

Candidate explains that the diagnosis can be confirmed by suspending (not stopping) breastfeeding for 24-48 hours, which will result in a fall in serum bilirubin, after which breastfeeding can be continued — though this is acceptable but not necessary.
Candidate advises mother to express milk if temporary suspension of breastfeeding is advised, to maintain lactation.
Candidate emphasises that there is nothing wrong with her milk and that no treatment or further phototherapy is indicated.
Candidate reassures the mother appropriately.
keyIssues: Establishing that urine and stools are of normal colour.
Accurate interpretation of important pathology results — predominantly unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia.
Reassurance to mother that her milk is not harmful to her baby.
Accurate explanation of the possible causes for the jaundice and logically excluding other important diagnoses.
criticalErrors: Not appreciating the significance of predominant unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and insisting that the baby has biliary atresia or haemolytic disease.
Insisting that the breast milk is unsatisfactory for the baby and recommending permanent cessation of breastfeeding.
commentary: Jaundice in the newborn is very common, affecting at least 50% of full term infants. Prolonged jaundice (persisting beyond the expected physiological range) can be divided into predominantly unconjugated or conjugated types. Breast milk jaundice fits into the unconjugated variety but should be considered a diagnosis by exclusion of other important causes. Key clinical features supporting the diagnosis are: baby is breastfed, thriving and gaining weight well; bowel motions and urine are normal colour; physical examination except for jaundice is entirely normal; and important investigations (e.g. hypothyroidism screening) are normal. Breast milk jaundice is a benign condition requiring no treatment except explanation and support. Breastfeeding should not be interrupted. The cause is unknown but is thought to be due to a factor in breast milk that causes increased enteric absorption of bilirubin. The jaundice may persist for up to three months but the baby thrives and remains well.
underlyingDiagnosis: Breast milk jaundice

title: A painful penile rash in a 23-year-old man
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: 23-year-old male carpenter, no steady partner, heterosexual, last had sex with a woman met at a disco a week ago. No serious past illnesses, no allergies, no medications.
candidateInfo: You are the Hospital Medical Officer (HMO) in a hospital primary care clinic. A 23-year-old man presents with penile pain and a penile rash.
tasks: Take a focused history to assess the presenting problem.
Explain to the patient your provisional diagnosis and recommended management.
The penis appears as in the illustration provided.
openingStatement: 'I've got a problem with my penis, doctor.'
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient volunteers: penile pain for two days; started as intermittent tingling, now constant; noticed today a lumpy penile rash with blisters.
historyWithPrompting: No serious past illnesses and on no medications. No allergies. Sexual history: no steady partner, heterosexual, last had sex with a woman met at a disco a week ago. Does not volunteer information about sexual behaviour unless asked specifically.
patientQuestions: 'What is wrong with me?'
'What is the cause?'
'Can I pass it on?'
'How can it be treated?'
'Will I be cured?'
physicalExaminationFindings: Vesicular penile rash (as illustrated) — lumpy rash with blisters on the penis.
aimsOfStation: To assess ability to diagnose and manage genital herpes. This patient has penile herpes simplex. The patient also needs to be assessed in terms of other possible sexually transmissible infections.
expectationsOfPerformance: Take an appropriate sexual history and identify the vesicular penile rash as most likely due to genital herpes simplex.
Confirm diagnosis by viral testing.
Assess the risk of possible exposure to other sexually transmissible infections, for which the patient should be screened (VDRL/syphilis, chlamydial, gonococcal and HIV testing).
Counsel to reduce risk of transmission of any sexually transmissible infections.
Antimicrobial therapy: first clinical episodes of genital herpes, when severe, treated with a five day course of oral aciclovir or similar agent.
Topical treatments for symptomatic management: e.g. topical povidone-iodine; lignocaine.
Supportive treatment: rest, salt baths, ice packs, analgesics, wear loose clothing.
Investigate for other STIs including HIV; swabbing oral, genital and anal areas for Chlamydia and other infections. All testing with informed consent.
keyIssues: Ability to identify rash as herpes simplex.
Ability to take a sexual history and investigate possible concomitant sexually transmissible infections.
Counselling to reduce risk of further transmission of herpes simplex virus.
Ability to treat herpes infection.
criticalErrors: Failure to assess for other sexually transmissible infections.
commentary: Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV) is one of a family of herpes viruses. HSV 2 is largely associated with genital infection and is most common in young, sexually active adults. The risk of infection increases with numbers of sexual partners. Transmission is by vaginal, oral or anal sex. Condoms reduce the risk of transmission. Primary herpes simplex infections have an incubation period of 3–6 days. During the first attack there may be tingling and burning, a vesicular rash often in crops, small red painful ulcers after a day, associated fever, myalgia and local lymphadenopathy. The first attack lasts around two weeks. Recurrence occurs in about 50% and may be associated with shooting pains in the buttocks and legs. Recurrences are more frequent in the presence of immunosuppression including HIV infection. HSV infection may be associated with other sexually transmissible infections.
underlyingDiagnosis: Genital herpes simplex (HSV)

title: Primary amenorrhoea in an 18-year-old woman
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: 18-year-old woman who has never had a menstrual period. No previous operations or significant illnesses. Never been sexually active. Mother had first period at age 17. Younger sister (aged 14) has not yet started breast development.
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. Your next patient is an 18-year-old woman who is concerned because she has never had a menstrual period.
tasks: Take any further relevant history you require.
Ask the examiner for relevant findings you wish to ascertain on general and gynaecologic examination.
Advise the examiner of investigations you wish to order and your provisional diagnosis.
Counsel the patient appropriately.
openingStatement: Not explicitly stated; patient presents concerned about never having had a menstrual period.
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient is 18 years old and has never had a menstrual period. She is concerned about this.
historyWithPrompting: Evidence of pubertal changes: growth spurt three years ago; breast changes started three years ago; pubic hair began to develop three years ago; now of similar height to peers. No history of abdominal or pelvic pain. Mother had first period at age 17. Sister (aged 14) has not yet started breast development. No operations or significant illnesses. Never been sexually active.
patientQuestions: 'Why hasn't my period started yet?'
'All my friends at school started years ago. Will I ever have periods?'
'Will I be able to have a baby?'
physicalExaminationFindings: Weight 48 kg, normal height for weight. Breasts show Tanner stage 5 of puberty. Pubic and axillary hair also show stage 5. Blood pressure 120/80 mmHg. Vulval inspection — normal appearance. Hymen intact, but apparently perforate. Lower vagina above hymen appears normal. Pelvic examination per vaginum should not be done.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to define the cause of primary amenorrhoea in a young woman who has gone through an apparently normal puberty.
expectationsOfPerformance: Pelvic examination per vaginum should not be done and should not be specified by candidate.
Order abdominal-pelvic ultrasound (not vaginal) to check development of uterus and vagina and confirm they are normal.
Order hormone tests: FSH, LH, prolactin, and oestradiol levels.
Chromosome analysis is not necessary if uterus is shown to be normal and hormonal levels are normal.
Reassure that this is likely to be just a physiological delay in the first period as all else appears normal on examination.
Review after investigations; if normal, reassure and review in about 12 months. If period has still not occurred, repeat oestradiol estimation.
keyIssues: Ability to define the most likely cause of primary amenorrhoea (delayed menarche).
Ability to arrange the appropriate investigations.
criticalErrors: Inadequate history to evaluate current pubertal status.
Performance of pelvic vaginal examination as she is virginal — requesting that pelvic examination should be done would be a significant and potentially failing error.
Failure to order abdominal ultrasound. Pelvic (vaginal) ultrasound is also inappropriate.
Failure to order hormonal analyses of FSH, prolactin and oestradiol.
commentary: This case illustrates the situation where a slightly delayed menarche can be a normal situation particularly where there is a familial trait. It is therefore essential to obtain an appropriate history, both in regard to her own history and that of other family members. Noninvasive investigations will enable this patient to be reassured in the presence of other pubertal changes. Common problems: failure to recognise that an apparently normal puberty is occurring and that the cause is likely just a slight delay in the first period; failure to reassure the patient and advise review in 12 months if a period has still not occurred.
underlyingDiagnosis: Delayed menarche (physiological delay in first period)
differentialDiagnosis: Obstruction to outflow of blood from the uterus (intact hymen or vaginal septum)
Absence of development of the uterus

title: A skin lesion on the cheek of a 50-year-old man
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: Retired 50-year-old builder. Most of working life spent outdoors. Has had a firm nodule on his cheek for a year or so, which has slowly increased in size, recently developed an ulcer in the centre, and has bled a little. Otherwise healthy and well, no serious past illnesses, no allergies, on no medications.
candidateInfo: You are the Hospital Medical Officer (HMO) in a hospital primary care clinic. A retired 50-year-old builder presents with a skin lesion on his right cheek.
tasks: Assess the lesion by a focused history and physical examination.
Present your case summary and diagnosis to the examiner.
Explain to the patient your recommended management.
The lesion appears as in the illustration provided.
openingStatement: 'I've had this thing on my cheek for about a year doctor.'
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient has had a firm nodule on his cheek for a year or so, which has slowly increased in size. It has recently developed an ulcer in the centre and has bled a little. He thinks this may have followed picking at it. He is otherwise healthy and well.
historyWithPrompting: No serious past illnesses. No allergies. On no medications. Most of working life spent outdoors.
physicalExaminationFindings: Skin lesion on right cheek: firm nodule, slowly increasing in size, with central ulceration and minor bleeding — appearance consistent with basal cell carcinoma (rodent ulcer) as illustrated.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to diagnose a facial skin lesion suspicious of basal cell carcinoma.
expectationsOfPerformance: Recognise from the history and physical findings that this is a 'suspicious' lesion which requires excision.
Advise the patient accordingly.
Ability to present a focused case summary.
Ability to identify and manage a 'suspicious' skin lesion, in this case basal cell carcinoma.
Most appropriate treatment is local excision with an adequate margin.
keyIssues: Ability to present a focused case summary.
Ability to identify and manage a 'suspicious' skin lesion, in this case basal cell carcinoma.
criticalErrors: Failure to diagnose that this is a 'suspicious' skin lesion needing excision.
commentary: This scenario has been arranged in the format of a summary case presentation to the examiner of a suspicious skin lesion, followed by advice to the patient about treatment. It tests accuracy of observation and ability to summarise the problem concisely. The diagnosis should be straightforward. The most likely diagnosis is an ulcerating basal cell cancer and the most appropriate treatment is local excision with an adequate margin.
