Differentiating a basal cell cancer from a squamous lesion or other skin malignancies is less important than identifying the lesion as requiring appropriate histologic diagnosis after adequate excision. Common clinical features of BCC: most common skin cancer (80% of skin cancers), occurring usually in people older than 35 years, more frequently in males and in fair-skinned people; occurs most commonly on sun-exposed areas; ulceration produces the characteristic 'rodent' ulcer; BCCs grow slowly over years; they do not metastasise, but local spread can cause problems with surrounding structures.
underlyingDiagnosis: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) of the cheek

title: A pigmented mole on the trunk of a 30-year-old woman
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: Fair complexioned 30-year-old schoolteacher. Has had a dark spot on her back for many years, thinks she was born with it. The spot has become darker over the last few months. She has a few other dark spots that have not changed. Otherwise healthy and well, no serious past illnesses, no allergies, on no medications.
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. A fair complexioned 30-year-old schoolteacher seeks advice concerning a 'mole' on her trunk. The skin lesion appears as in the illustration of her back shown.
tasks: Assess the lesion by a focused history and physical examination.
Explain to the patient your diagnosis and recommended management.
openingStatement: 'It seems to have changed and become more itchy lately, doctor.'
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient has had a dark spot on her back for many years, thinks she was born with it. The spot has become darker over the last few months so she thought she would have it checked out. She has a few other dark spots, but they have not changed.
historyWithPrompting: Otherwise healthy and well, no serious past illnesses. No allergies, on no medications.
physicalExaminationFindings: Pigmented lesion on back: large (>5mm), irregular mole with irregular, ill-defined borders, irregular pigmentation, background redness, variable colours — consistent with a dysplastic melanocytic naevus suspicious of malignant change.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to diagnose a pigmented focal skin lesion and to identify a 'suspicious' lesion.
expectationsOfPerformance: Recognise that the presence of change in appearance and irritative symptoms in a previously stable pigmented naevus raises concerns about malignant change, especially in a large lesion with appearances suggesting a dysplastic naevus.
Refer the patient for excisional biopsy.
Ability to identify a 'suspicious' pigmented lesion, possibly at risk of malignant melanoma.
Ability to manage pigmented skin lesions appropriately.
Suspicious pigmented lesions should have complete excisional biopsy, and not be treated by cryotherapy.
keyIssues: Ability to identify a 'suspicious' pigmented lesion, possibly at risk of malignant melanoma.
Ability to manage pigmented skin lesions appropriately.
criticalErrors: Failure to suspect malignant potential.
commentary: Most pigmented skin lesions are benign. About one third of melanomas arise in pre-existing naevi. Removal of such naevi is important for melanoma prevention. The incidence of melanoma is rising in Australia and around the world. Incidence is higher in fair-skinned people; appears to be related to brief intense sunlight exposure as well as effect of chronic exposure. Most people have an average of 5–10 benign melanocytic naevi. Multiple dysplastic naevi carry a greater risk of malignant change. Clinical features of 'suspicious' dysplastic melanocytic naevi include: large (>5mm) irregular moles appearing usually on the trunks of young adults; lesions with irregular, ill-defined borders, irregular pigmentation, background redness, variable colours. Most are stable and do not lead to melanoma, but excision is indicated if any diagnostic concerns. Signs indicative of possible malignant melanoma include: any change in size, shape, colour, surface, or border; bleeding or ulceration; other symptoms (itching).
underlyingDiagnosis: Dysplastic melanocytic naevus suspicious of malignant melanoma

title: An itchy rash on the hands of a 19-year-old woman
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: 19-year-old female computer student. Rash present for about one week. No other abnormal examination findings apart from the rash. Boyfriend has had a similar rash though not as bad and has not sought medical advice. Sexually active with boyfriend.
chiefComplaint: Itchy rash on hands for one week
candidateInfo: You are a Hospital Medical Officer (HMO) in a general medical outpatient clinic. A 19-year-old female computer student presents with an itchy rash on her hands. The rash has been present for about one week and appears as shown in the photograph. There are no other abnormal examination findings apart from the rash.
tasks: Take a further focused history.
Explain the most likely diagnosis to the patient and how this can be confirmed.
Advise the patient about treatment.
openingStatement: 'I want to get rid of this rash on my hands.' Follow with: 'It started about a week ago and I can't stop scratching my hands because of the itch.'
historyWithoutPrompting: Rash started about a week ago. Intense itch, cannot stop scratching hands.
historyWithPrompting: Has not had anything like this before. No rash or itchiness elsewhere on body. Itch is intense and made worse by warming hands (washing up in hot water, bathing or showering). Itch is worst at night and interferes with sleep. Hands have not been in contact with any irritants, chemicals or plants. General health is excellent. No past history of any serious illness. No known allergies. No history of mental or behavioural disturbance. No recent travel away from home. No medication except oral contraception. Boyfriend with whom she is sexually active has had a similar rash though not as bad and has not sought medical advice — does not reveal this spontaneously.
physicalExaminationFindings: Erythematous papular rash on hands, particularly on backs of knuckles and between bases of fingers — consistent with scabies as illustrated.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to diagnose, confirm and treat scabies, and to prevent recurrence.
expectationsOfPerformance: Approach to patient: display interest and intention to deal effectively with the condition; be nonjudgmental about possible sexual transmission of scabies from boyfriend; provide reassurance that condition is simply cured and not serious; obtain compliance with the whole treatment regimen.
History: identify site and severity of itch and question about sexual activity after other possible sources have been excluded.
Confirm diagnosis: advise that diagnosis must be confirmed by taking skin scrapings from the lesions for microscopy.

Patient education and counselling: condition is transmitted by close contact during sexual activity; describe the scabies mite; reassure that condition is not serious but very uncomfortable and readily treatable; patient should advise boyfriend to seek medical advice.

Management: application of permethrin cream or lotion 5% (Lyclear) or benzyl benzoate emulsion 25% (Ascabiol) to entire body from jawline down including nails, flexures and genitals; avoid hot baths or scrubbing before application; treat household contacts even if nonsymptomatic; wash clothing and bed clothes in hot water and expose to sun to dry; repeat treatment in one week if infestation is considered severe; avoid intimate contact with boyfriend until he has also been properly treated.
keyIssues: Approach to patient — ability to establish satisfactory relationship to achieve compliance and cooperation of patient to get boyfriend to seek treatment.
History — ability to take an appropriate history including site and severity of pruritus and sexual partner as source of infection.
Diagnosis — should advise microscopy of skin scrapings to facilitate diagnosis.
Management — provide adequate advice for proper treatment and advise the patient to avoid intimate contact with boyfriend until he has been treated.
criticalErrors: Failure to suspect scabies or to take action to confirm diagnosis.
commentary: Scabies is a highly contagious infestation which is spread through close contact including sexual contact. It is characterised by widespread inflammatory papules and severe pruritus. The female scabies mite (Sarcoptes scabiei) burrows just beneath the skin to lay her eggs and then dies. The eggs hatch into mites which spread out across the skin and live for about 30 days. A mite antigen in the excreta induces a hypersensitivity rash. Clinical features include intense itching, worse at night and when hands and body are warm, with an erythematous papular rash usually on hands and wrists, web spaces, male genitalia, elbows, axillae, feet and ankles, or nipples of females. Diagnosis is confirmed by microscopy of skin scrapings.
underlyingDiagnosis: Scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei infestation)

title: Red painful dry hands in a 30-year-old bricklayer
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: 30-year-old man, working as a bricklayer/contractor for about a year. Hands have been itchy and dry for some months, getting worse. Rash on front and back of both hands. Rash definitely improved significantly after a holiday from work. Brother has skin problems but type unknown. Otherwise healthy and well, no serious past illnesses, no allergies, on no medications.
chiefComplaint: Red dry itchy hands for months
candidateInfo: You are the Hospital Medical Officer (HMO) in a hospital primary care clinic. A 30-year-old man presents with red dry hands. The rash appears as in the illustration below, and is on the front and back of both hands.
tasks: Take a history about the presenting problem.
Explain to him your diagnosis and the possible causes of the condition.
Outline your management of the problem.
openingStatement: 'I've got problems with this rash on my hands.'
historyWithoutPrompting: Hands have been itchy and dry for some months now, and are getting worse. The rash is on no other part of the body. Otherwise healthy and well, no serious past illnesses, no allergies, on no medications.
historyWithPrompting: Rash definitely improved significantly after a holiday from work. Has been working as a bricklayer/contractor for about a year. Brother has skin problems but type unknown.
physicalExaminationFindings: Red, dry, scaly rash on palmar and dorsal surfaces of both hands — consistent with allergic contact dermatitis (occupational dermatitis secondary to concrete/cement exposure) as illustrated.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to diagnose occupational dermatitis and advise an initial management plan.
expectationsOfPerformance: History — must elicit occupational history.

Diagnosis — should suspect allergic contact dermatitis and its cause (cement/concrete) from the history and physical findings involving palmar and dorsal surfaces of both hands. Confirmation of diagnosis is by patch testing by dermatologist (not essential).

Management — explain that the rash will persist as long as there is exposure to cement, although its severity may be reduced by: washing only with water and avoiding soap; patting dry after washing; applying topical corticosteroid cream to gain initial control; oral antibiotics may be required for secondary infection in severe cases; consider using emollient agents for future prevention.

For cement dermatitis, specific measures: avoiding contact with wet cement; using barrier creams before putting on gloves (do not use barrier creams on damaged skin); using protective gloves when working and washing hands after being exposed to cement.
keyIssues: Ability to identify the type and cause of the dermatitis.
Ability to manage occupational contact dermatitis.
Consideration of cement as most likely cause of dermatitis.
criticalErrors: Failure to suspect causal work association in diagnosis.
commentary: Allergic contact dermatitis is due to a delayed hypersensitivity reaction. While physical appearance of the skin can be similar to other forms of dermatitis, rash site and exposure history are critical for diagnosis, management and prevention. Trigger factors only affect some people. Common trigger factors include cosmetic ingredients, topical antibiotics, topical anaesthetics, topical antihistamines, plants (rhus, grevillea, primula, poison ivy), metal salts (nickel sulphate, chromates — as occur in cement and concrete), dyes, rubber/latex, epoxy resins, glues, acrylates, coral. In cement dermatitis, individuals can become sensitised to chromate salts at any time, even after working with cement for many years.
underlyingDiagnosis: Occupational allergic contact dermatitis (cement/chromate sensitisation)

title: Examination of a 20-year-old woman who dislocated her shoulder 6 months ago
patientProfile: 20-year-old woman who dislocated her right shoulder playing competitive basketball 6 months ago. Anterior dislocation complicated by a nerve injury, treated by closed reduction, immobilisation, physiotherapy and gymnasium programme. Attending hospital outpatient department for a check-up, wishes to recommence basketball.
candidateInfo: You are a Hospital Medical Officer (HMO). Your next patient dislocated her shoulder playing competitive basketball six months ago. It was a typical anterior dislocation which was complicated by a nerve injury and was treated by closed reduction, several weeks immobilisation in a sling, subsequent physiotherapy and a gymnasium programme. The patient has returned for a check up at the hospital outpatient department, has told you the shoulder now seems to be working fine, and that she would like to recommence playing basketball next season.
tasks: Perform an appropriately focused and relevant physical examination of the area.
Describe your findings to the observing examiner as you proceed.
Discuss future activities with the patient.
In the final two minutes you will be asked questions by the examiner.
openingStatement: (No specific opening statement given; patient has told you the shoulder now seems to be working fine and she would like to recommence playing basketball next season.)
historyWithoutPrompting: The patient dislocated her right shoulder six months ago whilst playing competitive basketball. It was reduced successfully without anaesthesia at courtside. She initially had an area of numbness over the lateral side of the upper arm below the shoulder tip and could not raise or keep her arm above her head (nerve injury). Over the next month feeling gradually returned and power came back. By four months she had full range of movements. She then began a graduated gymnasium programme. She has resumed fully normal activities of daily living and is keen to return to basketball.
historyWithPrompting: If asked about current symptoms: shoulder now seems to be working fine, no discomfort. Physiotherapist and gym supervisor feel she is ready to return to sporting activity but suggested a final clearance from her doctor.
patientQuestions: Am I able to restart sport in three months, doctor?
Do I need any other tests done?
Would it help if I saw a specialist in Sports Medicine?
Is it likely to happen again?
physicalExaminationFindings: Range of movement — flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, internal and external rotation and circumduction — all normal. Power of movement — normal. Absence of wasting, particularly of deltoid muscle (supplied by axillary nerve). Sensation — now normal. Shoulder function and movement now normal.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to perform a focused examination of the shoulder joint and of axillary nerve function.
expectationsOfPerformance: Check range of movement — flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, internal and external rotation and circumduction — all will be normal.
Check power of movement — normal, using the standard 0–5 scale.
Check for absence of wasting, particularly of deltoid muscle, which is supplied by axillary nerve.
Check sensation — now normal.

After six minutes, examiner will ask: 'Is shoulder function and movement now normal?' (Yes); 'Which nerve is at most risk from the usual type of shoulder dislocation?' (Axillary nerve); then ask candidate to finish discussion with patient about her desire to return to sport.
Advise patient that all seems satisfactory for return to basketball; small likelihood of recurrence; advisable to have shoulder strapped before each game.
keyIssues: Examination of the shoulder area indicating the appropriate technique to evaluate the shoulder and axillary nerve function.
Display appropriate counselling skills when advising the patient concerning her desires to return to sporting activities.
criticalErrors: Inability to assess adequately the normal range of movement of the shoulder joint.
Failure to test the sensory and motor functions covered by the axillary nerve.
Giving inappropriate advice concerning the likelihood of recurrent dislocation.
commentary: A dislocated shoulder is a common injury in body contact sports. The shoulder joint is the most mobile synovial joint in the human body. The major weak spot is below, where protection by surrounding muscles is less, and the capsule is more lax. The common mechanism of dislocation is displacement of humeral head downwards and forwards. The axillary nerve runs between the muscles immediately below the capsule from front to back and is therefore at hazard from stretching injury in shoulder dislocations. The axillary nerve gives motor branches to deltoid and to teres minor, and supplies sensation as the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm. It is important to test for the integrity of the vulnerable nerve before reduction is undertaken and afterwards.
underlyingDiagnosis: Previous anterior shoulder dislocation with axillary nerve injury — now recovered; normal shoulder function.

title: Assessment of a groin lump in a 40-year-old man
patientProfile: 50-year-old man (noted as 40-year-old in heading), works as a builder's labourer. Two weeks ago felt pain in right groin after heavy lifting; a week later noticed a lump. Lump is not acutely painful but uncomfortable on exertion or walking; eased on lying down. Good general health, no relevant past history.
candidateInfo: You are working in a primary care clinic attached to a teaching hospital. Your next patient is a 50-year-old man who works as a builder's labourer. Two weeks ago he felt a pain in his right groin after heavy lifting at work and a week later noticed a lump in the groin which had not been there before. The lump is not acutely painful, but is uncomfortable on exertion or walking. Discomfort is eased on lying down. He is in good general health, without relevant past history and has no problems with lungs or heart, bladder or bowels.
tasks: Perform a focused physical examination to assess the lump, which is illustrated below.
Give your diagnosis and management plan to the patient.
openingStatement: (No specific opening statement given; patient presents with a groin lump noticed after heavy lifting.)
historyWithoutPrompting: Two weeks ago felt pain in right groin after heavy lifting at work. A week later noticed a lump in the groin which had not been there before. The lump is not acutely painful but uncomfortable on exertion or walking. Discomfort is eased on lying down. Good general health, no problems with lungs, heart, bladder or bowels.
physicalExaminationFindings: The patient has a reducible right inguinal hernia. A groin lump with an expansile impulse on coughing confirms the diagnosis. The lump is seen and felt to expand uniformly and expansively when the patient coughs. The impulse ceases and the lump lessens or disappears when he relaxes. The hernia is reducible. It arises from the external ring, has an expansile cough impulse and reduces on lying down.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to perform a focused inguinoscrotal assessment, and to diagnose and advise on management of a reducible groin hernia.
expectationsOfPerformance: Expose the groin area and examine the patient both standing and lying.
Identify a groin lump with an expansile impulse on coughing confirming hernia.
Assess whether the hernia is reducible.
Determine whether it is an inguinal or femoral hernia — this lump arises from the external (superficial) inguinal ring, above the inguinal ligament, medial to the pubic tubercle.
Check both right and left sides — both inguinal and femoral orifices.
Carefully check the spermatic cords/testes and their coverings to exclude additional testicular swelling, hydrocele, or epididymal cyst.
Feel femoral pulses and check for abnormal lymph node enlargements.
Stand the patient up to check for venous swellings or small hernias.
Give diagnosis and management plan to patient — refer appropriately, explaining benefits of surgery usually outweigh risks.
keyIssues: Performance of an appropriately focused inguinoscrotal assessment with appropriate technique and accuracy.
criticalErrors: Failure to display appropriate clinical skills in diagnosis of a reducible groin hernia.
Causing significant patient discomfort by rough technique.
commentary: Inguinoscrotal lumps are very common and present throughout life from birth to old age. The history of onset of pain following a lifting strain and followed by a lump is very suggestive of a groin hernia. The examination should be thorough but gentle. Inspection and palpation of the area enable one to answer: Is a groin hernia present? Is the hernia reducible? Is it an inguinal or femoral hernia? Is the inguinal hernia direct or indirect? Multiple pathologies are very common — both right and left sides must be checked. Additionally check the spermatic cords/testes and their coverings. Although surgery is not obligatory for all hernias, it is likely to be appropriate in this manual worker.
underlyingDiagnosis: Reducible right inguinal hernia

title: Eye problems in an Aboriginal community
patientProfile: A trained nurse who has recently joined the staff of a general practice clinic in a remote setting in the Northern Territory. The nurse has noticed eye problems in the local Aboriginal community and has taken digital photographs of four separate affected individuals (including everted upper eyelids in two photographs). She wants the doctor to explain the condition and what can be done.
candidateInfo: You are a doctor working in a general practice in a remote setting in the Northern Territory. You are about to see a nurse who has recently joined the staff of the general practice clinic. The nurse made a time to see you to discuss eye problems she has noticed in the local Aboriginal community. The nurse has taken digital photographs of eye problems that were noticed in a number of affected individuals (see figures below of four separate individuals). In the upper two photographs the upper eyelid is everted. The nurse wants you to explain what can cause these appearances, and what can be done about the problem in the local community.
tasks: Study the photographs and describe the abnormalities to the clinic nurse.
Explain to the nurse what disease is illustrated in the photographs, and its epidemiology.
Discuss with the nurse how the problem should be managed.
Answer any questions that the nurse may have.
openingStatement: (No specific opening statement given; nurse presents photographs of eye problems in the local Aboriginal community.)
historyWithoutPrompting: The nurse has noticed eye problems in a significant proportion of the local Aboriginal community. She has taken digital photographs of affected individuals. She wants to understand what the condition is and what can be done about it in the local community.
patientQuestions: Do you think these might all be due to the same problem?
How does the infection get from one person to another?
What will happen if the condition is not treated?
Is this condition found anywhere else and how common is it?
Is it preventable? If so, how can it be prevented?
physicalExaminationFindings: Photographs show: Figure 1 — inflammatory follicles on the under surface of the upper tarsus (TF) on everted upper eyelid. Figure 2 — inflammatory changes on the everted upper eyelid (TI). Figure 3 — entropion with trichiasis (TT) and corneal opacification (CO). Figure 4 — further example of trachomatous changes. Eversion of the upper eyelid facilitates diagnosis.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to interpret photographs of trachoma, to assess their knowledge of the disease, and their ability to advise on appropriate management of the condition.
expectationsOfPerformance: Recognise that eversion of the upper eyelid facilitates diagnosis.
Describe the changes in the photographs.
Recognise that the condition is trachoma, because of the setting of an indigenous Australian Aboriginal community, with classical appearances.
Describe the agent responsible — Chlamydia trachomatis — and the vector of transmission (flies, hand contact, fomites).
Understand the pathological changes produced by the organism.
Describe the FISTO staging: Follicles, Inflammation, Scarring, Trichiasis, Opacity of the cornea.
Describe simple and appropriate measures to reduce the risk of infection — the SAFE strategy: Surgery, Antibiotics (azithromycin), Facial cleanliness, Environmental upgrade.
Explain that azithromycin is specific for Chlamydia trachomatis.
Explain that trachoma is the most common infectious cause of blindness globally and is preventable.
keyIssues: Interpretation of clinical photographs of eye changes produced by trachoma.
Knowledge and understanding of trachoma, its mode of transmission and measures used to reduce the risk of infection.
criticalErrors: Failure to recognise trachoma.
commentary: Globally, trachoma is the most common infectious cause of blindness and is a preventable disease. It is endemic in Africa, the Middle East, Asia and Aboriginal communities of Australia. More than six million people are blind as a result of trachoma. It is caused by Chlamydia trachomatis, transmitted by direct contact or fomites between children and their mothers, and by flies. Poor facial hygiene facilitates spread. Recurrent infection causes chronic conjunctival inflammation followed by scarring of the tarsal conjunctiva, leading to entropion, trichiasis, corneal abrasions, infection, opacification and blindness. The WHO SAFE strategy (Surgery, Antibiotics, Facial cleanliness, Environmental upgrade) is the recommended public health approach. The WHO simplified grading scheme (TF, TI, TS, TT, CO) can be taught to community health workers.
underlyingDiagnosis: Trachoma (Chlamydia trachomatis infection)

title: Positive test for hepatitis C in a 26-year-old woman
patientProfile: 26-year-old woman who was found to be hepatitis C positive when she attended the Red Cross blood bank as a blood donor one week previously. Other blood tests were negative for hepatitis B and HIV. She had never given blood before and had not been tested for these infections previously.
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. Your next patient is a 26-year-old woman who has been sent to see you because she was tested for hepatitis B and C and for HIV when she attended the Red Cross blood bank as a blood donor one week previously and was found to be hepatitis C positive. She has just been notified that she was found to be hepatitis C positive and advised to see her local doctor for further assessment. Other blood tests were negative for both hepatitis B and HIV. She had never given blood before, and had not been tested for any of these infections previously.
tasks: Take a relevant history from the woman.
Advise her about subsequent management and likely prognosis.
openingStatement: (No specific opening statement given; patient presents after notification of hepatitis C positive result.)
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient has just been notified of a positive hepatitis C result from blood bank testing. She feels well, has a normal level of energy and no difficulties at work. No symptoms of liver disease.
historyWithPrompting: She was an intermittent intravenous drug-user over a two-year period, but last had a 'shot' about six years ago. On occasions she had shared needles with a friend. She has never had a blood transfusion or given blood products. She has had two sexual partners in her life — current relationship has lasted four years, married two years ago. No family history of hepatitis. No previous operations or illnesses. No tattoos or body piercing. Works in a hospital as a cleaner. Never had a needlestick injury. Alcohol intake is 1–2 glasses of wine a day. Taking the oral contraceptive pill (Microgynon 30). Wishes to have a child in about two years time. Has never been jaundiced. No symptoms of liver disease (no tiredness, bruising, itch, appetite change, abdominal discomfort, gastrointestinal bleeding, leg swelling).
patientQuestions: Do I need to have any more tests?
Will I be able to have a baby?
Can anyone catch this infection from me?
Must you notify this to the health department?
aimsOfStation: To assess the ability of the candidate to take a focused history assessing the possible mechanism for her becoming hepatitis C positive, and then to appropriately advise the patient in regard to the mode of contracting the disease, the tests required to assess the current activity of the disease, the likely long-term outcome, whether any treatment is likely to be helpful, the likely possibility of transmitting the disease to other people, and the need for notification of the disease.
expectationsOfPerformance: Take a focused history to determine the likely source of infection — intravenous drug use with shared needles is the most likely cause.
Assess current status — no current symptoms of liver disease; moderate alcohol intake.
Order liver function tests (ALT) and HCV PCR to assess whether infection has spontaneously cleared and any effect on the liver.
Consider indirect Coombs test given shared needle use (blood group immunisation).
Counsel that hepatitis C is transmitted mainly via infected human blood; risk of transmission to husband through sexual activity is extremely low.
Advise that hepatitis C is a notifiable disease — notification is confidential.

Advise that pregnancy should not be allowed until at least 6 months after cessation of antiviral therapy if given (ribavirin is teratogenic); risk of vertical transmission to baby is about 5% in PCR-positive patients.
Advise no sharing of objects with potential for blood contamination (razors, toothbrushes); no recommendations against breastfeeding.
If ALT elevated, refer to gastroenterologist for full liver assessment including biopsy; antiviral therapy (interferon and ribavirin) may be indicated.
Natural history: 15–50% resolve completely; 50–75% have chronic infection; 20% of those with elevated ALT develop cirrhosis; 1–5% develop hepatocellular carcinoma.
Provide patient with appropriate supplementary patient education material.
keyIssues: Taking a focused history in regard to determining the source of the infection.
Advising the patient appropriately regarding subsequent care, the risk of liver pathology, and the need to ensure blood transmission does not occur.
criticalErrors: Failure to recognise the need for LFT (ALT) assessments.
Advising the patient of a benign course of disease in all instances.
commentary: Hepatitis C is a single stranded RNA virus. Risk factors include intravenous drug use (70%), sexual exposure (~10%), blood transfusion (6%), occupational exposure (3%), unknown (~10%). The viral infection is established and chronic at the time of diagnosis in most patients. If identified early, treatment with interferon within 3–6 months of infection can prevent chronicity in 98% of patients. The natural history: 15–50% resolve completely; the remaining 50–75% have chronic infection (PCR positive). Twenty percent of those with an elevated ALT will develop cirrhosis, of which 1–5% will develop hepatocellular carcinoma, and 20% will develop liver failure requiring transplantation. Good communication skills are required to address sensitive issues in this situation. Hepatitis C is a notifiable disease with confidentiality maintained.
underlyingDiagnosis: Hepatitis C infection (likely acquired through shared intravenous drug use needles)

title: Diagnosis of 'brain death' prior to organ donation
patientProfile: A secondary school science teacher who is preparing to chair a Year 11 seminar titled 'The Gift of Life' about organ transplantation and donation. He has found a brain death protocol on the internet and wants the doctor to explain it to him in understandable language.
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. Your next patient is a secondary school science teacher. Next week a doctor from the Australian Kidney Foundation is going to speak at a class seminar to all the Year 11 students about organ transplantation and the donation of organs and tissues from donors who have died. The teacher is to chair the seminar which has been titled 'The Gift of Life'. The teacher has heard about 'brain death' and found the protocol below on the internet and printed it out. He wants you to explain it to him in understandable language to help him comprehend the implications and facilitate his chairmanship.
tasks: Discuss the subject with him and respond to his queries.
openingStatement: (No specific opening statement given; teacher presents with a printed brain death protocol from the internet and asks for explanation.)
historyWithoutPrompting: The teacher is preparing to chair a school seminar on organ transplantation and donation. He has found a brain death protocol on the internet and wants it explained in plain language. He is not a medical professional.
patientQuestions: What does 'brain death' mean?
What are these predetermined criteria about?
Can you explain these tests to me?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of the principles of diagnosis of brain death and its certification. To assess the candidate's communication skills in public education by discussing and explaining aspects of cadaver organ donation in the context of brain death with a layperson.
expectationsOfPerformance: Explain that in Australia, death can be certified by either permanent and irreversible cessation of heartbeat, or permanent and irreversible loss of brain function.
Explain the concept of 'brain death' and its importance in transplantation — removing organs from a brain-dead, heart-beating cadaver minimises warm ischaemia time.

Explain the predetermined criteria: appropriate clinical setting (massive head injury or catastrophic stroke); deep unresponsive coma; exclusion of other contributors (hypothermia, CNS depressant drugs, neuromuscular blocking drugs, endocrine problems, hypoglycaemia).

Explain the tests — these confirm destroyed and absent brain stem reflexes from midbrain to medulla: fixed pupils unresponsive to light; absent corneal reflexes; absent pain response in cranial nerve distribution; absent gag reflex; absent oculocephalic reflexes; absent vestibulo-ocular reflexes; no spontaneous respirations after 10 minutes (apnoea test with high CO2).
Explain that diagnosis requires two independent doctors including the intensive care consultant, neither a member of the transplant team; two groups of tests preferably separated by 24 hours.
Distinguish brain death from the vegetative state (which is NOT brain death).
Explain that brain death has been ratified by all major religions.
Discuss the sensitive human, ethical and cultural issues around obtaining consent from next of kin for organ donation.
Explain that diagnosis of brain death is made by meticulous clinical observations and does not require elaborate technology.
keyIssues: Ability to discuss principles of 'brain death' to a lay person.
Ability to discuss principles of cadaveric organ donation for transplantation.
commentary: The candidate should be familiar with Australian legislation providing for legal certification of death by either permanent and irreversible cessation of heartbeat or permanent and irreversible loss of brain function. The concept of brain death has important implications in transplantation. Removing organs from the brain-dead, heart-beating cadaver minimises critical warm ischaemia time. The criteria and tests listed ensure that irreversible loss of function has occurred in both higher cortical brain functions and brain stem functions. Meticulous application of these universally accepted criteria has ensured that brain death can be diagnosed clinically with absolute confidence and without any risk of misdiagnosis. The vegetative state — deep coma with present but ineffective spontaneous breathing and retention of other brain stem activities — is NOT brain death. Doctors must employ great empathy and compassion in obtaining consent from relatives of the brain-dead individual.
underlyingDiagnosis: Brain death (permanent and irreversible loss of brain and brain stem function)
differentialDiagnosis: Deep coma from reversible causes (drugs, hypothermia, metabolic)

title: Breast biopsy concerns in a 20-year-old woman with a family history of breast cancer
patientProfile: 20-year-old single woman with cyclical mastalgia for 6 months. Physical examination of breasts was normal. Ultrasound showed an impalpable, focal well-circumscribed solid parenchymal lesion in the right breast 1 cm in diameter consistent with a fibroadenoma. Referred to a female surgeon who suggested ultrasound-guided percutaneous core biopsy. Patient is unhappy with this advice and wants the lump removed. Her 50-year-old mother had a Stage 1 breast cancer treated by mastectomy and axillary dissection five years ago.
candidateInfo: Your next patient for office consultation in a primary care community practice clinic is for review of a 20-year-old single woman whom you saw four weeks ago with a complaint of cyclical mastalgia for the last six months. Physical examination of the breasts was normal. She had no previous history of breast problems. Her 50-year-old mother, also a patient of the clinic, had a Stage 1 breast cancer treated by mastectomy and axillary dissection five years ago and is well on follow up. You ordered an ultrasound of the breasts in this young woman, which showed an impalpable, focal well-circumscribed solid parenchymal lesion in the right breast 1 cm in diameter consistent with a fibroadenoma. You referred her to the female surgeon who treated her mother, who suggested an ultrasound-guided percutaneous core biopsy to confirm the imaging diagnosis of benign fibroadenoma. The patient was also reassured that if this showed, as expected, a benign lesion, surgery would not be required, and she could be observed clinically with periodic ultrasound assessments. The patient is unhappy with this advice and feels she would like the lump removed and has come back to you to discuss this further. She is worried that the lump may be malignant or will become so, and feels that just taking a piece of it will leave her still worried.
tasks: Discuss her concerns with her and advise her on the future management you would propose.
openingStatement: I think this lump should be removed.
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient presents unhappy with the surgeon's advice for core biopsy and observation. She is worried the lump may be malignant or will become so, and feels that just taking a piece of it will leave her still worried.
patientQuestions: How can you be sure it's not a cancer?
Isn't it likely to turn into a cancer?
Can't I just have it out and then forget about it?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's counselling and educational skills in a patient with concerns about familial breast cancer risk.
expectationsOfPerformance: Reassure about likely benign diagnosis.
Reassure that impalpable fibroadenomas are very common in nonsymptomatic women on imaging, do not require excision, are not cancers and do not become cancerous.
Reassure that the biopsy takes several representative pieces and can save unnecessary surgery and avoid potentially unsightly scarring.
Reassure that with a homogeneous lesion such as this, the biopsy could be relied upon to give a definitive diagnosis.
Show sympathy for concerns about cancer and about continuing clinical and ultrasound monitoring; reassure of noninvasive nature of ultrasound monitoring.
Assure that if patient is still concerned, the surgeon would be likely to accede to her wishes, and if not, she could be referred for a second opinion.
Whatever the patient decides, periodic followup with clinical and imaging reviews will be advisable because of her family history and her concerns.
Explain the 'triple test check' — clinical, imaging, and pathology all confirmed and negative for cancer — makes the lesion virtually certain to be benign.
keyIssues: Counselling and communication skills in dealing with an anxious patient.
Knowledge of pathology and natural history of breast fibroadenomas.
commentary: Breast cancer is very common in Australian women and around 1 in 14 women will develop breast cancer in their lifetime. The risk is increased in the presence of family history of breast cancer in first degree relatives. Benign impalpable (or palpable) fibroadenomas and other benign parenchymal lesions are very common in this age group. No convincing evidence exists that benign fibroadenomas are premalignant. An appropriate core biopsy would take five or more representative samples and would be expected to give a definitively accurate diagnosis with minimal likelihood of either a falsely negative or falsely positive result. If the 'triple test' (clinical, imaging, and pathology) is negative, the lesion is virtually certain to be benign. The patient will require continuing periodic clinical and imaging review from her family doctor and surgeon. If the patient remains unconvinced and unhappy, the best decision may be to agree to her own wishes that the lesion is removed, requiring an image-guided needle localisation operation.
underlyingDiagnosis: Benign breast fibroadenoma (impalpable, right breast)

title: Urinary frequency in a 60-year-old man
patientProfile: 60-year-old man, infrequent attender at the practice, consulting about urinary symptoms. Father died of prostate cancer. Has concerns about cancer and loss of sexual function.
chiefComplaint: I seem to need to go to the toilet more often lately
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. Your next patient is a 60-year-old man. He has attended the practice infrequently in the past. Today he is consulting you about urinary symptoms.
tasks: Take a focused history with regard to the presenting symptoms.
Give the examiner a summary of the patient's presenting history with the most likely diagnosis.
Ask the examiner what aspects of physical examination are most likely to confirm this diagnosis and any initial office tests you would perform. The examiner will respond accordingly.
Tell the patient your diagnostic conclusions, what investigations are indicated and the reasons for these.
openingStatement: I seem to need to go to the toilet to pass urine more often lately doctor.
historyWithoutPrompting: Over about the last three months the patient has been passing urine more often during the day and has to get out of bed to pass urine at least twice each night. He also suffers from leg cramps, worse at night, and his feet have felt slightly numb. He has felt thirstier lately and his mouth has been dry. He has been worried that his symptoms are due to prostate trouble because of his father's history and recent publicity about prostate cancer.
historyWithPrompting: He has lost 4 kg in weight over the past three months. He feels tired recently. He admits to loss of libido and inability to obtain and sustain an erection over last 3-4 months if asked. He admits to recent deterioration in eyesight if asked ('I suppose I need new glasses at my age'). No dysuria. No incontinence. Normal stream. No other symptoms suggestive of prostatism with bladder neck obstruction. No chest pain or breathlessness. Nonsmoker. Two cans light ale daily. No drug sensitivities. Father died from a stroke aged 80 years — also had prostate cancer. Mother in nursing home with dementia. No serious past illnesses.
patientQuestions: Is this prostate trouble?
Could I have cancer?
physicalExaminationFindings: He is significantly overweight with abdominal obesity. Blood pressure is 140/90 mmHg, pulse is regular. He has mild blunting of all sensory modalities in his feet. Neurological examination is otherwise normal. Genital and rectal examinations are normal, without evidence of prostatic enlargement or nodularity. Urinalysis: positive for glucose (++++), ketones (+), negative for protein. Random blood sugar (glucometer): 21 mmol/L.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's history-taking skills, knowledge of the symptomatology and confirmatory testing for maturity onset Type 2 diabetes and the investigations which should be undertaken in a recently diagnosed diabetic.
expectationsOfPerformance: Summarise the problems presented by the patient: urinary symptoms, thirst, weight loss; numbness in feet and visual disturbance; fear of cancer; erectile dysfunction/reduced libido; and maturity-onset diabetes as the most likely diagnosis.
Request urinalysis and random blood sugar using glucometer.

Order further investigations: urea, creatinine and electrolytes; glucose tolerance test to confirm definitive diagnosis; microscopy and culture urine (checking for microalbuminuria); serum lipids; ECG; glycosylated Hb as baseline; full blood examination and ESR; prostate specific antigen (PSA) level in view of family history and concerns.
keyIssues: Diagnosis of Type 2 diabetes mellitus by appropriate consultation (history, examination and office tests).
Appropriate further investigation of newly diagnosed diabetic.
Recognition of fear of cancer and sexual dysfunction.
criticalErrors: Failure to test urine or measure random blood sugar at this consultation.
commentary: The constellation of symptoms of polyuria, thirst, weight loss, sensory and visual disturbances, and erectile dysfunction, should raise suspicion of maturity-onset Type 2 diabetes mellitus. Urinary chemical testing and random blood sugar assessment applied to overweight adults over 45 years will pick up at least as many nonsymptomatic undiagnosed diabetics as are found on symptomatic presentation.
underlyingDiagnosis: Type 2 diabetes mellitus

title: Neonatal jaundice in the first day of life
patientProfile: Baby Jessica, born 24 hours ago, term vaginal delivery, birth weight 3700 g, Apgar scores 9 and 10. First child of healthy mother. Jaundice noticed soon after birth within first 24 hours. Feeding well from breast. Mother wants to go home as soon as possible.
candidateInfo: Baby Helen is brought to see you in a general practice setting, as her mother is concerned about her continuing jaundice. Helen is now two weeks old and was born at term by easy vaginal delivery weighing 3.7 kg. Apgar scores were 9 and 10 (at 1 and 5 minutes respectively). She became jaundiced in the neonatal period starting on day three. Investigations then revealed no blood group incompatibility, both mother and baby being group O positive and no red blood cell (including enzymes) abnormality. The infant was treated with phototherapy for two days. Since discharge from hospital at eight days of age the jaundice has persisted and the mother is concerned. Baby is feeding well from the breast. Current weight is 3.9 kg. The baby was active and clinically normal apart from the jaundice when you saw her yesterday. You arranged investigations as set out below. The mother has now returned with the baby to discuss the results and your advice about treatment. Investigation results: Serum bilirubin Total: 250 umol/L; Conjugated: less than 10 umol/L; Neonatal thyroid screening: normal; Urine culture: sterile; Full Blood Examination (FBE): normal.
tasks: Obtain any further necessary history you require. You should not take more than 2-3 minutes to do this.
Discuss the results of investigations with the mother.
Explain the diagnosis to her and advise about future management.
openingStatement: Does Helen need to go under those lights again?
historyWithoutPrompting: This is the mother's first baby. She has always intended to breastfeed, and established breastfeeding successfully. Baby has been normal in all respects since birth. She has gained weight and is having normal bowel actions — motions and urine are both normally coloured. Mother is a healthy, breastfeeding mother, nonsmoker, on no medication.
historyWithPrompting: Urine and stools are of normal colour (key question to establish). Baby is feeding well and gaining weight. No medications taken by mother.
patientQuestions: Does Helen need to go under those lights again?
Is there something wrong with my milk, should I stop breastfeeding?
If breastfeeding is suspended for 1-2 days to see if the jaundice decreases, will this affect my capacity to continue breastfeeding?
physicalExaminationFindings: The baby was active and clinically normal apart from the jaundice. Serum bilirubin Total: 250 umol/L; Conjugated: less than 10 umol/L. Neonatal thyroid screening: normal. Urine culture: sterile. FBE: normal.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of the causes of persisting neonatal jaundice, ability to make an appropriate diagnosis and to convey this to the patient.
expectationsOfPerformance: Recognise the significance of the baby's unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and come to the most likely diagnosis (breastmilk jaundice) without need for further investigations.
Establish that urine and stools are of normal colour.
Explain that the most likely diagnosis is breast milk jaundice.
Explain that the condition is self-limiting and requires no treatment.

Advise that the diagnosis can be confirmed by suspending (not stopping) breastfeeding for 24-48 hours which will result in a fall in the serum bilirubin, after which breastfeeding can be continued. This is acceptable but not necessary.
Advise the mother to express her milk in order to maintain lactation if temporary suspension of breastfeeding is advised.
Emphasise that there is nothing wrong with her milk. No treatment is indicated and further phototherapy is not indicated.
keyIssues: Establishing that urine and stools are of normal colour.
Accurate interpretation of important pathology results.
Reassurance to mother that her milk is not harmful to her baby.
Accurate explanation of the possible causes for the jaundice and logically excluding other important diagnoses.
criticalErrors: Not appreciating the significance of predominant unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and insisting that the baby has biliary atresia or haemolytic disease.
Insisting that the breast milk is unsatisfactory for the baby and recommending permanent cessation of breastfeeding.
commentary: Jaundice in the newborn is very common, affecting at least 50% of full term infants and a significantly higher proportion of premature infants. Generally termed physiological jaundice, this is an adaptation process to extrauterine life that takes some days to mature. Breast milk jaundice fits into the unconjugated variety but should be considered as a diagnosis by exclusion of the other important causes of unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia. Breast milk jaundice is a benign condition, which requires no treatment except explanation and support. Breastfeeding should not be interrupted. The cause is unknown, thought to be due to a factor in breast milk that causes increased enteric absorption of bilirubin. The jaundice may persist for up to three months but over that time the baby thrives and remains well.
underlyingDiagnosis: Breast milk jaundice
differentialDiagnosis: Haemolytic disease of the newborn (ABO or Rh incompatibility)

title: Immunisation advice to the parent of a 6-week-old baby
patientProfile: Baby Laura, 6 weeks old, breastfed, gaining weight normally. First child of couple. Mother wants to know about immunisation after hearing conflicting views in the media. Baby given first hepatitis B vaccination soon after birth. General examination of baby reveals no abnormality.
chiefComplaint: Advice about immunisation for my baby
candidateInfo: Your next patient is baby Laura brought by her mother to a general practice at six weeks of age, as part of routine postnatal followup. Laura is the couple's first child. The babe is breastfed and gaining weight normally. Her mother wants to know what you would advise about immunisation because she and her husband have recently heard conflicting views expressed in the media. General examination of the baby reveals no abnormality. She was given her first hepatitis B vaccination soon after birth.
tasks: Outline the current immunisation protocol you would recommend and what diseases the programme is protecting against.
Discuss any concerns the parents have about immunisation.
openingStatement: What vaccines or injections will Laura need to be immunised with, up to school age?
historyWithoutPrompting: Mother is an educated parent in a stable marriage. She is widely read and takes an interest in popular medical articles especially during her pregnancy, as she was concerned about some information she had read and heard about immunisation and its possible adverse effects.
historyWithPrompting: Mother has concerns about whooping cough vaccine causing brain damage, side effects of vaccines, whether some children should not have vaccines, whether homeopathic oral vaccines are an alternative, whether chicken pox vaccine is available, and whether high fever after injections can be managed.
patientQuestions: What vaccines or injections will Laura need to be immunised with, up to school age?
What problems might she have?
Are there any children who shouldn't have these vaccines?
I've heard the whooping cough vaccine can cause brain damage. Can we leave it out?
What other side effects happen with these vaccines?
What if we didn't give these vaccines — can't you just treat any infections with antibiotics anyway?
I have a friend who goes to a homoeopath and he gives the same vaccines, but very diluted, by mouth and there are no side effects. Is that an alternative?
What about other alternative vaccines?
I've heard babies can get high fever and be quite sick after some of these injections. Can you do anything to ease the side effects?
I've also heard about a vaccine for chicken pox. Is this available and do you advise it?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of the currently recommended immunisation programme in Australia, knowledge of side effects and the latest information concerning claims of associations with serious medical conditions.
expectationsOfPerformance: Give the parent a succinct and accurate regimen for the immunisation currently recommended, with special reference to those given at two months.

At birth: Hepatitis B (hepB). At 2 and 4 months: DTPa, Hib, OPV/IPV, hepB, 7VPCV. At 6 months: DTPa, OPV/IPV, (hepB in some states), 7VPCV. At 12 months: MMR, Hib, MenC, (hepB in some states). At 18 months: VZV, 23VPPV. At 4 years: DTPa, MMR, OPV/IPV.
Address specific concerns about possible side effects and the incidence of these.

Discuss the few absolute contraindications to vaccination: encephalopathy within seven days of a previous DTP-containing vaccine or an immediate severe or anaphylactic reaction to vaccination with DTP.
Advise that live vaccines (MMR, oral poliomyelitis, rubella, chicken pox) should not be administered to immunocompromised patients.
Advise that there is no evidence for the efficacy of alternative (homoeopathic) oral vaccines given sublingually.
Stress that many of these diseases are still prevalent in the community.
Suggest paracetamol for fever and pain after vaccination as necessary.
keyIssues: Knowledge of basic principles of current immunisation regimens.
Explanation and accurate information regarding benefits of immunisation.
Exploration of parental concerns.
criticalErrors: Candidate provides wrong advice regarding contraindications to immunisation.
Recommendation or acceptance of sublingual homoeopathic vaccines.
commentary: This scenario is concerned with counselling a young mother on the advantages and disadvantages of immunisation. This requires of candidates a sound knowledge of the topic and an ability to give the information to the parent in a manner that gives a balanced overview, without domineering with their own personal feelings. Candidates should be aware of the absolute contraindications to the standard vaccinations and also the false contraindications which are so often quoted. The reported association of measles vaccination with autism — the knowledgeable candidate will be aware that no association has been convincingly demonstrated and several studies show no link at all.
underlyingDiagnosis: Routine immunisation counselling (no underlying disease)

title: Dark urine, facial swelling and irritability in a 5-year-old boy
patientProfile: 5-year-old boy with swelling around the eyes, only passing small amounts of dark urine, restless and irritable in the past 12 hours. Had school sores (impetigo) three weeks ago treated successfully with topical antibiotic. No other prior illnesses. Both parents well, only child, always kept in good health.
chiefComplaint: Swelling around the eyes and dark urine
candidateInfo: A five-year-old boy is brought to the Emergency Department because of swelling around the eyes. He has only been passing small amounts of urine, which is dark in colour. In the past 12 hours he has become restless and irritable. The child had school sores (impetigo) three weeks ago, treated successfully with a topical antibiotic cream, but has had no other prior illnesses. Both parents are well. The child is an only child and has always kept in good health.
tasks: Ask the examiner for the relevant physical findings you wish to elicit.
Discuss with the parent your provisional diagnosis.
Advise details of any investigations that are required and advise the parent of the treatment that will be needed.
openingStatement: Why has this happened?
historyWithoutPrompting: Swelling around the eyes, only passing small amounts of dark urine, restless and irritable in the past 12 hours. Had school sores (impetigo) three weeks ago treated successfully with topical antibiotic cream.
patientQuestions: Why has this happened?
Will my son be all right?
What is going to happen now?
What are they looking for with these tests?
How long will it take to get results?
Is that really necessary? (if kidney biopsy mentioned)
Why is blood testing not enough?
physicalExaminationFindings: Resting blood pressure 145/90 mmHg. No postural hypotension. Temperature 36.5°C. Pulse 90/min regular. Periorbital oedema present; no oedema elsewhere; no ascites or pleural effusions. Cardiovascular system normal. Liver edge palpable just below the costal margin. Optic fundi normal. ENT examination normal. Urine dipstick strongly positive (++) for blood and protein.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to recognise that this child most likely has acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) which requires hospitalisation in view of hypertension and recent irritability.
expectationsOfPerformance: Explain the cause of the child's clinical picture in terms the parent can understand: the original skin streptococcal infection (impetigo) has triggered an immune reaction against the organism occurring in the kidneys, causing a fall in urine output, salt and fluid retention causing swelling and raised blood pressure.
Advise hospital admission is desirable in view of the acute presentation and hypertension.
Order investigations: urea and electrolytes, creatinine, inflammatory markers — C3, C4, ASOT, DNAase B; urine micro and culture; full blood examination.

Immediate management: admission to hospital; strict fluid balance and restricted fluid intake; test all urine; four-hourly blood pressure and other vital signs; daily weight; low protein, low salt/high carbohydrate diet; antihypertensive treatment; penicillin therapy may be suggested.
Advise that renal biopsy is not needed for diagnosis at this stage.
Future management: monitor blood pressure and renal function; regular urinalysis (microscopic haematuria may persist for up to two years); long term prognosis is excellent.
keyIssues: Diagnosis of acute PSGN.
Ability to specify appropriate plan of investigations.
Development of coherent treatment plan.
criticalErrors: Failure to admit to hospital.
commentary: This scenario involves diagnosis, from clinical signs and appropriate investigations, and an empathic explanation of treatment. From the information given, the candidate should be able to arrive at the correct diagnosis and investigate and treat appropriately. Failing to do so puts the patient at risk. While classical poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis has become rare in many parts of Australia, knowledge of the condition is important in considering the differential diagnosis of this child's symptoms.
underlyingDiagnosis: Acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN)

title: Fever and sore throat in a 5-year-old boy
patientProfile: Peter, a 5-year-old boy, brought by his parent with a fever of 40°C that developed overnight. Complains of intensely sore throat and finds it sore when swallowing food or fluid, although he is able to do so. No past history of significant illness. Father is an office-worker, mother is at home. Three-year-old sister at kindergarten is well. No previous antibiotic reactions.
candidateInfo: Peter, a five-year-old boy is brought to you in a general practice setting by his parent with a fever of 40°C that developed overnight. He complains of an intensely sore throat and finds it sore when he swallows food or fluid, although he is able to do so.
tasks: Indicate to the examiner the clinical examination you would perform to diagnose the problem. The examiner will give you the results of the physical examination.
Discuss with the parent any investigations you feel are necessary.
Explain your diagnosis and suggest management to the mother.
openingStatement: He had a cold yesterday doctor and now he has got this nasty wheeze still. Does he need an antibiotic?
historyWithoutPrompting: Fever of 40°C developed overnight. Intensely sore throat, sore when swallowing food or fluid but able to do so.
patientQuestions: How would he get this infection?
Is he likely to get it again?
What causes this infection?
I've heard that this sort of infection can damage your heart or your kidneys or something — is that right?
How long will he take to get better?
Is antibiotic therapy required?
physicalExaminationFindings: A flushed child, tonsils acutely inflamed with follicular exudate, with moderately enlarged and tender cervical lymph nodes on both sides. Temperature 40°C, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiration rate 24/min, pulse rate 110/min. Tympanic membranes are normal on otoscopy. Examination is otherwise normal.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to make a diagnosis of tonsillitis most likely due to Group A β-haemolytic Streptococcus. Some investigations to confirm this are indicated.
expectationsOfPerformance: Enquire as to the important clinical findings on examination allowing confident diagnosis of acute tonsillitis, most likely bacterial.
A throat swab could help confirm this prior to antibiotic treatment.
Explain in simple terms the diagnosis and its associated complications in a manner that the parent can understand.
Arrange for further review in a few days to ensure the expected recovery is occurring.
Immediate management: throat swab for culture (appropriate but not obligatory); check for antibiotic reaction previously; prescribe oral penicillin; advise frequent fluids; prescribe analgesics.
Stress that a full course of 10 days penicillin treatment is required.
Indicate that viral infection may cause similar features, as might acute infectious mononucleosis.
Suggest review in a few days or earlier if concerned and if the child has not responded as expected.
keyIssues: Appropriate examination interpretation, with appropriate diagnosis.
Adequate treatment plan.
Appropriate explanation.
criticalErrors: Failure to consider streptococcal tonsillitis as the diagnosis.
Failure to discuss followup and screening for other conditions if there is no initial improvement.
commentary: This scenario assesses the ability of the candidate to come to a logical conclusion as to the most likely diagnosis (acute bacterial tonsillitis) in this situation based on the information provided and knowledge of the natural history of disease processes. The scenario tests diagnostic acumen by showing how several conditions can be safely excluded because of the history and the time frame and gives scope to considering other diagnoses if the provisional diagnosis is not confirmed.
underlyingDiagnosis: Acute bacterial tonsillitis, most likely Group A β-haemolytic Streptococcus

title: Breech presentation in labour at 38 weeks in a 25-year-old woman
patientProfile: 25-year-old woman in her second pregnancy at 38 weeks, in early labour. Vaginal examination unexpectedly reveals a breech presentation with both legs apparently extended. Cervical dilatation 4 cm. Previous pregnancy resulted in normal cephalic vaginal delivery of a 4 kg baby at 41 weeks. Current pregnancy uneventful to date, fundal height 38 cm.
chiefComplaint: So it is a breech, doctor. Does that cause any problems?
candidateInfo: You are working in the Emergency Department of a general hospital. This patient is a 25-year-old woman in her second pregnancy, at 38 weeks of gestation and is in early labour. Vaginal examination unexpectedly reveals a breech presentation: the legs of the fetus are apparently both extended. The cervical dilatation is 4 cm. The previous pregnancy resulted in a normal cephalic vaginal delivery of a 4 kg baby at 41 weeks of gestation. The current pregnancy has been uneventful to date and the fundal height is 38 cm above the pubic symphysis at the time of admission in labour at 38 weeks.
tasks: Advise the patient of the possibilities in regard to subsequent management and the pros and cons of these.
openingStatement: So it is a breech, doctor. Does that cause any problems?
historyWithoutPrompting: Breech presentation not previously diagnosed. All tests and progress have been normal, as was previous pregnancy. Previous delivery was spontaneous vaginal delivery of a 4 kg baby at 41 weeks with no problems. Only a very small episiotomy was necessary. No family history of diabetes or other problems. Patient would prefer vaginal delivery if possible but would accept Caesarean section if recommended.
historyWithPrompting: Patient desires: would prefer vaginal delivery if possible but would accept Caesarean section if recommended as necessary. Antenatal course in this pregnancy has been normal. No family history of diabetes or other problems.
patientQuestions: What are my options regarding delivery?
Are there any significant risks to the baby or me if I have my baby normally?
What are the potential problems to the baby of vaginal delivery versus Caesarean section?
physicalExaminationFindings: No additional findings beyond those outlined in the candidate's instructions need be given.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to appropriately advise a patient concerning the advantages and disadvantages of vaginal breech delivery or Caesarean section when the fetus is found to be presenting by the breech in early labour at 38 weeks of gestation.
expectationsOfPerformance: Diagnose breech presentation in early labour. The type of breech presentation (extended legs) is a favourable one, and as she is keen to avoid a Caesarean section, an attempt at vaginal breech delivery would be appropriate.
X-ray pelvimetry is unnecessary in view of the size of her previous baby (4000 g) born at 41 weeks; the current baby is being delivered three weeks earlier and should be smaller.
Cardiotocography (CTG) monitoring is necessary as there is an increased risk of cord prolapse associated with breech presentation.
Vaginal examination as soon as the membranes rupture to exclude cord prolapse and confirm the type of breech presentation is mandatory.
Advise that ultrasound examination would have been of value to check fetal size, type of breech presentation, and whether the fetal neck was extended, had the breech been diagnosed prior to labour.
Caesarean section would be indicated if there was slow progress of labour, fetal distress, or a significant CTG abnormality in the first stage of labour.
Advise that the risk of vaginal breech delivery is higher than that of delivery by Caesarean section, with the risk being approximately doubled.
Advise that about 3-5% of patients do have problems during the latter stages of delivery due to difficulty delivering the legs, arms or head.
keyIssues: Ability of the candidate to advise and counsel a patient of the current options in regard to breech delivery by vaginal or Caesarean delivery.
criticalErrors: Failure to advise of the appropriate risks of vaginal breech delivery.
Recommending that external cephalic version should be attempted despite the fact she is in labour.
Indicating to the patient that vaginal breech delivery is absolutely contraindicated despite her desires.
commentary: Approximately 4% of all babies present by the breech, and vaginal delivery is safe in selected patients. This particularly applies where the baby is of normal size (between 2.5 and 4.0 kg); the breech presentation is a complete breech or a breech with extended legs; the fetal neck is not extended; where labour occurs spontaneously and progresses at the appropriate rate; and where the pelvic dimensions are normal. The recent Term Breech Trial clearly showed the risk of vaginal delivery was higher than that associated with delivery by Caesarean section. If the candidate suggests external cephalic version should be attempted at this time, when she is clearly in labour, this is contraindicated and clearly WRONG.
underlyingDiagnosis: Breech presentation in labour at 38 weeks

title: The 4 station progressive OSCE
subcategory: Interactive Clinical Assessment — Other Methods and OSCE Modifications
patientProfile: 68-year-old man with a three month history of dysphagia, weight loss, past history of heartburn, smoker (20 cigarettes/day), alcohol 20g/day, past myocardial infarction, on aspirin, antihypertensives and medication for obstructive lung disease, previous knee replacement complicated by DVT.
candidateInfo: A proposed examination sequence (OSLER) is outlined. Each MCAT station is 10 minutes and supervised by an assessor. A global marking scheme is used. The aims are to assess clinical skills of history-taking and physical examination, problem-solving and management; communication and counselling skills; and the candidate's knowledge. The case is carcinoma of the oesophagus presented across four sequential stations.
tasks: Station 1: Take a history and examine the patient. Establish a likely diagnosis. Assess general state of health. Ensure the patient understands the possible causes of the illness.
Station 2: Summarise the problem to the assessor. Provide a likely diagnosis. Discuss the management strategy and reasons for performing any investigations.

Station 3: Interpret the investigations provided (blood results, ECG, pulmonary function tests, echocardiogram, CT chest and abdomen, blood and coagulation studies, endoscopy findings). Describe findings to the assessor and outline a management plan.
Station 4: Counsel the patient on the results of the investigations. Counsel the patient on a proposed plan of management.
historyWithoutPrompting: Three month history of dysphagia. Some loss of weight. Past history of heartburn. Smokes 20 cigarettes a day. Consumes 20 g of alcohol a day. Three years ago suffered a myocardial infarction. Currently takes aspirin and is on medication for hypertension and obstructive lung disease. Had a knee replacement two years ago complicated by a deep vein thrombosis.
physicalExaminationFindings: Examination findings to be elicited by the candidate as part of Station 1. Relevant normal and abnormal findings to be summarised in Station 2.
aimsOfStation: Station 1: To establish a likely diagnosis; assess general state of health; ensure the patient understands the possible causes of the illness. Station 2: To summarise the problem to the assessor; provide a likely diagnosis; discuss the management strategy and reasons for performing any investigations. Station 3: To interpret investigations. Station 4: To counsel the patient on the results of the investigations and on a proposed plan of management.
expectationsOfPerformance: Station 1: Candidate takes a focused history and performs a relevant examination, communicates with the patient appropriately.

Station 2: Candidate provides a succinct and clear summary of history and relevant examination findings in a logical format. States relevant features (smoking, alcohol, heart and lung disease) and discusses how these may influence treatment. Discusses establishing the diagnosis (endoscopy/radiology), looking for tumour spread (blood tests, chest imaging, CT), assessment of cardiorespiratory state (ECG, pulmonary function tests, echocardiography), assessment of haematologic status, and assessment of anaesthetic and operative risk factors (ASA staging).
Station 3: Candidate describes investigation findings to the assessor and outlines a management plan. Notes implications of previous DVT in perioperative care.
Station 4: Candidate counsels the patient on investigation results and proposed management plan using appropriate communication skills.
keyIssues: Ability to take a focused history and perform a relevant examination in a patient with dysphagia.
Ability to synthesise clinical information and provide a likely diagnosis of oesophageal carcinoma.
Appropriate selection and interpretation of investigations including endoscopy, CT staging, cardiorespiratory and haematologic assessment.
Ability to counsel the patient on diagnosis and management in plain language.
Recognition of comorbidities (ischaemic heart disease, COPD, previous DVT) and their implications for management and operative risk.
commentary: The four-station progressive OSCE (OSLER) represents a move away from testing knowledge alone and allows assessors to focus on clinical skills and counselling abilities. Through the integration of the four stations, candidates can show their ability to synthesise a clinical problem, put the different aspects of the case into perspective, discuss the diagnosis and management, and interpret clinical, radiological and laboratory data in a suitably holistic manner. The global marking scheme allows assessors to look at the overall performance of the candidates rather than concentrate on minutiae. One disadvantage of this system is the narrow range of problems covered in the examination unless the patient has a number of ailments and multisystem problems; this could be addressed by running an eight-station OSLER based on two patient scenarios.
underlyingDiagnosis: Carcinoma of the oesophagus
differentialDiagnosis: Benign oesophageal stricture (e.g. from chronic reflux oesophagitis)

title: Hair loss in a 38-year-old man
patientProfile: 38-year-old male newsagent, married with two children. General health is excellent, does not smoke or use alcohol. Works long hours with usual stress of running a small business, no social or family problems. Worried about appearance because of contact with customers. Concerned about cause of hair loss and anxious to have treatment.
chiefComplaint: What is happening to my hair?
candidateInfo: You are a Hospital Medical Officer (HMO) working in a primary care clinic attached to a teaching hospital. This 38-year-old male newsagent has just consulted you about recent (2-3 weeks) hair loss from the scalp. One eyebrow is also affected. He is otherwise well with no significant past or family history. The patient is very concerned about possible future progression and wishes to ask you about the diagnosis and possible treatments. You have completed an examination of the scalp. The findings are as depicted in the illustrations (Figures 1 and 2).
tasks: Discuss the condition with the patient.
Advise him about treatment.
openingStatement: What is happening to my hair?
historyWithoutPrompting: Recent (2-3 weeks) hair loss from the scalp. One eyebrow is also affected. He is otherwise well with no significant past or family history. Works long hours with usual stress of running a small business but no social or family problems. Worried about appearance because of contact with customers.
historyWithPrompting: Patient becomes impatient if simple reassurance is the main advice given. No significant past or family history including baldness.
patientQuestions: Will I go completely bald?
Will it improve?
How long will it last?
What can be done about it?
Is treatment effective?
Should I see a specialist?
Could it have anything to do with my glands? OR with my thyroid gland?
physicalExaminationFindings: Discrete circular areas of hair loss on the scalp and one eyebrow, consistent with alopecia areata (as depicted in Figures 1 and 2).
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to deal with a cosmetic problem, almost certainly alopecia areata, for which treatment and prognosis are uncertain.
expectationsOfPerformance: Recognise that the patient has alopecia areata and is concerned about his appearance.
Explain the nature of the condition such that the possibility of improvement or return to normal is emphasised but the natural history is unpredictable in individual cases.
Answer the patient's questions directly with supportive explanation.
Explain that initial management is by topical medication which aims to stimulate hair regrowth; any one agent should be used for 3-6 months before changing therapy.
Discuss potent topical corticosteroid applied once or twice daily (e.g. betamethasone dipropionate 0.05%).
Discuss intralesional corticosteroid such as triamcinolone acetonide 10 mg/mL for small areas on the scalp or eyebrows — multiple injections usually required.
Discuss topical dithranol applied once daily, commencing at 0.5% increasing gradually to 2% (avoid eye contact).
Discuss topical minoxidil (5% lotion) applied twice daily in cases not otherwise responding (but has very limited effectiveness).
Oral corticosteroids may be trialled if topical treatment is ineffective, tapering dosage downwards over two months.
If the condition worsens, disguising the hair loss with a wig may be considered.
Consideration of topical immunotherapy and ultraviolet phototherapy would require referral to a dermatologist.
Some patients may require early referral for confirmation of the diagnosis and reinforcement of advice about the likely course.
Followup arrangements should be made appropriately.

Show empathy, sensitivity and perceptiveness, as well as being honest and generating trust and confidence — particularly important in a chronically relapsing condition significantly affecting the patient's appearance.
keyIssues: Effective communication skills are very important in this case. Appropriate language, verbal and nonverbal communication, and good interpersonal skills should be displayed.
