Showing empathy, sensitivity and perceptiveness, as well as being honest and generating trust and confidence.
Place of topical and systemic treatment and prognosis.
commentary: Alopecia is a generic term for hair loss. Alopecia areata is a descriptive term for one or more discrete circular areas of hair loss. These areas can occur anywhere on the body including the beard area in males. Alopecia universalis is where there is complete hair loss from the whole body and is a variant of alopecia areata. The condition is a chronically relapsing autoimmune disease with an extremely variable natural history. Patches on the scalp may regrow spontaneously, remain unaltered or enlarge and coalesce into alopecia totalis (whole scalp). In a case such as this scenario, there is approximately a 33% chance of complete regrowth within six months and a 50% chance within one year. Approximately 80% of patients, however, will eventually relapse. If the hair loss persists for years, the prospect of regrowth is diminished, although the potential for hair regrowth always remains because the hair follicle is not destroyed. The aetiology is unknown, but a family history is present in 20% of cases and there is a linkage with other organ-specific autoimmune diseases. There may be a specific trigger such as a febrile illness or severe emotional stress. Less severe day-to-day stress is not considered to be a trigger. The condition itself is stressful, especially in females.
underlyingDiagnosis: Alopecia areata

title: An unusual feeling in the throat in a 30-year-old man
patientProfile: A 30-year-old married man with two children. Smokes 10-15 cigarettes daily, drinks 2-3 alcoholic drinks only at weekends. Had a vasectomy two years ago. No serious illnesses. Previous patient of the clinic.
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. Your next patient is a 30-year-old man who is consulting you about a throat problem. He is a previous patient of the clinic, is married with two children, parents and his siblings are well. He smokes 10-15 cigarettes daily and takes 2-3 alcoholic drinks only at weekends. He had a vasectomy two years ago and has had no serious illnesses.
tasks: Take a focused history about his throat problem. The examiner will then give you the examination findings.
Discuss the most likely causes of the problem and its nature with the patient.
Discuss whether any investigations are necessary and if so, what is most likely to be found.
openingStatement: 'I keep getting a lump in my throat. It feels like a knot.'
historyWithoutPrompting: Over the last 4-6 weeks the patient notices that his throat tightens up; he has excessive saliva; he clears his throat repeatedly; a few days ago it was very bad and he vomited; it occurs mostly after his evening meal. He has become concerned since the recent vomiting episode. He has had no previous worry about his health and has no idea what the nature of his complaint is, but now wishes to have it thoroughly investigated.
historyWithPrompting: The feeling lasts 3-4 hours, usually until he goes to bed. He swallows more often when he has it. There is definitely no difficulty swallowing solids or liquids which go down easily without discomfort. When his throat tightens, his voice can 'catch', and his eyes water. His voice is otherwise unaffected. No hoarseness, no sore throat, cough or nasal/postnasal discharge. No loss of weight, appetite change, abdominal pain or regurgitation. No shortness of breath, palpitations, chest pain or dizzy spells. No aggravation by lying down. He sleeps well, has no other symptoms and does not take any medications. He believes he is in good health and never takes time off work. No past history of anxiety or other psychiatric illness. Does not feel depressed or anxious. No particular worries about everyday life, wife and children. He is conscientious and hardworking but not a worrier. He usually enjoys work but has felt annoyed in the past few months when having to work weekends. A cousin died from cancer of the larynx three months ago — this is still on his mind, as well as the thought of cancer at times.
patientQuestions: 'You don't seem to be taking my complaint seriously.' (if reassured without discussing investigations)
'Isn't there just a simple test to check my throat out?' (if multiple investigations all at once are advised)
physicalExaminationFindings: Physical examination of this patient is completely normal (provided by examiner after approximately five minutes).
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to take a satisfactory history about globus pharyngeus disorder and to display perspective in selecting appropriate investigations, and skill in counselling and educating a patient about the condition.
expectationsOfPerformance: The focused history is expected to exclude dysphagia and hoarseness, any disturbance in general health and to reveal the patient's concerns about the cause of his cousin's death.
The diagnosis of globus disorder (subject to laryngoscopy) should be made, but may not be directly stated to the patient.

Choice of investigations: Laryngoscopy and pharyngoscopy must be done. Chest X-ray with thoracic inlet views and barium swallow are acceptable. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy may be suggested for reassurance. Bronchoscopy is not indicated.

Patient counselling and education: the correct diagnosis should be explained as most likely being a nonserious condition with transient change in sensation or function of the throat; the condition is brought on by emotional factors; the condition needs limited investigations which should be explained; and investigations are unlikely to reveal any serious process.
keyIssues: Ability to take a focused history.
Ability to discuss a probable functional disorder with a concerned patient.
criticalErrors: Failure to request laryngoscopy or upper pharyngeal/oesophageal endoscopy.
Failure to indicate to the patient that serious disease is extremely unlikely.
commentary: This patient is presenting with recent onset of mild symptoms localised in the same anatomical region as his cousin's recent cause of death, the role of normal grief and worry about cancer being largely unrecognised. Globus pharyngeus disorder (globus hystericus) is a physiological symptom associated with altered mood states, often grief, but not associated with any specific psychiatric disorder or necessarily requiring psychiatric treatment. Elevated cricopharyngeal pressure or abnormal hypopharyngeal motility may exist at the time of the symptoms. The same sensation may result from gastro-oesophageal reflux or from frequent swallowing and mouth dryness. Other causes of upper oesophageal or laryngeal compression are retrosternal goitre, carcinoma of laryngopharynx, oesophageal or cricothyroid web (sideropenic dysphagia). Skeletal muscle disorders, myasthenia gravis, myotonia dystrophica, and polymyositis are other potential causes of dysphagia. None is likely in this case.
underlyingDiagnosis: Globus pharyngeus disorder (globus hystericus)

title: Pain in the testis following mumps in a 25-year-old man
patientProfile: A 25-year-old man who contracted mumps from his five-year-old son approximately three weeks ago. He has a wife and two children (aged 5 and 3 years). The couple have contemplated having another child. He was seen five days ago with painful facial swellings, fever and malaise and diagnosed with mumps.
candidateInfo: Your next patient in a general practice is a 25-year-old man who consulted you five days ago because of painful swellings on both sides of his face associated with fever and malaise. You made a confident diagnosis of mumps. You had previously diagnosed mumps in the patient's five-year-old son, a little less than three weeks beforehand. The son has now fully recovered. The patient's other child, aged three years, is well. You are aware that the couple have contemplated having another child. You advised the patient to rest at home, suggested paracetamol as an analgesic, and asked him to see you in a few days time before returning to work. The patient has come to see you today because of a relapse of his fever associated with the onset of severe pain in his left testis. You have found the left testis to be swollen to twice the size of the right one. It is very tender. The right testis feels normal. The patient has a temperature of 38.4°C. His face is slightly swollen. Apart from a tachycardia, there are no other abnormal clinical signs.
tasks: Advise the patient of the diagnosis.
Advise him about treatment and prognosis.
Answer any questions asked by the patient.
openingStatement: The patient has come to see you because of a relapse of his fever associated with the onset of severe pain in his left testis.
historyWithoutPrompting: The patient thought he was recovering from mumps, having noticed the onset of facial swelling about a week ago. He contracted the disease from his older son aged five years. He is now consulting the doctor about a painful and very tender left testicle. Analgesics (Panadol®) have had little effect. He is in quite severe pain and feels most unwell. He is anxious about possible sterility, whether the other testis will also be affected, possible impotence, his infectivity to his wife and younger son, and how long he will be away from work.
patientQuestions: 'What is the connection between the mumps and this trouble?'
'What can I take for the pain?'
'Are there any antibiotics or other drugs for this condition?'
'Will it affect both of my testicles?'
'Will we be able to have another child, if we decide to?'
'Will my sex life be affected?'
'What will happen to the testicle eventually?'
'When will I be able to go back to work?'
'Why wasn't my son affected in this way?'
'Will our younger son get mumps too?'
'Are there any other complications of mumps?'
physicalExaminationFindings: Left testis swollen to twice the size of the right, very tender. Right testis feels normal. Temperature 38.4°C. Face slightly swollen. Tachycardia present. No other abnormal clinical signs.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of mumps orchitis including natural history, management, prognosis and preventive medicine aspects, and communication skills in dealing with an unwell and anxious patient.
expectationsOfPerformance: The candidate should be confident with the diagnosis of mumps orchitis and should state this to the patient. Torsion of the testis need not be considered under the circumstances.
A sympathetic and reassuring manner is expected.
Pain control — paracetamol with 8 or 30 mg of codeine (e.g. Panadeine®, Panadeine Forte®).
Steroids can be used to relieve severe pain but have no other effect on the illness. Their use in mumps is controversial.
Advise general measures for a febrile illness — adequate fluids, light diet, and rest, until pain and swelling have subsided.
Local measures — scrotal support, application of heat.

Provide information about immunisation for mumps — live attenuated vaccine combined with rubella and measles vaccines (MMR) is available. Immunisation is advised for all children after 12 months of age, with booster dose before going to school.
The risk of infectivity to other adults is very low.
Recognising patient anxiety about possible infertility and impotence and giving appropriate reassurance.
Giving an adequate explanation of the condition including the infectivity of mumps, and its prevention by immunisation.
keyIssues: Confidence in diagnosing mumps orchitis.
Recognising patient anxiety about possible infertility and impotence and giving appropriate reassurance.
Giving an adequate explanation of the condition including the infectivity of mumps, and its prevention by immunisation.
commentary: Mumps has the following associations: The infecting organism is a paramyxovirus, spread by droplet infection or direct contact. Infectivity is 50-60%. Incubation period is 12-25 days (usually about 18). Period of infectivity is from 6 days before swelling of face occurs till 9 days after. One attack gives lifelong immunity. Complications of mumps: Common — orchitis and aseptic meningitis; Rare — encephalitis, arthritis, pancreatitis and oophoritis in females. Orchitis complicating mumps: occurs in 20-30% of postpuberal males; onset is 3-4 days after the parotitis is subsiding; usually is unilateral, subsides over one week; significant atrophy of testis occurs in 50% of cases; sterility is rare — only if bilateral involvement; sexual performance is not affected after recovery; and mumps orchitis does not predispose to testicular malignancy.
underlyingDiagnosis: Mumps orchitis
differentialDiagnosis: Torsion of the testis (excluded by clinical context)

title: Contraceptive advice for a 24-year-old woman
patientProfile: A 24-year-old woman who works as a secretary and lives alone but has a steady boyfriend. Never been pregnant, no history of gynaecologic or medical problems. In a stable monogamous sexual relationship for the past 12 months, using barrier contraception with condoms. Periods have always been irregular, occurring every 2-3 months. Not on any medications, does not smoke, only drinks alcohol occasionally. Blood pressure always normal. No history of migraine.
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. A 24-year-old woman has come to see you for advice as to the most appropriate pill she should go on for contraception for the next two to three years. She knows that various types of pills are available and wants to know how to decide which is the most appropriate pill for her.
tasks: Take a further relevant and focused history.
Ask the examiner about findings you wish to elicit on general and gynaecological examination.
Advise the patient of the appropriateness of oral contraceptive pill (OCP) therapy, which pill should be given, and how it should be administered.
openingStatement: The patient has come seeking advice on the most appropriate oral contraceptive pill for her.
historyWithoutPrompting: The patient wants to know how to decide which pill is most appropriate for her. She has been using condoms with her steady boyfriend for the past 12 months.
historyWithPrompting: No previous deep vein thrombosis, active liver disease, breast cancer or abnormal bleeding. Periods have always been irregular, occurring every 2-3 months, but are otherwise normal. Blood pressure has always been normal. No history of migraine. Not on any medications, does not smoke and only drinks alcohol occasionally.
physicalExaminationFindings: Blood pressure 120/80 mmHg. Pelvic and abdominal examination: no abnormality. Pap smear: normal six months ago. Breast examination: normal. No hirsutes.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to appropriately assess and advise a patient requesting oral contraception.
expectationsOfPerformance: Exclude absolute contraindications on history: need to exclude deep venous thrombosis, oestrogen-dependent malignancies particularly breast cancer, active liver disease or previous cholestatic jaundice, unexplained vaginal bleeding and focal migraines.
Check for relative contraindications: hypertension, cigarette smoking, diabetes, and very irregular cycles or oligomenorrhoea.

Choice of pill: Choose a low monophasic dose oestrogen pill (such as Microgynon 30®). Because her cycles are irregular (oligomenorrhoea) it is probably better to choose a triphasic preparation such as Triphasil® or Triquilar® as these have less post-pill amenorrhoea associated than a monophasic constant dose pill.
Need for followup in about three months to check blood pressure and advise if the pill needs to be changed.
Patient starts in red sector at time of next period. Contraceptive efficacy is satisfactory after seven hormone tablets have been taken.
Explain about breakthrough bleeding, missed pills and diarrhoea and the appropriate management.
Common side effects — breakthrough bleeding for first three months, sore breasts in the first 1-2 cycles.
keyIssues: Ability to take an adequate history to exclude absolute contraindications to the OCP and facts that would influence the pill chosen, and its dose.
Ability to advise a patient as to how to take the pill, the timing of its effectiveness and the likely problems during its use.
criticalErrors: Failure to exclude absolute contraindications to OCP use.
commentary: A young woman needs to be fully informed of all the benefits and side effects and risks of taking the OCP. She also needs to be carefully assessed to ensure that she has no condition making her unsuitable to take the pill. Any patient prescribed the OCP must have a full explanation of how to commence taking the pill, when it becomes effective as a contraceptive, and what to do if a pill is missed accidentally. As part of the assessment of a patient who is to be prescribed the OCP, the type of pill and its cost should be taken into account. Having excluded absolute and relative contraindications to use of the OCP (as is the case in this patient), an appropriate low dose oestrogen pill should be advised with a low breakthrough bleeding rate and low failure rate. Alternatively a triphasic preparation could be used which has less post-pill amenorrhoea. Common problems likely with candidate performance are: failing to advise patients as to when contraception will be achieved following the commencement of therapy; what to do if a pill is missed or the patient gets diarrhoea; and failing to advise the common side effects.
underlyingDiagnosis: Appropriate candidate for oral contraceptive pill

title: Thalassaemia minor in a 22-year-old woman
patientProfile: 22-year-old woman of Greek descent, recently recovered from suspected infectious mononucleosis, now engaged to be married to a man also of Greek descent
chiefComplaint: Hypochromic microcytic anaemia with elevated HbA2
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. Your next patient is a 22-year-old woman who recently had a self-limiting febrile illness, which was suspected to be infectious mononucleosis (IM). She is now fully recovered. Blood tests for IM were negative, but the full blood examination showed a hypochromic microcytic anaemia of 108 g/L. The mean corpuscular volume (MCV) was below normal (68 cubic microlitres — normal 80-101). You followed this up but there was no evidence of chronic blood loss (other than normal menstruation). Serum iron and ferritin estimations were also normal. You suspected β-Thalassaemia minor ('Mediterranean anaemia') and this has been confirmed by electrophoresis which showed an elevated HbA2 level (4.3%). You are aware of her Greek descent and that she has just become engaged to be married. Her fiancé is also of Greek descent. The family history is that her mother, father and brother are all alive and well. Her grandparents died in Greece and both were very old. One of her father's brothers was reported to have died in childhood from an unknown cause. The patient is very worried about being told she is anaemic, and as she is to be married shortly, is worried about the effects on any of the children she hopes to have. The patient has returned to discuss her results with you.
tasks: Explain the nature of the condition to the patient.
Answer the patient's questions.
Advise the patient what should be done now.
openingStatement: <UNKNOWN>
aimsOfStation: <UNKNOWN>
underlyingDiagnosis: β-Thalassaemia minor (Mediterranean anaemia)

title: Pre-pregnancy advice to a 28-year-old woman with previous thromboembolism
patientProfile: 28-year-old woman with previous DVT and pulmonary embolus in puerperium
candidateInfo: Because of her previous deep venous thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolus, she is at increased risk (probably at least 20%) of a repeat thrombosis in her next pregnancy.
tasks: Assess the patient's risk of recurrent thromboembolism in pregnancy
Advise on screening for thrombophilia prior to pregnancy
Recommend appropriate anticoagulant therapy during pregnancy and puerperium
Explain risks and benefits of heparin versus warfarin in pregnancy
Provide advice on delivery planning and management
openingStatement: Not explicitly stated in handbook
historyWithoutPrompting: Previous DVT and pulmonary embolus on day three after birth, treated with intravenous heparin for two weeks followed by warfarin for six months. Off all treatment for 12 months. Currently using condoms for contraception. No previous DVT or pulmonary embolus apart from previous pregnancy. No trauma or operations on legs or pelvis. No current shortness of breath. No varicose veins but occasional ankle oedema on right side. Previous spontaneous vaginal delivery of live male infant weighing 3550 grams with small episiotomy. Did not breastfeed. No family history of clotting problems or thromboses. Previously rubella-immune and took folic acid from before pregnancy and for first 16 weeks.
historyWithPrompting: If asked about contraception: previously on contraceptive pill prior to first pregnancy without problems. If asked about investigations: no investigations done since ceasing warfarin therapy and none done prior to commencing anticoagulants after pulmonary embolism.
patientQuestions: Will I get another deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolus?
Do I need anticoagulant treatment during the next pregnancy? If so, what?
Is there any risk from anticoagulant treatment during pregnancy to my baby or me?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to advise a patient of the appropriate management she should receive during her next pregnancy, when the last one was complicated by a pulmonary embolus during the puerperium.
expectationsOfPerformance: Pregnancy should be managed in consultation with a consultant physician or haematologist and an obstetrician
Screen for clotting propensity (thrombophilia) prior to pregnancy with tests including anticardiolipin antibody, lupus anticoagulant, protein S, protein C, anti-thrombin 3, and factor V Leiden
Advise anticoagulant therapy at least during puerperium, and safer to treat throughout pregnancy starting at about 14 weeks gestation until at least 4-6 weeks postpartum
Optimal therapy is subcutaneous low dose heparin 7500 units twice daily or low molecular weight heparin such as enoxaparin 20-40 mg 12 hourly
Avoid warfarin during pregnancy due to 5% risk of teratogenesis in first trimester and increased miscarriage rate, fetal and maternal haemorrhage, neurologic problems and stillbirth
Avoid prolonged immobilisation during pregnancy and consider compression stockings
Deliver in controlled manner at about 38-39 weeks gestation with morning heparin dose withheld
Continue folic acid therapy as in previous pregnancy
keyIssues: Ability to recognise that she is at increased risk of a recurrent thrombosis in her next pregnancy and requires at least low dose heparin during the puerperium if not for most of the antenatal period as well
Recognition of relative risks and indications for heparin and warfarin therapy
Recognition of significant risks of warfarin during pregnancy
criticalErrors: Failure to screen for an inherited or acquired coagulation disorder
Failure to advise anticoagulant therapy at least for 4-6 weeks postpartum in the next pregnancy
Advising warfarin therapy throughout pregnancy
commentary: This young woman has survived a life-threatening pulmonary embolus in a previous pregnancy. She must receive treatment to prevent a similar episode occurring in her next pregnancy. It is essential to exclude any acquired or inherited coagulation disorder. However, in any event, she will need anticoagulant therapy for the majority of her next pregnancy. The benefits of heparin must be carefully explained, as opposed to the risks of warfarin in pregnancy. It is also important that the anticoagulant therapy be continued beyond the birth of her infant for at least 4-6 weeks. Common problems likely with candidate performance are: failure to enquire about the particulars of the previous thrombosis/pulmonary embolism; suggesting that warfarin therapy should be given throughout the pregnancy, which can be teratogenic if given in the first trimester, and is impossible to reverse quickly in the late third trimester when labour is likely to occur.
underlyingDiagnosis: Previous thromboembolism in pregnancy with increased risk of recurrence

title: Pre-pregnancy advice to a 24-year-old woman with Type 1 diabetes mellitus
patientProfile: 24-year-old nulliparous woman with Type 1 diabetes mellitus diagnosed at age 9
candidateInfo: The candidate needs to advise her of the need to have very good blood sugar control prior to conception and during the pregnancy, that the pre-pregnancy counselling applicable to all pregnant women is also applicable to her, that the risk of certain pregnancy complications are increased, that the baby is also at increased risk, and that delivery at 38 weeks or sometimes earlier will probably be required.
tasks: Assess diabetic control and screen for effects of diabetes on target organs
Advise on blood glucose targets prior to conception and during pregnancy
Recommend appropriate investigations prior to pregnancy
Counsel on risks to mother and baby
Advise on folic acid supplementation
Arrange referral to diabetic physician and ophthalmologist
openingStatement: Not explicitly stated in handbook
historyWithoutPrompting: Diabetes diagnosed at age 9 (15 years ago). Well controlled on insulin, currently on long-acting insulin twice daily. Last review by diabetic physician 12 months ago. Manages own insulin dosages and tests blood sugar levels three times a day, generally keeping levels at 6-8 mmol/L. No urinary infections or vaginal infections. Vision good and kidney function normal. Never had hyperglycaemic coma but occasionally has hypoglycaemic episodes. Has not had pre-pregnancy blood tests such as rubella antibodies, FBE, blood group, indirect Coombs test or syphilis serology.
historyWithPrompting: If asked about previous investigations: no pre-pregnancy blood tests have been done.
patientQuestions: Will I be able to have a baby?
Will my baby be OK?
Will the pregnancy adversely affect my diabetes?
How does the diabetes affect my pregnancy?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to counsel a nulliparous diabetic woman about the effects of her diabetes on a future pregnancy, the effect of future pregnancies on her diabetes, and the actions she should take to minimise the risk of potential problems.
expectationsOfPerformance: Assess diabetic control and look for evidence of effects of diabetes on target organs
Measure haemoglobin A1C (HbA1c) and review blood glucose assessments to ensure satisfactory control
Keep blood glucose levels within range of 5-7 mmol/L during first trimester to reduce risk of fetal malformation
Keep blood glucose levels controlled during remainder of pregnancy to reduce fetal and maternal complications, particularly macrosomia and unexplained fetal death

Refer to diabetic physician to check general state with assessment for peripheral neuropathy, renal function (renal function tests and 24 hour urinary protein), and check of optic fundi by ophthalmologist
If all assessments normal, advise to attempt pregnancy and commence folic acid 0.5-1 mg per day from time pregnancy is attempted until at least mid-gestation
Perform routine tests prior to pregnancy including blood group and indirect Coombs test, FBE, hepatitis screening, VDRL, rubella serology, and midstream urine culture
keyIssues: Ability to counsel a nulliparous diabetic woman about effects of her diabetes on future pregnancy
Understanding effects of future pregnancies on her diabetes
Knowledge of actions to minimise risk of potential problems
commentary: The handbook slice ends mid-sentence in the expectations section and does not provide complete commentary for condition 014.
underlyingDiagnosis: Type 1 diabetes mellitus in a woman planning pregnancy

title: Suspected hearing loss in a 10-month-old infant
patientProfile: 10-month-old girl named Helena referred by local childcare nurse for suspected hearing loss
candidateInfo: The candidate is expected to take a relevant history to determine if hearing loss may be present as suspected by the child health worker despite the good sign of babbling, and to provide succinct, accurate advice to concerned parents, who have noted nothing amiss with her development or hearing.
tasks: Take relevant history to assess for hearing loss
Determine if hearing loss is present despite babbling
Provide accurate advice to concerned parents
Counsel on need for specialist assessment
Explain importance of early diagnosis
openingStatement: Not explicitly stated in handbook
historyWithoutPrompting: Parents feel baby Helena's hearing is normal. Baby is babbling (good sign). Parents have noted no abnormalities in response to sound.
historyWithPrompting: If asked about family history of deafness: none. If asked about maternal problems in pregnancy, especially infectious diseases: none. If asked about perinatal problems or health problems during or soon after birth: none. If asked about general development and if baby is growing and thriving: satisfactory, same as other children. If asked if baby responds to sounds, including loud sounds: seems to hear them and respond. If asked if baby responds when called by name: usually. If asked if baby turns towards sources of sounds: sometimes. If asked if baby responds to television: sometimes.
patientQuestions: Do you think further tests would help?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to deal with suspected hearing loss in an infant aged 10 months and ability to appropriately counsel the parent.
expectationsOfPerformance: History should cover most of the questions detailed regarding family history, maternal problems, perinatal problems, general development, and response to sounds
Explain that hearing loss is common in preschoolers — most cases are mild and transient due to conductive deafness from middle ear effusions
Explain that sensorineural hearing loss is less common but important to detect as early as possible
Explain that distraction tests are only a screening tool and do not diagnose deafness
Explain that often it is the parents who recognise that there is something wrong; but the child health nurse is also a professional and her concerns must be followed up
Explain that if there is a problem then the earlier the diagnosis the better
Refer the child to a Paediatric Audiologist as soon as possible for more formal hearing assessment by an audiogram
Review following audiogram
If normal, reassure and review hearing and early language development in about three months
If abnormal, refer to appropriate specialist for further evaluation
Exhibit understanding for the parent's concern and provide guarded reassurance and support
keyIssues: Appropriate history relevant to deafness
Counselling with reference to early definitive screening for hearing
Providing appropriate level of support and reassurance
criticalErrors: Failure to refer for specialist assessment (audiogram) for definitive diagnosis
commentary: The candidate is expected to proceed from the cue of 'possible hearing loss' to assess whether other evidence, such as failure to turn to sound, is present to support this diagnosis. In addition, enquiry regarding features suggesting associated developmental delay is appropriate. Given a working hypothesis of possible hearing loss, features in the history which could be causative, such as maternal illness in pregnancy, perinatal problems such as jaundice or drug administration, should be evaluated. However, the importance of early diagnosis and treatment of significant hearing deficit in this age group make referral for definitive diagnosis mandatory despite no other concerning features being present. Adequate communication skills in dealing with a concerned patient are essential, used in combination with appropriate knowledge-based clinical skills.
underlyingDiagnosis: Suspected hearing loss in 10-month-old infant

title: A duodenal ulcer found on endoscopy in a 65-year-old man
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: 65-year-old retired bank clerk
candidateInfo: You are a retired bank clerk. You have had dyspepsia (epigastric pain after meals) for about six weeks. Your general health has been reasonably good apart from some recent generalised aches and pains in your joints. You have been taking Nurofen® tablets (ibuprofen), which you obtained from the pharmacist, which have not given much relief. I do not smoke or drink alcohol.
tasks: Explain the aetiology of duodenal ulceration
Discuss the role of Helicobacter pylori and NSAIDs
Outline principles of management including triple therapy
Address patient concerns about contagion, treatment, recurrence, malignancy, and surgery
openingStatement: Not explicitly stated in handbook; patient presents with dyspepsia history
historyWithoutPrompting: Dyspepsia (epigastric pain after meals) for about six weeks; taking ibuprofen from pharmacist without relief; recent generalised aches and pains in joints; does not smoke or drink alcohol
historyWithPrompting: If asked: Why have I developed the ulcer? Is it contagious? Can my family members be affected? What treatment do I need? Would those Nurofen tablets have anything to do with this? Is it likely to come back once healed? Can relapse be prevented? Is the ulcer cancerous? Would surgical treatment ever be required?
patientQuestions: Why have I developed the ulcer?
Is it contagious? Can my family members be affected?
What treatment do I need to get rid of this duodenal ulcer?
Would those Nurofen tablets have anything to do with this?
Is it likely to come back once it has healed? If so, can this relapse be prevented?
Is the ulcer cancerous?
Would surgical treatment ever be required?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to counsel a patient on the aetiology and complications of duodenal peptic ulceration and the principles of management.
expectationsOfPerformance: Explain that the majority of duodenal ulcers are caused by Helicobacter pylori and gastric hydrochloric acid; NSAIDs commonly contribute to peptic ulcer formation
Describe triple therapy (proton pump inhibitor and two antibiotics) for H. pylori-related ulcers, healing more than 90% within one week and low recurrence if organism eradicated
For NSAID-related ulcers, acid suppression alone is required for healing
Recommend cessation of NSAIDs and smoking; replace ibuprofen with paracetamol
Explain that bacterial infection is not highly contagious to family members
Discuss that peptic ulcers are common, about 70% related to H. pylori; left untreated most heal spontaneously but relapse; about 10% develop complications
Explain that most patients managed with medical therapy without surgery; complications requiring surgery include perforation, gastric outflow obstruction, and severe persistent bleeding
Clarify that malignancy in chronic peptic ulcer is uncommon (1-2% in gastric ulcer); duodenal ulcers not associated with malignancy
keyIssues: Knowledge of the usual medical therapy and risk factors for patients with peptic duodenal ulceration
Understanding that surgery is required for perforation, obstruction and for patients with severe, persistent bleeding or intractable pain
criticalErrors: Informing the patient that the duodenal ulcer is likely to be malignant
commentary: With the advent of proton pump inhibitors and identification of Helicobacter pylori, management of peptic ulcer disease has undergone considerable change. From being a chronic, indolent problem frequently requiring surgical intervention, peptic ulcer disease is now a relatively benign and easily treated condition. The mucosal lining is normally protected by a layer of mucus and bicarbonate secretion modulated by prostaglandins. NSAIDs interrupt this protection by interfering with prostaglandin synthesis. H. pylori disrupts the mucous layer by producing ammonia through urease enzyme activity. Most peptic ulcers worldwide are H. pylori-related. When not related to H. pylori, NSAIDs are the most likely aetiological factor. All patients with suspected peptic ulcer should have endoscopy to confirm diagnosis and test for H. pylori; gastric ulcers must be biopsied to exclude malignancy. If H. pylori is present, eradication with treatment leads to low recurrence. If absent, likely cause is NSAID; withdraw NSAID and give acid-suppressing agent for 4-8 weeks. Success judged by symptom relief and breath test for H. pylori eradication.
underlyingDiagnosis: Duodenal ulcer, likely Helicobacter pylori-related or NSAID-related

title: Advice on autologous blood transfusion to a 55-year-old man awaiting elective surgery
subcategory: 3-A The Management Process — Counselling and Advice
patientProfile: 55-year-old man awaiting elective surgery
chiefComplaint: Seeking advice on autologous blood transfusion
candidateInfo: You are a 55-year-old man awaiting elective surgery. You wish to understand the principles of preoperative blood collection for autologous intraoperative transfusion and the advantages and disadvantages of using your own blood.
tasks: Explain the principles of preoperative blood collection for autologous transfusion
Discuss advantages of autologous over homologous blood transfusion
Address patient questions about blood storage duration, collection volume, blood regeneration, weakness, and compatibility
Counsel on circumstances in which autologous transfusion may be considered
openingStatement: Are there any advantages in using my own blood if I need a transfusion?
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient is awaiting elective surgery and is interested in autologous blood transfusion options
historyWithPrompting: If asked: How long is the blood good for? (Up to five weeks) How much do they take? (Up to 2 litres over a period of 2-5 weeks) Don't I need all my own blood? (Your blood rapidly regenerates, being a renewable tissue from the bone marrow) Won't it make me very weak? (Not significantly) What are the advantages of my blood over blood bank blood? (It is your own, which is fully compatible)
patientQuestions: Are there any advantages in using my own blood if I need a transfusion?
How long is the blood good for?
How much do they take?
Don't I need all my own blood?
Won't it make me very weak?
What are the advantages of my blood over blood bank blood?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to explain the principles of preoperative blood collection for autologous intraoperative transfusion.
expectationsOfPerformance: Explain the normal functions of bone marrow and blood, and that blood is a renewable tissue with main functions of oxygen transport and maintenance of circulatory volume

Explain that blood donors can give readily 10-15% of blood volume (450 mL) and fluid volume is rapidly replaced within hours; replacement of red cells starts immediately and slight anaemia resolves within weeks
Explain that blood can be stored safely for reuse within a few weeks (up to five weeks), allowing one, two or more donations over weeks prior to operation
Explain that blood units can be stored and used during operation as whole blood or reconstituted red cells to replace operative blood loss in operations on large joints or blood vessels
Explain that the procedure removes risk of disease transmission (particularly viruses) or incompatibilities and allergies inherent in standard homologous blood transfusion

Explain that the procedure is recommended in patients with no medical contraindications to blood donation who are to have planned major elective surgical operation on a defined date with expected moderate or high blood losses
Explain that the procedure does not increase liability of operative problems or complications; modestly lowered haemoglobin may be protective against thrombotic complications
Explain that the procedure would not necessarily remove all likelihood of requiring additional non-autologous transfusion, but will significantly reduce such prospects
Explain that non-autologous/homologous blood transfusion is used to save thousands of lives yearly and, although not entirely free of risk, is rigorously supervised and safe in Australia
Explain that preoperative autologous blood collection depends on verified and non-cancellable time for surgery; blood would be unavailable if operation was long delayed or cancelled
keyIssues: Appropriate explanation of blood being a renewable tissue, thus allowing for preoperative collection and storage
Advantages of autologous over homologous blood transfusion
Circumstances in which autologous transfusion may be considered
commentary: There are two components to the task: to provide education on the transfusion of blood and blood products, and to explain the benefits, risks and principles of autologous blood transfusion. In industrialised communities, blood transfusion is now extremely safe. Blood is usually collected from volunteer donors only. In Australia there is no remuneration. All blood samples are carefully screened for communicable diseases including HIV, hepatitis B and C, and syphilis. Concerns about safety, particularly in the 1980s about HIV transmission, stimulated interest in avoiding or reducing use of donor blood. Autologous transfusion is one such technique, reducing risk of viral infection and alloimmunisation and incompatibility reactions. Stored blood at 4°C has shelf-life of up to five weeks, at which time around 70% of red cells still survive normally. With pre-deposit autologous transfusion, patients can donate 2-5 units (450 mL) at approximately weekly intervals before elective surgery. Collection and administration is expensive; if blood is not used, it cannot be put into general donor pool and will be wasted. Autologous transfusion is only considered when the date of surgical procedure can be virtually guaranteed, patient's haemoglobin exceeds 110 g/L (135 g/L in women), and substantial blood loss is likely. Other techniques include intraoperative autotransfusion (operative blood salvage) and erythropoietin. In Australia and UK, where blood transfusion is generally perceived as safe, and where emergency surgery and operating list revisions make definite dates difficult, autologous transfusion techniques are applied only in minority of cases.
underlyingDiagnosis: Patient awaiting elective surgery considering autologous blood transfusion

title: Advice on stopping smoking to a 30-year-old man
subcategory: 3-A The Management Process — Counselling and Advice
patientProfile: 30-year-old information technology specialist, employed in State Government Department, engaged to be married
candidateInfo: You are a 30-year-old information technology specialist, employed in a State Government Department. You are engaged to be married and have attended your general practitioner as a followup to a recent episode of an upper respiratory infection associated with a cough. You have been a cigarette smoker since your mid-teens and currently smoke 20 cigarettes a day (on average). With your impending marriage, you have been considering stopping smoking.
tasks: Assess patient's motivation and readiness to stop smoking
Assess degree of nicotine dependence
Counsel on withdrawal symptoms and their timeline
Discuss treatment options including nicotine replacement therapy and bupropion
Address patient questions about alternative remedies, side effects, and behavioural support
openingStatement: I've been thinking about your advice last time about my smoking. I would like to stop. What can you do to help me?
historyWithoutPrompting: Cigarette smoker since mid-teens; currently smokes 20 cigarettes a day; considering stopping smoking due to impending marriage; recent upper respiratory infection with cough
historyWithPrompting: If asked: Is it too late for me to stop? Is the damage already done? How easy is it to stop? Do I have to stop abruptly or can I just cut down gradually? Do hypnotherapy or acupuncture or alternative remedies like herbs or vitamins work? What are the risks/benefits of nicotine patches/gum or mood tablets? Are they expensive? What are the side effects? What should I expect by way of withdrawal symptoms? Will the treatment affect my sex life? Do I need to do a course or join a quit smoking group? Is the desire to smoke inherited? Will I pass it on to my children?
patientQuestions: Is it too late for me to stop? Is the damage already done?
How easy is it to stop?
Do I have to stop abruptly or can I just cut down gradually?
Do hypnotherapy or acupuncture or alternative remedies like herbs or vitamins work?
What are the risks/benefits of nicotine patches/gum or mood tablets? Are they expensive? What are the side effects?
What should I expect by way of withdrawal symptoms?
Will the treatment affect my sex life?
Do I need to do a course or join a quit smoking group?
Is the desire to smoke inherited? Will I pass it on to my children?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of nicotine-dependence and the ability to obtain relevant information to counsel the patient appropriately and to answer his questions about the withdrawal process and treatment options.
expectationsOfPerformance: Display appropriate empathy, rapport and enthusiasm for explaining benefits and pitfalls of smoking cessation
Assess motivation to stop by asking direct questions about intentions and desire to stop (e.g., on a scale from 'not at all' to 'very much')
Recognise patient is in 'preparation' stage of behaviour change (planning to stop in near future) and is hoping to learn 'action' strategies
Assess degree of nicotine dependence by asking about number of cigarettes per day and pattern of smoking during day (smoking more in first hours of waking suggests greater dependence)

Ask appropriate questions: How many cigarettes a day? How soon after waking first cigarette? Difficulty not smoking in non-smoking areas? Is first cigarette hardest to give up? What is pattern of smoking during day? Smoke even if too ill to get out of bed? Tried to stop in past but could not?
Recognise that patient smoking 20+ cigarettes/day with first smoke within half hour of waking is likely to benefit from nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) or bupropion

Counsel on nicotine withdrawal: within 24 hours of reducing or stopping nicotine intake may experience depression or feeling unwell, insomnia, restlessness and irritability, anxiety and difficulty concentrating, drop in heart rate, increase in appetite (up to 3 kg over 12 months), cravings for sweet things, and cravings for cigarettes
Explain withdrawal symptoms peak in intensity over first four days of abstinence; most residual symptoms improve significantly within a month; hunger and weight gain may persist for a year or so
Explain that about 5% of nicotine-dependent individuals can stop smoking unaided and less than 25% succeed at first attempt; overall successful quit rate about 45% eventually
keyIssues: Counselling abilities
Awareness of principles of a tobacco quitting programme
criticalErrors: Lack of awareness of the key elements of a nicotine quitting programme
commentary: Cigarette dependence is a chronic relapsing condition which, once established, involves a long term, even lifetime struggle to achieve abstinence. The candidate should display appropriate empathy, rapport and enthusiasm for explaining the benefits and pitfalls of smoking cessation. The patient is in the 'preparation' stage of behaviour change (planning to stop in the near future) and is hoping to learn 'action' strategies that will help him succeed in overcoming his dependence on nicotine. In order to assess how dependent the patient is on nicotine, the candidate should not only ask about the number of cigarettes a day the patient smokes, but focus more on the pattern of smoking during the day (smoking more in the first hours of waking is more significant and suggests a greater degree of dependence). A 'yes' response to questions about previous failed attempts suggests that the patient will need help to stop smoking. When a patient smokes 20 or more cigarettes/day and has to have the first smoke within half an hour of waking up then the patient is likely to benefit from nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) or bupropion. Principles of a tobacco cessation programme follow.
underlyingDiagnosis: Nicotine dependence; patient motivated to quit smoking

title: Hyperactivity in a 7-year-old boy
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: Jonathan, a 7-year-old boy
candidateInfo: You are a general practitioner. Jonathan's mother has brought him to see you because she is concerned about his behaviour at home and at school. She describes him as hyperactive, inattentive, impulsive and difficult to manage. She is frustrated and at her wits' end.
tasks: Obtain a logical and focused history to exclude various possible causes for Jonathan's behaviour
Show empathy with the parent's frustration
Have a clear approach to the management plan
Outline briefly the probable diagnosis and plan of management
openingStatement: I'm at my wits' end with Jonathan. He's hyperactive, can't concentrate at school, and I can't control him at home.
historyWithoutPrompting: Jonathan has been hyperactive and difficult to manage. He is inattentive and impulsive. The mother is frustrated.
historyWithPrompting: The candidate should explore: physical or congenital lesions; behaviour secondary to neonatal problems; subsequent medical or physical illnesses including significant head injury; reaction to unfavourable home circumstances or child rearing and family environment; difficulties in the child's adaptation to school; visual or auditory problems. Question about similar behaviour in other family members, particularly father's history.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to take a comprehensive history in a child presenting with hyperactivity, to exclude organic causes, and to outline a management plan that does not immediately resort to stimulant medication.
expectationsOfPerformance: Obtaining a logical and focused history to exclude various possible causes for Jonathan's behaviour
Showing empathy with the parent's frustration
Having a clear approach to the management plan
Give a logical explanation as to the possible causes for Jonathan's presentation having excluded organic pathology and indicate that ADHD is a strong possibility
Suggest a neurodevelopmental assessment be performed with an experienced psychologist if possible
If hearing and vision have not been enquired about, suggest having hearing and vision assessed
Briefly outline the management of ADHD by behavioural modification with or without stimulant medication
Indicate willingness to review and monitor progress in a supportive manner
keyIssues: Obtaining a logical and focused history to exclude various possible causes for Jonathan's behaviour
Showing empathy with the parent's frustration
Having a clear approach to the management plan
criticalErrors: Coming to a premature conclusion of ADHD and recommending stimulant medication, without having explored the history for other causes
commentary: This presentation of childhood hyperactivity is common in both paediatric and general practice and skill is required to sort out the myriad of causes for the behaviour exhibited. This is done by a careful history exploring the child's past history, current developmental status, and family and school situation. Monitoring progress over several consultations may be required to give a positive diagnosis of ADHD. The great majority of children presenting in this manner have major family dynamics problems. However the current scenario is designed to indicate that a diagnosis of ADHD is by exclusion of all the other causes for this symptom; and that stimulant medication is not the immediate treatment in this situation, a tendency for which has developed in the general community and is often the expectation of family members who have spoken with neighbours or read the lay press. The doctor is often under great pressure to prescribe these medications at first visit and this should be resisted. There are no specific tests available that make a diagnosis of ADHD. Diagnosis is made from the pattern of behaviour, which can be confirmed by psychometric testing. While behavioural modification is recommended, parents have often already arranged this prior to the consultation, and also have often tried a variety of exclusion diets that in some cases may be helpful, but overall are not. Parents should be supported to try exclusion diets if they are keen to institute them; but the practitioner should monitor progress and make a critical appraisal as to the success or otherwise of this treatment and support the parent accordingly.
underlyingDiagnosis: ADHD (Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder) — diagnosis by exclusion
differentialDiagnosis: Subsequent medical or physical illnesses including significant head injury
Reaction to unfavourable home circumstances or child rearing and family environment
Difficulties in the child's adaptation to school

title: Tremor in a 40-year-old man
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: A 40-year-old man who has consulted a general practitioner complaining of 'the shakes'
candidateInfo: You are a general practitioner. The patient is a 40-year-old man who has consulted you complaining of the shakes. The doctor will seek details of your symptoms and will ask questions about your health status, medical and social history.
tasks: Assess the patient's tremor by developing diagnostic pathways
Obtain comprehensive history including past, family and social histories
Perform appropriate physical examination
Advise appropriate investigations
openingStatement: I've got the shakes doctor. I spill my drink sometimes.
historyWithoutPrompting: I have had the shakes since my early 20s. It happens when I get nervous about something, but it hasn't been a problem until recently (6-12 months). Sometimes my head shakes and often I spill my drink when I put a glass to my mouth. I feel much better after a couple of beers. Recently I heard something about Parkinson's disease which can cause the shakes, so I thought I should see a doctor.
historyWithPrompting: It doesn't seem to bother me when I get up in the morning and doesn't stop me going to sleep. It can go away for a few days then comes back. Seems to be when I am doing something with my hands like using a knife and fork, or writing. The newspaper shakes when I am trying to read it. Sometimes I have trouble lighting a cigarette. My right hand is the worst. I don't have any stiffness and I don't have trouble moving from one position to another, nor in walking. I can control the shakes by gripping things firmly. Hands and fingers shake if you hold your arms out in front of your body. Voice could be affected because you have noticed some shakiness in your voice, but only occasionally and of minor degree. Shakes were first noted in dominant right hand. Left hand is less affected and not until recently. Always been a tense and nervy person, stress makes the shakes worse. Like to have a 'few beers' especially at the weekends but never get 'drunk'. Deny any other symptoms affecting the central nervous system, cardiovascular system, respiratory, gastrointestinal or urinary systems, but admit to a diminished libido and difficulty in maintaining an erection if asked directly. No loss of weight. Work as a storeman. Married with two teenage children. Smoke 10-15 cigarettes daily and drink up to five 375 mL cans of full strength beer on most days. No serious illnesses or operations. Father died from lung cancer aged 58 years and also used to get the 'shakes' — was a non-drinker. Mother died of stroke aged 62 years. Her father had Parkinson disease. Brother, sister, wife and two children all keep in good health. Anxious about the cause of symptoms. Ignorant of long term harmful effects of alcohol. Be reluctant to confirm or reveal the true level of alcohol intake. Never been charged with exceeding .05, and have never had an injury or motor accident associated with alcohol use.
patientQuestions: Could my voice be affected?
physicalExaminationFindings: Hepatomegaly and any stigmata of chronic liver disease, cerebellar signs, increased muscle tone, tachycardia, cardiomegaly
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidates skill in defining a presenting symptom of tremor, making a probability diagnosis from the history and selecting with discrimination which aspects of physical examination and investigations will clarify the diagnosis.
expectationsOfPerformance: Approach to patient — establishing trust and confidence by having a non-judgmental attitude, listening to patient's concerns and being reassuring
History — comprehensive but focused, using appropriate communication skills. Diagnosis must include benign tremor and alcoholic tremor
By the process of listening (using an open-ended approach followed by direct questioning), to develop the two most likely diagnostic pathways: benign tremor and tremor associated with heavy drinking
Other differential diagnoses such as an anxiety state, Parkinsonism and thyrotoxicosis are much less likely from the history
Past, family and social histories should be sought
Knowledgeable candidates will be reassuring because of their confidence in the likelihood of a benign tremor
Physical examination should include checking for hepatomegaly and stigmata of chronic liver disease, tachycardia and cerebellar signs
Investigations — must advise liver function tests, but thyroid function tests not essential
keyIssues: Approach to patient — establishing trust and confidence by having a non-judgmental attitude, listening to patient's concerns and being reassuring
History — comprehensive but focused, using appropriate communication skills. Diagnosis must include benign tremor and alcoholic tremor
criticalErrors: Failure to indicate the most likely diagnosis is essential tremor
Failure to advise liver function tests
commentary: Essential tremor is one of the most common neurological disorders, with prevalence increasing with age. An autosomal dominant family history is present in 50-60% of patients and the genetic basis is unknown. Functional imaging reveals abnormal cerebellar activity and no histological or structural changes have been identified. Age of onset is bimodal, with the largest peak in the second decade, and a smaller peak in the fifth decade. The characteristic finding is a postural and kinetic tremor of the upper limbs which interferes with fine manual tasks. Head tremor is also present in 40%. Less commonly legs are involved or there is voice tremulousness. With advancing age, the tremor frequency often slows and amplitude increases, leading to a coarse tremor which can be disabling, although this is uncommon. Patients with benign essential tremor often drink as a means of controlling the tremor as alcohol has an ameliorating effect in 50% of cases. In this case, the patient may also be suffering from the effects of prolonged heavy drinking. This would require further assessment with the investigations recommended above.
underlyingDiagnosis: Essential tremor (benign familial tremor) with possible concurrent effects of heavy alcohol use

title: Syncope in a middle-aged man
subcategory: 2-A The Diagnostic Process — History-taking and Problem-Solving
patientProfile: Middle-aged patient with syncope
candidateInfo: <UNKNOWN>
openingStatement: <UNKNOWN>
historyWithoutPrompting: <UNKNOWN>
historyWithPrompting: <UNKNOWN>
aimsOfStation: To assess ability to diagnose syncope caused by serious underlying illness, particularly aortic stenosis
expectationsOfPerformance: Take careful history including eyewitness account
Distinguish syncope from seizures
Identify cardiac cause from history of exertion, chest pain, and shortness of breath
Perform systematic cardiovascular examination looking for structural heart disease
Examine peripheral pulse, blood pressure, carotid pulses
Assess cardiac apex beat, heart sounds, and cardiac murmur characteristics
Order ECG and echocardiogram as key investigations
Refer to cardiologist appropriately
keyIssues: Distinguish syncope from seizures and other neurological causes
Identify cardiac cause, particularly aortic stenosis
Recognize classical triad of aortic stenosis symptoms
Avoid unnecessary and costly investigations
criticalErrors: Requesting inappropriate investigations such as full blood count, electrolytes, blood glucose, carotid Doppler studies, electroencephalogram, Holter or loop monitoring, and coronary angiography without clinical justification
commentary: Syncope increases with age and can cause significant morbidity. An attack of syncope is associated with major morbidity such as fractures and motor vehicle accidents in 6% of cases, and minor injuries in about a third of cases. Causes include neurally-mediated syndromes (vasovagal/vasodepressor syncope, carotid sinus syncope), orthostatic syncope, cardiac arrhythmias, structural heart disease, and cerebrovascular disease. Taking a careful history, including eyewitness account, is critically important and can prevent inappropriate and costly investigations. This patient's history of syncope on exertion with chest pain and shortness of breath points towards a cardiac cause. The absence of palpitations is against cardiac arrhythmia but this could still be a possibility. Candidates should use appropriate questioning to exclude epilepsy and other neurological causes. Carotid sinus syncope and vasovagal/vasodepressor syncope may be considered but other features of these conditions are not apparent.
underlyingDiagnosis: Aortic stenosis

title: Assessment of a comatose patient
patientProfile: A young person found in a coma in their flat this morning, breathing without difficulty
candidateInfo: You are examining, in the Emergency Department, a comatose young person found in bed this morning, who is haemodynamically stable. The patient is feigning a partially responsive coma, with a Glasgow Coma Score of 10-11 out of the normal score of 15, breathing spontaneously, reacting by localising to pain, and with inappropriate verbal response when stimulated.
tasks: Examine for evidence of injury to the head or elsewhere
Look for evidence of neck stiffness
Examine eye movement by gently opening the lids
Examine pupillary size and response to light (direct and consensual)
Examine breathing pattern
Examine for evidence of intravenous drug use or insulin injection sites
Check for pulse rate, rhythm and character
Arrange immediate blood sugar estimation
openingStatement: You are to play the part of a young person found in a coma in your flat this morning breathing without difficulty with a Glasgow Coma Score of 10 out of 15.
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient is feigning unconsciousness and stupor on a hospital bed, wearing shorts and T-shirt. Eyes closed, not opening spontaneously or to verbal command but opening in response to painful stimulation. No response to verbal command. Localises pain when stimulated — moves arms towards source of pain or withdraws limb if stimulated. Uses inappropriate words when painful stimuli applied. Neck is stiff when flexion is attempted.
historyWithPrompting: If candidate tests corneal response, there is normal eye closure to cotton wool testing. If candidate wishes to look at fundi or ear drums, advise that they are normal. Blood pressure and temperature are normal.
physicalExaminationFindings: Eyes closed, open to pain only. Pupils reactive to light. Neck stiff on flexion. Localises to pain. Inappropriate verbal responses. Normal pulse, breathing, blood pressure and temperature. Normal fundoscopy and ear drums.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to examine and diagnose a patient presenting with coma.
expectationsOfPerformance: Examine for evidence of injury to the head or elsewhere
Look for evidence of neck stiffness
Examine eye movement by gently opening the lids
Examine pupillary size and response to light (direct and consensual)
Examine breathing pattern — no hyperventilation or hypoventilation is present
Examine for evidence of intravenous drug use or insulin injection sites
Check for pulse rate, rhythm and character, which are normal
Arrange immediate blood sugar estimation
Determine Glasgow Coma Scale level (around 10-11 out of 15)
Name at least four possible causes of the coma
Describe an initial investigation plan including brain CT/MRI, drug screen, serum electrolytes and blood glucose, and oxygen saturation
keyIssues: Ability to perform a focused, relevant and accurate examination to aid determination of the level and cause of the coma
Neck stiffness should be tested and identified
No response to verbal command but response to pain
The candidate should indicate appropriate knowledge of the Glasgow Coma Scale
Ability to provide an adequate differential diagnosis
Ability to describe an initial investigation plan
Mandatory investigations should include Brain CT or MRI, drug screen and blood sugar level
criticalErrors: Failure to determine reasonably the level of coma by the Glasgow Coma Scale score
Failure to check for neck stiffness
commentary: Coma is a state of deep unconsciousness where the patient shows no meaningful response to external stimuli. The comatose patient has no verbal response, does not obey commands and does not open the eyes spontaneously or in response to command. Stupor is also a state of inaccessible consciousness without awareness, but the stuporous patient shows some response to painful stimuli. Coma and stupor, and other levels of deep unconsciousness, are best graded on the Glasgow Coma Scale. This has three elements: eye opening, and best verbal and motor responses to standard stimuli. A score of 15 represents a fully responsive and conscious patient. A score of 3, the lowest level, a deeply comatose patient unresponsive to external stimuli.
underlyingDiagnosis: Coma with Glasgow Coma Score of 10-11 (possible causes include drug overdose, meningitis, cerebral vascular accident, diabetic hypoglycaemia or hyperglycaemia, head injury, or psychiatric problem)

title: Recent onset of poor distance vision in a 17-year-old male
patientProfile: A 17-year-old apprentice complaining of poor distance vision of recent onset
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. Your next patient is a 17-year-old apprentice who is complaining of poor distance vision of recent onset. He can no longer read notices, street signs, scoreboards etc. at a distance. He says this is most inconvenient and is gradually getting worse. Both eyes are affected. He has asked you if he may be short-sighted like his father and his older brother. He wants to be tested to check for short-sightedness or any other problems, to ask whether he will need glasses or contact lenses, whether surgery can help and whether he should see an optician or an eye specialist doctor.
tasks: Examine the patient's eyes to exclude serious eye disease
Test the patient's visual acuity using the Snellen test chart provided and state your findings to the patient
Explain the problem to the patient
openingStatement: He can no longer read notices, street signs, scoreboards etc. at a distance. He says this is most inconvenient and is gradually getting worse. Both eyes are affected.
historyWithoutPrompting: Poor distance vision of recent onset. Cannot read notices, street signs, scoreboards at a distance. Gradually getting worse. Both eyes affected. Family history of myopia (father and older brother).
historyWithPrompting: You do not need to take any further history.
patientQuestions: May I be short-sighted like my father and older brother?
Will I need glasses or contact lenses?
Can surgery help?
Should I see an optician or an eye specialist doctor?
physicalExaminationFindings: Patient has mild myopia. Visual acuity is worse than 6/6 but improves with pinhole test, indicating refractive error. Fundoscopic examination is normal. Corneal response is normal. Ear drums are normal.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of myopia, and ability to test visual acuity and distance vision using a Snellen test chart.
expectationsOfPerformance: Examine eyelids (ptosis, retraction of upper or lower lids)
Examine conjunctiva (chemosis, injection, pallor)
Examine cornea (ulceration)
Examine anterior chamber (blood or pus)
Examine sclera (jaundice)
Examine orbit (tenderness, paraesthesia)
Examine eyeball (intraocular pressure, glaucoma)
Use ophthalmoscope to test red reflex (to exclude cataract)
Use ophthalmoscope to examine retina (detachment, exudates, haemorrhage, new vessel formation)
Use ophthalmoscope to examine optic disc (bulging, blurring of margins)
Use ophthalmoscope to examine macula (exudates)
Perform pinhole test for both eyes
Test visual acuity using Snellen chart at 6 metres distance
Examine each eye separately
State findings in conventional way (normal vision being 6/6)
Explain nature of myopia
Discuss management options including glasses, contact lenses, and surgical correction
Advise on need for periodic check of intraocular tension when over 40 years of age
keyIssues: Exclusion of serious eye disease with ophthalmoscope and pinhole test
Correct use of the Snellen test chart
Accuracy of examination (compare with examiner's findings)
Diagnosis — must state myopia, or short-sightedness or near-sightedness
Patient counselling/education — cause, treatment, need for periodic check of intraocular tension when over 40 years of age
criticalErrors: Failure to exclude serious eye disease
Failure to mention myopia as a possible diagnosis
commentary: A comprehensive history and careful physical examination will provide a diagnosis in most common ophthalmic disorders. Although unlikely in this case, the possibility of the patient's complaint of recent impaired vision being due to a serious cause (namely retinal detachment, glaucoma, cataract or macular degeneration) should be considered by the candidate. Ophthalmoscopic examination and the use of the pinhole test cover these concerns at this stage of assessment of vision. Myopia (near-sightedness or short-sightedness) is a common inherited condition which in most cases is due to the axis of the eyeball being too long so that the visual image is focused in front of the retina. Less often the refractive power of the lens is too strong. The condition is readily corrected by the use of a concave (minus) lens. Onset can be in childhood, but more commonly in late teens. The condition tends to worsen in early adult life and then stabilises. The corrective lens can be prescribed by an optician, although an initial assessment by an ophthalmologist is preferable to exclude any other cause of visual impairment, especially retinal detachment and macular degeneration, both more common if myopia is severe. Contact lenses can be worn to correct myopia, without the risks of surgical correction. Corrective operations (excimer laser surgery) can produce excellent results by altering corneal curvature and thus the refractive power of the eye. This procedure is not without significant risk. Otherwise glasses will need to be worn. Reading is not affected much until middle age. Myopia can affect the accurate measurement of intraocular pressure, and therefore intraocular pressure should be checked periodically to detect chronic open angle glaucoma which is asymptomatic in the early stages.
underlyingDiagnosis: Myopia (near-sightedness or short-sightedness)

title: A painful rash on the trunk of a 45-year-old child-care worker
patientProfile: 45-year-old child-care worker
candidateInfo: You are a child-care worker in a kindergarten. You are single and live by yourself.
tasks: Take history of the rash and associated symptoms
Diagnose herpes zoster
Assess for underlying causes given weight loss and tiredness
Initiate appropriate antiviral treatment
Provide patient education on infectivity and occupational implications
Arrange further investigations for weight loss and tiredness
openingStatement: I've had a pain in my lower chest and now there is this rash.
historyWithoutPrompting: You have been feeling a bit unwell for a few days. You have had a burning pain over your lower chest and flank for a few days. You noticed today that you have developed a blistery rash that runs in a line around your chest and abdomen in the area where the pain started.
historyWithPrompting: In addition, you have lost 6 kg in weight over the past few months and have been feeling more tired than usual (you have nothing to add to this general statement. You have noted no disturbance to any body system function). You have had no serious past illnesses, there is no relevant family history. You have no allergies and are on no medications. You have been considering having a checkup but have no other symptoms, and you had not considered that there might be something seriously wrong until the pain and this rash appeared.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's approach to a patient with a dermatomal rash from herpes zoster plus weight loss and tiredness, which could be incidental but may be associated with underlying malignancy. These symptoms need to be further assessed.
expectationsOfPerformance: History: Typical history and rash of herpes zoster with prodromal preherpetic neuralgia
Diagnosis: Must make diagnosis of herpes zoster. Must show concern over recent weight loss and tiredness
Initial management: Treat the rash with symptomatic measures such as calamine or cold compresses and a drying lotion
Use analgesics with or without codeine
Treat with antiviral medications if patient presents within first 72 hours of the rash — aciclovir, famciclovir or valaciclovir
Monitor for the development of postherpetic neuralgia which may require further management
Examine patient and perform investigations for any possible precipitating cause. In this case, the weight loss and tiredness demand further investigation
Patient education and counselling: Explain the cause to the patient (i.e. relationship of herpes zoster to varicella [chicken pox])
Explain that the condition is only mildly contagious, but that chickenpox can be acquired by those persons in close contact with the patient who have not previously had chicken pox

Appropriate infection control measures need to be taken including management of occupational and community contacts (for example, she is a child-care worker in a kindergarten and young children and babies should not be exposed to varicella zoster virus)
keyIssues: History-taking must elicit weight loss and tiredness
Diagnose herpes zoster/shingles
Management must consider use of aciclovir or other related antiviral drugs
Must advise further assessment regarding weight loss and tiredness and discuss implications of infectivity
criticalErrors: Failure to diagnose herpes zoster
Failure to consider the possibility of an additional underlying cause in this patient
Failure to assess implications for contacts in community and work settings
commentary: Herpes zoster (shingles) is caused by reactivation of varicella zoster virus (VZV) acquired originally through primary infection with chicken pox. The condition is more common in people over 50 years of age. The virus is found in the dorsal root ganglion. In most cases the reason for reactivation is unknown, although occasionally this can be related to an underlying malignancy such as a lymphoma, leukaemia or immunosuppression including HIV infection. Occasionally patients may get rare complications including meningoencephalitis. Post-herpetic neuralgia is an important sequel. The incidence of post-herpetic neuralgia increases with age, affecting around 30-50% of adults aged 70-79 years.
underlyingDiagnosis: Herpes zoster (shingles) with possible underlying malignancy

title: Acute low back pain and sciatica in a 30-year-old man
patientProfile: 30-year-old self-employed landscape gardener
chiefComplaint: Acute low back pain and sciatica
candidateInfo: You are a self-employed landscape gardener aged 30 years. You have consulted this doctor because of the sudden onset of severe disabling pain in your lower back yesterday which moved down your left thigh and leg into your foot. It came on when you lifted a heavy rock and you have not been able to work. You could not sleep last night despite taking Panadeine® tablets. It hurts to move and to cough. You usually keep in excellent health with no serious medical problems in the past.
tasks: Take history of the injury and pain
Examine the patient systematically
Diagnose the likely cause of sciatica
Explain the anatomy and cause to the patient
Provide immediate pain relief and management
Advise on rest, activity and return to work
Discuss indications for further investigation
Provide advice on prevention of recurrence
openingStatement: You are a self-employed landscape gardener aged 30 years. You have consulted this doctor because of the sudden onset of severe disabling pain in your lower back yesterday which moved down your left thigh and leg into your foot.
historyWithoutPrompting: It came on when you lifted a heavy rock and you have not been able to work. You could not sleep last night despite taking Panadeine® tablets. It hurts to move and to cough. You usually keep in excellent health with no serious medical problems in the past.
historyWithPrompting: Show concern about how you are going to be able to work now and in the future. Appear to be in severe pain - sit uncomfortably be restless. State dissatisfaction with level of pain relief — you could not sleep last night. Expect the doctor to 'do something' to get rid of the pain. Resist advice (irrationally) not to go to work even in a supervisory capacity because of important jobs needing to be finished. Become compliant if the doctor explains the situation and gives appropriate advice.
patientQuestions: What has happened to my back?
How does this happen?
How long will I be away from work?
Should I see a chiropractor?
Will I be able to lift heavy objects in the future?
Will I always have a bad back?
Do I need to see a Specialist?
Can't I have an operation to fix it and relieve the pain?
What else can I take for the pain?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to diagnose and treat the problem of acute exertion-related low back pain and sciatica.
expectationsOfPerformance: Diagnosis and explanation of condition: Anatomy of lumbar spine (this is an L5/S1 level problem, involving the S1 nerve root)
Causes of pain particularly disc prolapse with nerve impingement/irritation (radiculopathy)
Expected course both short and long term — most resolve completely
A diagram would assist
Immediate management: Adequate rest is essential (3-4 days rest at home, but up and about as tolerated)
Pain-relieving medication — Panadeine®, Panadeine forte® or similar (including NSAID)
Subsequent physiotherapy and back-strengthening exercises
Indications for further investigation — lack of, slow, or incomplete resolution. Then needs CT or MRI
Avoidance of manipulation
Gentle traction may have a place in treatment if progress slow — would be advised after specialist referral
Emphasis on positive approach. Prognosis for recovery within a few weeks is good despite ankle jerk being affected

Need for investigation — this is particularly important if there is no improvement, or there is continuing evidence of neurologic or muscle weakness (CT acceptable, MRI preferred, plain X-ray gives limited information only)
Physiotherapy — stretching and arching active mobilising exercises appropriate once initial symptoms ease
Orthopaedic, neurological or rheumatologic consultation — will be required for lack of resolution
