Back education including advice regarding bending and lifting, and the value of walking, swimming
Reassessment in short term (2-3 days). This is essential
keyIssues: Ability to determine the likely cause of the sciatica and to explain the cause to the patient
Adequate knowledge of the management of a patient with acute sciatica including what further investigations or referral are required and when these should be done
Ability to advise the patient about work practice modifications required to prevent a recurrence of the problem
Ability to advise early rest and short term review
criticalErrors: Failure to make correct diagnosis of a likely disc lesion
commentary: Low back pain is a very common problem in Australian society. The incidence increases with age and is more common in manual workers than sedentary workers. A major problem in spinal assessment is the fact that there is often a poor correlation between clinical presentation (the patient's history and examination finding) and the imaging findings. Imaging abnormalities will be found with increasing frequency in individuals with or without accompanying symptoms from their third decade onwards. Back pain, acute and chronic, is thus one of the most common of all conditions encountered but precise pathology is very frequently lacking. The condition and its preferred treatment remain controversial. This case scenario has been chosen to exemplify the classical syndrome of nerve impingement radiculopathy, the most likely diagnosis being compression from an intervertebral disc prolapse between L5 and S1.
underlyingDiagnosis: Acute exertion-related low back pain with sciatica due to intervertebral disc prolapse at L5/S1 with nerve root compression
differentialDiagnosis: Intervertebral disc prolapse with nerve impingement
Mechanical low back pain from musculoligamentous strain

title: Fever and a recent rash in a 30-year-old man
patientProfile: 30-year-old man
candidateInfo: You have been feeling unwell for two days. You have a fever and a sore throat. You have also developed a rash over the past one to two days. The rash is all over your body, and is especially apparent on the face and trunk.
tasks: Take detailed history including sexual history
Establish differential diagnosis
Perform appropriate physical examination
Order appropriate investigations including HIV serology
Discuss informed consent for HIV testing
Provide appropriate counselling and support
Arrange specialist referral if HIV infection confirmed
openingStatement: I think I may have developed an infection.
historyWithoutPrompting: You have been feeling unwell for two days. You have a fever and a sore throat. You have also developed a rash over the past one to two days. The rash is all over your body, and is especially apparent on the face and trunk.
historyWithPrompting: The rash is not itchy. You have 'aches and pains' throughout your body in the legs, arms and back. You have a headache and bright lights hurt your eyes. You have previously considered your health to be good. You have had no serious past illnesses or family history of relevance. You have no allergies and take no medications. No history of mental illness, no history of blood transfusion. You do not use injectable drugs. You have been in a sexual relationship with another man for two years and have been having anal sex without condoms for a few months now. You have also had a number of casual sexual relationships in the past six months. You have never had an HIV test in the past.
physicalExaminationFindings: Splenomegaly and lymphadenopathy
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's diagnostic approach to a young man presenting with fever and rash of 48 hours duration with signs of splenomegaly and lymphadenopathy.
expectationsOfPerformance: Approach to patient: the history should be taken and diagnostic possibilities discussed in a matter of fact and nonjudgmental way. Support for the patient should be shown when the possible seriousness of his condition is discussed
History: must obtain detailed sexual history

Explanation to patient: his condition is possibly due to one of a number of viral infections, such as infectious mononucleosis. However, the most likely infection is with human immune deficiency virus (HIV) and this must be confirmed or excluded by laboratory investigations. These must include HIV serology
Informed consent is required for HIV testing: ensure patient has pretest counselling
Laboratory tests may not be clear during time of acute seroconversion illness and may require consultation with HIV laboratory and/or specialist unit to manage his condition
If HIV infection is confirmed, referral to a specialist infectious diseases unit is required for management during seroconversion illness
Other tests are indicated and the candidate may mention other viral causes of this patient's fever, rash, sore throat, lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly

Differential diagnosis apart from HIV: Epstein-Barr virus infection; secondary syphilis; toxoplasmosis; rubella; cytomegalovirus (CMV); herpes simplex infection; disseminated gonococcal infection; hepatitis A, B, C, D or E; and other viral infections

Investigations — these are related to the differential diagnosis and should include: full blood examination and Epstein-Barr serology; tests for rubella, CMV infection and toxoplasmosis; Venereal Disease Research Laboratories/syphilis serology; liver function tests; and tests for hepatitis A, B, C, D or E
keyIssues: History must include sexual history
Investigations must include HIV serology
Differential diagnosis must include HIV infection
Approach to patient must discuss informed consent for HIV testing
criticalErrors: Failure to consider HIV infection as a likely cause of this patient's presentation
Failure to discuss informed consent for HIV testing
commentary: Diagnosis of HIV infection requires a careful history to identify potential high-risk behaviour and recognition of the constellation of clinical symptoms and signs. The rash of acute HIV infection is usually an erythematous, maculopapular rash. From 40-90% of patients who have acquired HIV infection will develop an acute febrile illness within the first six weeks of infection, often sooner. Common symptoms include: fever, night sweats, malaise, myalgia, arthralgia, headache, photophobia and sore throat. Neurological manifestations including headache and photophobia are common as well as transient neurological signs including peripheral neuritis and other central nervous system manifestations. These symptoms usually last for less than two weeks. Other nonspecific viral sequelae such as mucosal ulceration, desquamation and herpes simplex may also occur. Acute symptoms are self-limiting. The condition resembles infectious mononucleosis but is seronegative for infectious mononucleosis. Chronic lethargy, depression and irritability may persist after initial illness. Key to diagnosis in this patient is checking for recent risk exposure history of unprotected oral or anal sex, reuse of contaminated needles or other exposure, such as occupational exposure.
underlyingDiagnosis: Acute HIV infection (seroconversion illness)
differentialDiagnosis: Hepatitis A, B, C, D or E

title: A heart murmur in a 4-year-old boy
patientProfile: 4-year-old boy with a heart murmur
chiefComplaint: What is the matter with my child?
candidateInfo: This is almost certainly an innocent murmur. No concerning symptoms or signs are present which might suggest an alternative diagnosis. Parents need reassurance that the child is normal and that normal physical activity is allowed. Referral to a paediatrician/paediatric cardiologist is only indicated if parents wish it, or seem unconvinced. The consultant would consider echocardiography. It would be reasonable to do a chest X-ray and ECG, depending on degree of parental concern. This is unlikely to show any abnormality and may be reassuring, and may then render unnecessary further referral to a cardiologist.
tasks: Assess the features of an innocent heart murmur
Avoid unnecessary extensive investigation
Provide reassurance to parents
Determine if referral is necessary
openingStatement: What is the matter with my child?
historyWithoutPrompting: Parent is puzzled and concerned at being told that the child may have something the matter with his heart.
historyWithPrompting: Parent is prepared to accept reassurance if the explanation is adequate. If not, will insist on referral and ask what tests might be performed.
physicalExaminationFindings: Soft midsystolic murmur, which is an almost musical high-pitched murmur at the base of the heart with no radiation; the murmur varies with posture and respiration, and has no associated thrill; the murmur has no diastolic component.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to diagnose an innocent heart murmur in a young child and to advise a concerned parent.
expectationsOfPerformance: Ability to assess confidently the features of an innocent heart murmur
Avoidance of unnecessary extensive investigation
Reassurance that the child is normal and normal physical activity is allowed
Referral only if parents wish it or seem unconvinced
keyIssues: Ability to assess confidently the features of an innocent heart murmur
Avoidance of unnecessary extensive investigation
commentary: Cardiac murmurs in young children are very common. It is estimated that careful auscultation under ideal circumstances will detect an innocent soft murmur in over 50% of normal four-year-olds. Hence medical facilities would be overwhelmed if all of these murmurs were referred for specialist assessment. Primary care physicians should be confident in distinguishing innocent functional murmurs from those that are associated with an organic heart lesion. The child with an innocent murmur is well and thriving, has a normal exercise tolerance, is not cyanosed, and does not suffer from recurrent chest infections. If all features indicate an innocent heart murmur, no investigations are warranted. The parent should be reassured of the innocent nature of the murmur and that the practitioner will continue to observe the child until the murmur spontaneously disappears, usually between the ages of five and seven years.
underlyingDiagnosis: Innocent heart murmur

title: A knife wound to the wrist of a 25-year-old man
patientProfile: 25-year-old man with a knife wound to the left wrist
candidateInfo: The knife wound is across the wrist just above the crease line. The bleeding has been stopped by the dressing. The candidate should proceed to examination to ascertain the extent of injury describing findings to the examiner. The patient has presented to the Emergency Department with a knife wound to the left wrist produced by an assailant after an argument in a pub. The patient lifted his arm to protect his face and the assailant slashed the wrist with a knife. It bled a lot at first, but friends reduced this by local pressure and ambulance staff put on a dressing which controlled the bleeding, but which made the hand feel numb. The patient is unable to move fingers freely.
tasks: Diagnose tendon and nerve injuries in a deep wound
Conduct a logical and systematic examination to detect nerve, tendon and vascular injury
Identify the combination of median and ulnar nerve injury
Identify severed flexor tendons
openingStatement: The bleeding has been stopped by the dressing. Proceed to your examination to ascertain the extent of injury describing your findings to me, the examiner.
historyWithoutPrompting: Knife wound to left wrist from an assailant. Lifted arm to protect face. Bled a lot at first, reduced by local pressure. Dressing controlled bleeding but made hand feel numb. Unable to move fingers freely.
historyWithPrompting: Loss of sensation to touch and pin over the whole palmar aspect of hand, fingers and thumb, extending onto the back of the fingers and thumb over the nails and end of joint. Unable to flex fingers and thumb at the end two joints of thumb and unable to bend any of the three joints of four fingers. Unable to flex wrist but able to extend it. Cannot flex fingers and thumbs inwards towards the palm. Cannot move thumb across palm towards base of ring and little fingers. Cannot abduct thumb by lifting away from palm. Can only move thumb outwards and away from other fingers in plane of palm. Cannot hold card between fingers or move little finger away from others.
patientQuestions: Why is he unable to hold a card between his fingers?
Why is he unable to flex the end joints of fingers or thumb?
physicalExaminationFindings: Loss of sensation to touch and pin over whole palmar aspect of hand, fingers and thumb, extending onto back of fingers and thumb. Inability to flex fingers and thumb at end two joints of thumb and inability to bend any of three joints of four fingers. Inability to flex wrist but ability to extend it. Inability to flex fingers and thumbs inwards towards palm. Inability to move thumb across palm. Inability to abduct thumb. Inability to hold card between fingers or move little finger away from others.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to diagnose tendon and nerve injuries in a deep wound
expectationsOfPerformance: Diagnose accurately the deep and extensive injury to the median nerve
Diagnose accurately the deep and extensive injury to the ulnar nerve

Identify all severed flexor tendons: wrist flexors (flexor carpi radialis, ulnaris, palmaris longus), finger flexors (flexor digitorum superficialis to all four fingers, flexor digitorum profundus to all four fingers), thumb flexor (flexor pollicis longus)
Identify arterial injury (probably one or both, but not disturbing viability of hand)
Identify neurologic effects: paralysis of thenar and hypothenar small muscles
Conduct a logical and systematic examination to detect nerve, tendon and vascular injury
Recognize that failure to flex distal joints of fingers and thumb is due to concomitant tendon injury, not nerve damage
keyIssues: Ability to correctly identify the structures damaged by an appropriately focused examination
criticalErrors: Failure to identify the combination of nerve and tendon injury
commentary: Cuts to the wrist and hands from knives, glass breakages and other sharp items need careful evaluation to identify damage to important underlying structures, particularly major blood vessels, nerves and tendons. The median and ulnar nerves run to the hand on the volar aspect of forearm and wrist, carrying motor fibres to the intrinsic short muscles of the hand, sensory fibres to the vital grasping surfaces of thumb and fingers, and autonomic sympathetic fibres. Testing for damage to these nerves is usually easy and rapid with a cooperative patient. The pulp of the index finger is virtually always supplied by the median nerve. The pulp of the little finger is supplied by the ulnar nerve. For median nerve, abductor pollicis brevis is virtually always supplied by the median nerve just after entering the palm. For ulnar nerve, test whether the patient can abduct the little finger away from other fingers and whether the patient can hold a piece of paper between outstretched fingers. Long flexor tendons lie in three layers from superficial to deep: wrist flexors, superficial and deep long flexor tendons to thumb and fingers. The final diagnosis is severe deep knife wound of wrist severing all volar long flexor tendons to wrist and hand and severing median and ulnar nerves — a very severe injury requiring early reconstructive surgery.
underlyingDiagnosis: Severe deep knife wound of wrist severing all volar long flexor tendons to wrist and hand and severing median and ulnar nerves

title: Multiple skin lesions in a Queensland family
patientProfile: Farmer from Queensland concerned about six members of his family with various skin lesions
chiefComplaint: Multiple skin lesions in family members
candidateInfo: A farmer is very concerned about six members of the family and has photographs of each of the lesions. He has come in from his farm, which is a long way from the town. The photographs demonstrate: (1) His son has a lesion suspicious of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the lip. (2) His 50-year-old brother has a lesion suspicious of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) of the neck. (3) His father has a seborrhoeic keratosis on his face, which is benign. (4) His wife has a lesion suspicious of malignant melanoma of the leg. (5) His 52-year-old brother has a benign spider naevus of the chest. (6) His daughter has a lesion suggestive of a benign melanocytic dermal naevus of the face.
tasks: Diagnose a variety of common benign and suspicious skin lesions
Advise on management of each lesion
Distinguish benign lesions from malignant lesions
Prioritize treatment urgency
openingStatement: The farmer presents with photographs of skin lesions from six family members
historyWithoutPrompting: Farmer is concerned about six family members with various skin lesions. Has photographs of each lesion.
historyWithPrompting: A careful history of how long the lesions had been present would normally be required; this scenario focuses on pattern recognition from physical appearance.
physicalExaminationFindings: Figure 1: Son's lesion suspicious of squamous cell carcinoma of the lip. Figure 2: 50-year-old brother's lesion suspicious of basal cell carcinoma of the neck. Figure 3: Father's seborrhoeic keratosis on face (benign). Figure 4: Wife's lesion suspicious of malignant melanoma of the leg. Figure 5: 52-year-old brother's benign spider naevus of the chest. Figure 6: Daughter's lesion suggestive of benign melanocytic dermal naevus of the face.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to diagnose a variety of common benign and suspicious skin lesions and to advise on management.
expectationsOfPerformance: Indicate which lesions are likely to be benign (seborrhoeic keratosis, melanocytic dermal naevus and spider naevus) and do not have to be excised
Indicate which lesions are suspicious of malignancy (SCC, BCC and melanoma) and should be excised
Recognize that the wife's malignant melanoma requires the most urgent treatment
Advise that excision of melanoma should not be delayed
Advise that SCC and BCC should also be removed without excessive delay
Understand that BCC only spreads directly but local infiltration may be extensive
Understand that SCC spreads directly and mainly by lymphatics, and occasionally by blood spread
Understand that malignant melanoma spreads locally, by lymphatics and by blood spread with widespread metastases possible even from small lesion
Understand that prognosis of melanoma is favourable if depth is less than 0.75 mm
keyIssues: The candidate should indicate which lesions are likely to be benign (seborrhoeic keratosis, melanocytic dermal naevus and spider naevus) and do not have to be excised
The candidate should indicate which lesions are suspicious of malignancy (SCC, BCC and melanoma) and should be excised
criticalErrors: Failure to suspect that the wife's lesion is a malignant melanoma, and that surgical excision should occur without delay
commentary: This scenario illustrates six of the most common focal cutaneous lesions seen in the Australian population (benign melanocytic naevus, seborrhoeic keratosis, spider naevus, basal cell cancer, squamous cell cancer, malignant melanoma). Clearly benign, longstanding lesions are the most commonly seen pigmented skin 'moles'; and most can be confidently diagnosed. Benign melanocytic naevi are classified according to the site of the benign pigment-containing melanocytes. Seborrhoeic keratoses arise from the epidermis as the result of proliferation of keratinocytes and are often multiple with no dermal involvement. Spider naevi are small lesions with a red central spot surrounded by flaring telangiectases. Basal cell carcinomas are the most common type of skin cancer and are almost totally confined to fair skinned people. They are slow-growing and may take years to get to sufficient size to bother the patient. Squamous cell carcinomas are the second most common cancer of the skin and arise from the keratinocytes of the epidermis. Malignant melanomas are the most malignant of all skin tumours, more common in exposed skin, occurring throughout adult life, particularly prevalent in fair-skinned populations of tropical climates. Any brown or black mole showing an increase in size, irritation, bleeding, nodularity or ulceration should be regarded as suspect and should be excised with an adequate margin.
underlyingDiagnosis: Multiple skin lesions: benign (seborrhoeic keratosis, melanocytic dermal naevus, spider naevus) and malignant (squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, malignant melanoma)

title: Subcutaneous swelling for assessment
patientProfile: Patient with a longstanding subcutaneous swelling
candidateInfo: The case scenario is a real patient with a longstanding subcutaneous swelling — the findings and diagnosis are to be checked by the examiner personally prior to commencement of the examination. The lump will usually be a lipoma, sebaceous cyst, ganglion or bursa, or occasionally a less common diagnosis. Real patients should just answer questions as asked and will expect to be reassured about conservative treatment being offered, or note advice about possible surgery.
tasks: Perform an appropriately focused diagnostic examination of the subcutaneous lump
Establish the lump's physical characteristics — particularly contour and consistency
Determine which tissue layer the lump lies in (subcutaneous fat, attached to or beneath deep fascia)
Assess attachments to skin and deeper structures
Test for transillumination and fluctuation
Assess vascular characteristics
Make a diagnosis and counsel the patient
openingStatement: What is this lump?
historyWithoutPrompting: Patient presents with a longstanding subcutaneous swelling
physicalExaminationFindings: Findings vary depending on diagnosis. Lipomas are soft, painless, mobile swellings. Sebaceous cysts move with skin, are round, fluctuant and non-transilluminable. Ganglia are deeply placed around joints or tendon sheaths. Bursae are cystic sacs between skin and bony prominences.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to perform an appropriately focused diagnostic examination of a subcutaneous lump.
expectationsOfPerformance: Establish the lump's physical characteristics — particularly contour and consistency
Layer the lump — determine if it is in subcutaneous fat, attached to or beneath deep fascia, and if arising from muscle, tendon, ligament, bone, nerve or blood vessels
Assess pulsation and whether intrinsic or transmitted
Determine attachments — superficial to skin and deep effects of muscle or tendon contraction
Test for fluid-filled cystic lump using transillumination with proper technique
Test for fluctuation using proper technique with two fingers at perimeter and central compression
Assess vascular characteristics including pulsation, venous impulse, and bruits
Assess for emptying and refilling with compression or joint movements
Determine if lump is attached to skin
Make appropriate diagnosis and counsel patient about treatment options
keyIssues: Perform an appropriately focused and accurate physical examination of the subcutaneous lump
Determine which tissue layer the lump lies in, its physical shape, size, contour and consistency, and its relationship to adjacent anatomical structures
Display appropriate reasoning skills in making the correct diagnosis
Recognise differences between lipomas, sebaceous cysts, ganglia and bursae
Counsel the patient briefly but appropriately about the lump
criticalErrors: Very unsatisfactory examination technique
Major errors in accuracy of findings
commentary: The most common subcutaneous swelling is a lipoma. Sebaceous (epidermoid) cysts, bursae and ganglia are also common. Subcutaneous lipomas are slow growing, soft, painless, tabulated and mobile swellings beneath the skin. Epidermoid cysts are lined with squamous epithelium and full of desquamated debris with characteristic soft cheesy texture and offensive odour when infected. Sebaceous cysts move with and not separate from the skin, are usually round, fluctuant and non-transilluminable with smooth nonlobulated contour. Dermoid cysts are congenital lesions found on face, neck or scalp, thin-walled and contain fatty material and occasionally hair. Ganglia present as deeply placed subcutaneous lumps around joints or tendon sheaths. Bursae are cystic sacs between skin and underlying bony prominences or separate and aid gliding of adjacent tendons and ligaments. Many or most of these lumps require no active treatment other than reassurance.
underlyingDiagnosis: Varies — lipoma, sebaceous cyst, ganglion, bursa, or occasionally less common diagnosis

title: Examination of the knee of a patient with recurrent painful swelling after injury
patientProfile: Patient with history of twisting right knee six months ago
chiefComplaint: What is wrong with my knee?
candidateInfo: You have a history of twisting your right knee six months ago when you caught your foot on a piece of broken pavement. You fell on the right knee which became swollen and painful on the inner side of the knee. The swelling caused a painful limp for a few days then subsided. Since then you have had intermittent attacks of pain felt on the inner side of the right knee with swelling which settles within 24 hours. You have difficulty in straightening your right leg fully and occasionally have apprehension twisting to the right. You are otherwise well and between attacks can walk normally with only a minor feeling of pain on the inner side of your knee.
tasks: Examine both knees
Check stance and gait
Check active range of movement — flexion/extension
Check passive range of movement
Check for tenderness at joint line and around margins
Check for joint effusion using patellar tap and bulge test
Check patellofemoral mobility and tracking
Check integrity of ligaments
Check quadriceps for strength and wasting
Compare symptomatic side with normal side
Examine back of joint (popliteal fossa)
openingStatement: What is wrong with my knee?
historyWithoutPrompting: History of twisting right knee six months ago when caught foot on broken pavement. Fell on right knee which became swollen and painful on inner side. Swelling caused painful limp for a few days then subsided. Since then intermittent attacks of pain on inner side of right knee with swelling settling within 24 hours. Difficulty straightening right leg fully. Occasionally apprehension twisting to the right. Otherwise well. Between attacks can walk normally with only minor pain on inner side of knee.
physicalExaminationFindings: Tenderness at inner joint line anteriorly when right knee examined. Cannot fully straighten affected knee because of pain (deficit of around 15 degrees of extension).
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's technique of physical examination of the knee joint and the accuracy of examination.
expectationsOfPerformance: Check stance and gait
Check active range of movement initially — flexion/extension (range/power) noting positive signs of inability to fully extend affected knee and medial joint tenderness
Check passive range of movement with care, ensuring that the range of active movement is not exceeded

Check for tenderness at joint line and around margins over sites of attachments of collateral ligaments and patellar ligament, identifying tenderness anteriorly at the joint line on the inner aspect of the right knee
Check for joint effusion using patellar tap and bulge test for cross fluctuation
Check patellofemoral mobility and tracking
Check integrity of ligaments appropriately: valgus and varus strain to slightly flexed joint for collaterals; anterior and posterior glide (drawer) test for cruciates
Check quadriceps for strength and wasting
Compare symptomatic side with normal side
Examine back of joint (popliteal fossa) as well as front and sides
keyIssues: Perform a focused and accurate physical examination of the knee joints (physical examination skills)
Formulate a diagnostic/differential diagnosis plan appropriate to the clinical problem (clinical reasoning skills)
criticalErrors: Failure to test movements of the left knee to compare with the other (affected) side
Failure to test ligament integrity
commentary: The knee joint is the most complex synovial joint in the body. Traumatic soft tissue internal derangements of the knee (IDK) are common after domestic, recreational and sporting injuries giving: injuries to the intra-articular cartilages (more commonly to the medial meniscus); tears of the collateral ligaments from valgus or varus strains; cruciate ligament tears; traumatic synovitis; and chondromalacia or osteochondritis dissecans. Candidates should not omit examining the back of the affected knee. Occasionally candidates may mistakenly examine the normal knee instead of the affected one after asking the patient to turn over — a moderately serious mistake induced by nervousness and lack of concentration.
underlyingDiagnosis: Probable injury to medial intra-articular meniscus (medial cartilage)
differentialDiagnosis: Injury to medial intra-articular meniscus (medial cartilage)

title: Assessment of hearing loss, first noted during pregnancy, in a 35-year-old woman
patientProfile: 35-year-old woman with hearing loss first noted during pregnancy
chiefComplaint: What is wrong with my hearing?
candidateInfo: You gave birth to your first child a month ago. About midway during the pregnancy you became aware of reduced hearing. This has become progressively worse and is the reason for you consulting the doctor today. Your infant is breastfed and thriving. You have no other complaints. You should provide family history of deafness only when asked — your mother had operations for deafness on both ears many years ago. Your father, brother and sister are not deaf. Both ears are affected. It has no other special characteristics — just getting deaf. You have not had exposure to very loud music or industrial noise. You have no past history of ear infections. You have difficulty hearing the baby cry if he is not in the same room as you are. You have to have the television volume turned up (partner complains it is too loud). You have noticed that you seem to hear a bit better when there is a lot of outside noise.
tasks: Take appropriate history including family history
Perform clinical hearing assessment
Perform Weber test with tuning fork
Perform Rinne test with tuning fork
Distinguish between conductive and sensorineural hearing loss
Advise on referral for audiometry and/or ENT opinion
openingStatement: What is wrong with my hearing?
historyWithoutPrompting: Gave birth to first child a month ago. About midway during pregnancy became aware of reduced hearing. Has become progressively worse. Infant is breastfed and thriving. No other complaints.
historyWithPrompting: Family history: mother had operations for deafness on both ears many years ago; father, brother and sister are not deaf. Both ears affected. No other special characteristics — just getting deaf. No exposure to very loud music or industrial noise. No past history of ear infections. Difficulty hearing baby cry if not in same room. Television volume turned up (partner complains too loud). Seems to hear better when there is a lot of outside noise.
physicalExaminationFindings: Weber test: sound heard equally on both sides (same on both sides). Rinne test: bone conduction louder than air conduction (bone conduction > air conduction). Air conduction tested — sound not heard after about 10 seconds. Bone conduction tested — sound heard.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of types of hearing loss and their differentiation on examination.
expectationsOfPerformance: Ability to distinguish types of hearing loss (in this patient conduction loss due to otosclerosis)
Skill in use of tuning fork tests to define types of hearing loss — conductive versus sensorineural
Ability to explain to the patient the problem and its management
Appropriate referral for audiometry and/or ENT opinion
keyIssues: Skill in use of tuning fork tests to define types of hearing loss — conductive versus sensorineural
Ability to explain to the patient the problem and its management
criticalErrors: Failure to correctly use tuning fork in assessing hearing loss
Failure to advise a referral for audiometry and/or ENT opinion
commentary: This station is predominantly a test of skill in clinical assessment of hearing loss requiring that the candidate has a basic knowledge of types of hearing loss: conductive versus sensorineural, and that conductive deafness due to wax occlusion or other causes must be excluded. The patient evinces conductive deafness due to otosclerosis. Deafness is a common problem in older people in our community. The onset of bilateral deafness in a young woman during pregnancy is uncommon. The positive family history of a mother requiring surgery for her hearing loss points towards an inherited cause, namely otosclerosis. The onset of this condition is generally in early adulthood and may progress rapidly in pregnancy. The stapes footplate becomes ankylosed in the oval window causing conductive type deafness. Patients may notice they hear more clearly in noisy surroundings. The condition can be treated by prosthetic stapedectomy and vein grafting. Sensorineural deafness occurs when there is damage to the cochlear nerve fibres anywhere from the inner ear to the cochlear nuclei. The most common cause is degenerative changes in the elderly. Other causes include a fracture of the petrous temporal bone and an acoustic neuroma. Conduction deafness is caused by blockage of the ear canal and by damage or disease of the tympanic membrane or ossicular chain or fluid in the inner ear. In the Rinne test, air conduction (AC) is normally louder and is also louder in nerve deafness. Hearing the fork louder on bone conduction (BC) indicates conductive deafness (BC > AC). In the Weber test, normally the sound is heard equally in both ears. In unilateral conduction deafness Weber test localises to the affected side; in nerve deafness to the normal side. In bilateral conductive or bilateral nerve deafness the sound will be the same. Common causes of conductive deafness are wax, otitis media, otosclerosis and Paget disease. Otosclerosis is a common cause of bilateral symmetrical hearing loss in adults. The stapes footplate is ankylosed in the oval window. The condition is familial (autosomal dominant), more common in women and worsens with pregnancy. Patients may notice they hear more clearly in noisy surroundings, whereas in perceptive hearing loss background noise worsens hearing. Investigation by audiometry will be diagnostic. The condition can be treated by prosthetic stapedectomy and vein grafting. A hearing aid is less effective for this condition and effectiveness gradually diminishes.
underlyingDiagnosis: Conductive deafness due to otosclerosis

title: An elbow injury in an 11-year-old schoolgirl
patientProfile: Emily, an 11-year-old schoolgirl
chiefComplaint: Fell at school, right elbow swollen and painful
candidateInfo: You are working in a hospital Emergency Department. Emily an 11-year-old schoolgirl, fell at school injuring her right elbow which is swollen and painful. You arranged for X-rays which have been taken and are shown below. You are interviewing Emily's mother after examining Emily and her X-rays. The elbow region was swollen, painful and tender, with marked pain on attempted movement. There were no signs causing concern on examination of the hands.
tasks: Advise the parent regarding diagnosis and treatment.
Answer questions from the observing examiner near the end of the interview.
openingStatement: Is the arm broken, doctor?
historyWithoutPrompting: Emily fell at school and now has a sore, painful swollen right elbow.
historyWithPrompting: Parent may ask: 'What treatment will she need?' and 'What about school and writing?'
patientQuestions: Is the arm broken, doctor?
What treatment will she need?
What about school and writing?
physicalExaminationFindings: The elbow region was swollen, painful and tender, with marked pain on attempted movement. There were no signs causing concern on examination of the hands.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to identify a supracondylar humeral fracture on X-ray and advise regarding treatment.
expectationsOfPerformance: Advise that Emily has fractured the arm bone (humerus) just above the elbow with minimal displacement and no complications, with excellent prognosis for full functional recovery.
Explain that a back slab/plaster/splint will be applied to the elbow with a bandage and sling, with no anaesthetic needed.
Advise pain relief with paracetamol as required, dose for age.
Explain that the elbow will need to be in plaster for 4-6 weeks.
Arrange first followup tomorrow to check plaster and fingers, and ensure comfort.
Advise parent to report earlier if hand or fingers swell further.
Recommend sleeping with arm supported on a pillow.
Encourage unrestricted use of hand and fingers with self-maintenance finger stretches.
Advise that patient can write as soon as finger movements allow and can return to school when pain eases in a day or two.
keyIssues: Recognition of fracture on X-ray.
Understanding principles and practice of management of an undisplaced uncomplicated closed supracondylar fracture.
Understanding of potential complications in this type of fracture.
criticalErrors: Missing the diagnosis of fracture
Failing to arrange appropriate review and followup.
Failure to know of risk of vascular complications in displaced supracondylar fractures.
commentary: Supracondylar humeral fractures are common in children following falls on the arm or hand. Undisplaced fractures or those with minor displacements can be treated without need for reduction by immobilising the elbow using a padded backslab or plaster (leaving shoulder and wrist and hand free to move) in the position of function of partial elbow-flexion of around 100° flexion. Severely displaced fractures will require reduction under anaesthesia and similar splintage after alignment is checked. A serious complication to be watched for is injury to the brachial artery from the anteriorly displaced upper fragment, giving ischaemia of the hand and fingers shown by pallor, insensitivity and absent radial pulse. Unless circulation is clearly restored after reduction, such ischaemia must be treated by open exploration of the fracture site and the injured artery with restoration of adequate blood flow by vascular surgery, otherwise Volkmann ischaemic contracture of forearm muscles can occur. Fortunately in the majority of cases, displacement is minor or alignment is readily corrected and no vascular complications are present; but circulation must always be checked by review after 24 hours, and parents and patients advised to report earlier if symptoms of numbness, finger swelling or severe hand pain occur. Immobilisation is usually only needed in children for 4-6 weeks and active mobilising exercises then begin.
underlyingDiagnosis: Supracondylar humeral fracture

title: Sudden onset of chest pain and breathlessness in a 20-year-old woman
patientProfile: A 20-year-old woman
chiefComplaint: Sudden onset of right-sided chest pain and breathlessness
candidateInfo: This young woman has presented to the Emergency Department of the local hospital with the sudden onset of right sided chest pain and breathlessness while walking to work. She is otherwise in good health and is a nonsmoker. Physical examination of the chest showed no definite abnormality. Her breathlessness is less now. A chest X-ray has been taken, and is illustrated below. There is NO need to take any further history from the patient NOR repeat the physical examination.
tasks: Examine and interpret the patient's chest X-ray.
Explain to the patient the diagnosis and how she should be treated.
openingStatement: I've got a bit of a pain in my chest and I feel a bit breathless
historyWithoutPrompting: Developed chest pain while walking to work. Pain is sharp and stabbing, not made worse with every breath, worse if taking a deep breath, radiating to shoulder tip, moderately severe at onset but easing now. Feeling of breathlessness (not severe) at rest as well as on exertion. Irritating dry cough - not severe or distressing.
historyWithPrompting: Nonsmoker. Drinks alcohol on social occasions only (2-4 standard drinks). No known drug sensitivities. Lives 2 km from the hospital with several others at home most of the time.
physicalExaminationFindings: Physical examination of the chest showed no definite abnormality.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to recognise the right-sided pneumothorax on the chest X-ray and explain the diagnosis to the patient. The candidate needs to reassure the patient and then explain how the problem will be managed.
expectationsOfPerformance: Diagnose pneumothorax confirmed by chest X-ray.
Advise that a pneumothorax of this size may not need active treatment.
Explain that it would be prudent and reasonable to admit the patient overnight for observation and serial X-ray.
Explain that sending the patient home if she lives nearby is a less acceptable option with a pneumothorax of this size.

Advise that recurrence is possible after spontaneous pneumothorax, with recurrence rate approximately 35% on the same side and 10-15% on opposite side, with most recurrences occurring within 12 months.
Explain that if 25-30% or less lung collapse and no symptoms — observe.
Explain that if 25-30% or less lung collapse and persisting symptoms — drain.
Explain that if greater than 30% collapse, whether symptomatic or not — drain.
Discuss aetiology of pneumothorax — rupture of bleb on surface of lung.
Explain nature of pain associated with pneumothorax — possibly due to tear of adhesion as lung collapses.
Explain associated breathlessness — depends on size of pneumothorax.
Discuss confirmatory investigation — chest X-ray diagnostic.
Discuss that inserting a catheter with a Heimlich valve is an option should the pneumothorax increase in size.
Reassure patient with anxious approach about the cause of the pain.
Recognise the pain as being of respiratory origin, rather than cardiac.
keyIssues: Correct interpretation of chest X-ray.
Explaining the diagnosis and appropriate management to the patient.
criticalErrors: Failure to identify the pneumothorax on the chest X-ray.
commentary: Spontaneous pneumothorax is usually due to the rupture of a previously nonsymptomatic bleb on the pleural surface of the lung. Symptoms of breathlessness and local discomfort are proportional to the size of the pneumothorax which is often small, in which case no active interventional treatment is required. Elective intercostal catheter drainage is indicated for a large (> 30% chest volume) initial pneumothorax or progressive increase in size on serial X-rays. The common smaller size pneumothoraces are often difficult to identify on plain X-ray, even with erect films and magnified views. Larger pneumothoraces, as illustrated, are usually easy to identify.
underlyingDiagnosis: Right-sided pneumothorax (moderate, approximately 25-30% lung collapse)

title: Atypical ureteric colic in a 25-year-old man
patientProfile: A 25-year-old driver, previously in good health
chiefComplaint: Very severe acute colicky mid-line lower abdominal pain
candidateInfo: You are a medical officer in the hospital followup clinic. A few days ago your next patient, a 25-year-old driver, previously in good health, attended the Emergency Department with very severe acute colicky mid-line lower abdominal pain. Abdominal examination was normal. Because the patient's urine tested positive for blood, a diagnosis of atypical ureteric colic was made. The pain was controlled by an injection of pethidine. A plain X-ray of the abdomen was normal so an intravenous pyelogram (IVP) was arranged. The films are available for you to review (see below), but a formal report from the radiologist has not yet been received. The patient is seeing you today to find out the result of the IVP. He is now well and has been straining his urine but no calculus has been found.
tasks: Examine the IVP film, and give a commentary to the examiner.
Explain the X-ray findings to the patient.
Advise the patient about further management.
openingStatement: I'm here to find out the result of the IVP
historyWithoutPrompting: A few days ago attended the Emergency Department with very severe acute colicky mid-line lower abdominal pain. Pain was controlled by an injection of pethidine. Now well. Has been straining urine but no calculus has been found.
patientQuestions: What does this mean for the future?
Why is my kidney in the wrong place?
Is the stone still in there somewhere?
Will I get another stone?
Is this kidney likely to develop a cancer?
physicalExaminationFindings: Abdominal examination was normal.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to interpret an X-ray of an intravenous pyelogram (IVP), explain the findings to the patient, and give further advice about future management to the patient.
expectationsOfPerformance: Interpret the IVP appropriately and indicate that the abnormality shown is a left-sided crossed fused ectopic kidney with separate calyceal systems and ureters.
Describe the abnormal position of the right kidney.
Note that there is no evidence of a calculus in either ureter on the film.
Check and confirm that no stone has been found on straining the urine.
Reassure patient that on the X-ray there is no sign of any urinary calculus.
Avoid alarming the patient about the congenital renal abnormality.
Explain that the stone is likely to have been passed spontaneously.
Advise no need to strain urine anymore.
Ensure adequate fluid intake in future especially in hot weather.
Advise to report further symptoms.
Inform future medical attendants of the renal abnormality, especially if suffering from an abdominal complaint.
Recommend estimation of serum calcium to exclude hyperparathyroidism.
Explain that the abnormal position of the right kidney would explain the atypical nature of the pain.
Reassure that the renal abnormality is of no significance and needs no treatment except awareness of its presence in the case of trauma to the left side of the abdomen or left sided abdominal pain.
Explain that the condition is congenital (present from birth) and variations in kidney position are quite common.
Reassure concerning the future, although warn that recurrence of renal/ureteric colic may occur if further stone formation occurs.
keyIssues: Interpretation of investigations — must identify abnormal position of right kidney on the X-ray.
Initial management plan — no further action required as stone has most likely passed.
Patient counselling/education — reassurance, explanation of renal abnormalities. Ensure patient awareness if any future abdominal pain occurs.
criticalErrors: Failure to describe abnormal position of right kidney.
commentary: Although intravenous urography/pyelography (IVP) is now largely replaced by computed tomography (CT) when scanning for suspected urinary calculi, in this case a urogram is used to assess the candidate's ability to interpret an X-ray finding which is quite obvious if anatomical knowledge is sound. Congenital anomalies of the kidney and its vascular and urinary drainage systems are relatively common: up to 10% of infants may be born with some anomaly of the genitourinary system. Unilateral renal agenesis (congenital absence of one kidney) occurs in about 1 in every 1000 births, and may be accompanied in a male by the absence of the vas deferens on the affected side. In the female, uterine and vaginal abnormalities commonly co-exist. The kidney begins intrauterine development in the pelvis, ascending to its adult position on the posterior abdominal wall by birth, and acquiring fresh blood supply from progressively higher blood vessels with exclusion of others as differential growth of body segmental somites occurs. One or both kidneys may be in an abnormal position. Most ectopic kidneys are pelvic in position, and may present as a pelvic mass, or be felt on rectal or vaginal examination. An ectopic kidney may be on its own side, or on the side of the normal kidney (crossed ectopia), and may be fused with the normal kidney or pelvic in site — as is this instance of crossed fused renal ectopia. In 'pancake' kidney a single pelvic renal mass is served by two collecting systems and ureters. Ectopic pelvic kidneys usually receive their blood supply from local vessels; the ureter of the displaced kidney often crosses to its own side and opens into the bladder in the normal position. As kidneys ascend from the pelvis they normally remain separate. If they come into contact and adhere, a horseshoe kidney may result, the kidneys being joined by an isthmus, which the ureters need to cross to descend. Anomalies of the urinary collecting and drainage systems can predispose to urinary obstruction from hydronephrosis or calculus. Obstructive renal and ureteric pain (renal 'colic') is often an acute, constant and unremitting severe pain felt from the site of the kidney towards bladder, penis and testis. An abnormal site of the kidney with anomalous referral of pain can cause difficulties in diagnosis until functional imaging reveals the anomalous anatomy.
underlyingDiagnosis: Left-sided crossed fused ectopic kidney with separate calyceal systems and ureters; stone likely passed spontaneously

title: Investigation for male factor infertility in a 25-year-old man
patientProfile: 25-year-old man with abnormal semen specimen
candidateInfo: The doctor will generally be expected to advise you (the husband) of the significance of the semen findings, what further evaluation is required, and what treatment is likely to be given in an attempt to achieve a pregnancy. If there is no reversible factor present, and the semen analysis does not improve with time it will be necessary for you and your wife to consider the place of IVF, or the use of donor sperm not involving the use of IVF.
tasks: Advise the husband of the significance of the semen findings
Explain what further evaluation is required
Discuss treatment options likely to be given in an attempt to achieve a pregnancy
Counsel regarding IVF or donor sperm if no reversible factor is present
openingStatement: Not explicitly stated in handbook
historyWithoutPrompting: Works in an office writing computer programs for the banking industry. Has had no contact with any chemicals. Has had no surgery to testes or inguinal region. Has never had any testicular trauma. Does not smoke and has 3-4 glasses of alcohol, usually wine, per week. Is not on any drugs and has never taken any tablets except Panadol when he has a headache. Has never used any drugs of addiction or hashish. Has never used anabolic steroids. Had mumps when aged 10 years.
historyWithPrompting: If asked about testicular involvement with mumps: there was no testicular involvement. Has not had any viral illness, or high fever, nor were given antibiotics over the last three months. Does not use saunas. If asked whether would accept the use of donor sperm: indicates 'no'. If asked whether would accept the use of IVF: indicates 'yes'.
patientQuestions: Why is my test so bad?
Can't you do something to improve it?
If there are two million sperm present, why doesn't a pregnancy occur?
Will a change in my diet help?
Will IVF be required for all pregnancies my wife and I want?
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to advise a husband with an abnormal semen specimen of the subsequent evaluation and management required for the couple to best achieve a pregnancy.
expectationsOfPerformance: One semen specimen is insufficient to make a meaningful prediction of fertility potential. Preferably three specimens obtained about three months apart are required.
It is unlikely a cause of the abnormal semen specimen will be found.
A number of blood tests should be performed to provide information as to the likely reversibility of the problem, including at least the measurement of serum FSH and testosterone levels.
If the semen analysis improves spontaneously with time, the possibility of achieving a pregnancy is increased.
There is no documented evidence for the use of hormone or other treatment in improving the semen specimen.
There is a definite place for the use of IVF, with intracytoplasmic sperm injection in the oocyte (ICSI), with a pregnancy rate of about 20-40% per cycle.
IVF without the use of ICSI has poor results (about 2-5% pregnancy rate per cycle of transfer).
There is a place for the use of donor sperm and performing artificial insemination, with pregnancy rate about 20% per cycle of insemination.
Intrauterine insemination using his poor semen sample has a very poor success rate (about 1-2% pregnancies per cycle of insemination).
keyIssues: Need for appropriate history from husband
Knowledge of appropriate tests to assess him, and of the possibility of improvement with time
Need for empathic counselling
Ability to understand that a definitive cause is unlikely to be found
criticalErrors: Failure to advise that at least a second semen specimen (3 months after the first) must be examined
Failure to recognise that persisting severe abnormality of the semen specimen as currently obtained will result in a very low pregnancy rate
Failure to understand that ICSI (within IVF) is the best method of achieving pregnancy using his genetic material
commentary: In the advice to this young man, it must be recognised that a single sperm test is unreliable as a basis on which to make a meaningful fertility prediction. This test must be repeated 2-3 months later and preferably again after a further 3 months. The comprehensive aspect of the counselling is based upon the assumption that a repeat specimen would show a similar abnormality. Common problems likely with candidate performance are: Failure to repeat the semen specimen analysis a few months later (i.e. lack of understanding that one semen analysis's result is of little predictive value). Failure to ask questions to define the possible causes of the abnormal semen specimen. Failure to ask whether the use of donor semen would be acceptable, as this is very effective and cheap, although the child produced would not obtain DNA from the husband.
underlyingDiagnosis: Male factor infertility with abnormal semen specimen

title: Acute chest pain in a 60-year-old man
patientProfile: 60-year-old overweight man presenting to Emergency Department with acute chest pain
candidateInfo: You are working in a hospital Emergency Department. You are asked to see a 60-year-old man complaining of acute chest pain.
tasks: Take a concise, relevant and focused history
Present a summary of the patient's history for the examiner, who will then give you the findings on physical examination which you request
Tell the examiner your provisional diagnosis and the reasons for this
Interpret the ECG to the examiner (the ECG will be given to you at about 7 minutes into this consultation)
Institute emergency treatment
openingStatement: I have a very bad tightness in my chest.
historyWithoutPrompting: Acute and worsening chest pain. Severe 8/10. Central, retrosternal, radiating to lower jaw. Came on two hours ago, steadily getting worse. Started when playing third set of tennis. Associated with sweating, nausea, breathlessness. Recent angina for two months. Overweight. Diabetes (5 years). Hypertension (3 years). High cholesterol (3 years). Pain in left leg on walking 500 metres (1 year). Central chest pain when walking on cold mornings for the past two months. Short of breath on exertion and breathless at night (three days). Tiredness (two days). Recent black bowel motions (five days).
historyWithPrompting: No epigastric pain, oesophageal reflux or dysphagia. If no questions about bowels, volunteer information about recent black bowel motions.
physicalExaminationFindings: Overweight man of stated age in acute distress with pain, anxious and sweating. Blood pressure 150/96 mmHg, pulse rate 96/min and regular. Heart sounds dual rhythm, no murmur. No signs of heart failure. Examination otherwise noncontributory.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to take a medical history in an older male patient presenting to the Emergency Department with chest pain of two hours duration. The candidate needs to be aware of the potential seriousness of the situation, the importance of taking a focused history to distinguish between cardiac and non-cardiac sources of chest pain, whilst being aware of the patient's discomfort and the need to take steps to relieve this. As in clinical practice, the early performance of an ECG and its correct interpretation is a key step in the assessment of this patient.
expectationsOfPerformance: Demonstrate professionalism, empathy and seek relief of the patient's discomfort with use of oxygen whilst taking the history
Remain calm, confident and reassuring whilst being aware of the potential seriousness of the situation
Fully characterise the chest discomfort, its time course, the context and associated symptoms in a sensitive and focused way using a mixture of open-ended and direct questioning
Determine cardiovascular risk factors, including the past history of myocardial infarction
Define the occurrence of this pain in the context of recent chest pain on exertion and breathlessness
Obtain history of recent 'black bowel motions' if not volunteered by patient
Provide a concise clinical summary reflecting the manner in which a junior doctor would describe the key features of the history to a registrar or consultant
Identify the most likely diagnosis as acute myocardial infarction based on key features
Correctly interpret the ECG showing features of acute inferior myocardial infarction
Identify the potential significance of the history of melaena
keyIssues: Approach to patient — sensitivity to the patient's discomfort and a calm and professional manner

The ability to take an appropriate and focused medical history showing an awareness of the likely causes of chest pain and the main characteristics that distinguish cardiac and noncardiac sources of chest pain
Appreciation of cardiovascular risk factors, and an efficient ability to characterise associated symptoms and to define the context in which the symptom of chest pain has arisen

Commentary to examiner — a succinct summary which brings together the key features of the presenting complaint, the context in which it has arisen, the associated symptoms and the cardiovascular risk factors
Diagnosis/Differential diagnosis — the candidate must consider the diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction and why noncardiac causes of the chest pain are less likely
Interpretation of investigation — the most important findings on the 12-lead ECG must be identified
commentary: The patient is an overweight, anxious looking, sweaty 60-year-old man with chest pain described as 'a very bad tightness', 8/10 severity, in the central, retrosternal region, radiating to the lower jaw but not to the arms. The pain came on when playing tennis and has been increasing steadily over the past 2 hours, associated with shortness of breath, sweating and partly relieved by anginine and oxygen. In addition he has had exertional chest pain over the past 2 months and shortness of breath, orthopnoea and tiredness over the past few days. A concerning symptom is his 5 day history of passing black bowel motions, which is suggestive of gastrointestinal blood loss. He is at very high risk of acute coronary ischaemia, having had a prior myocardial infarct, and with risk factors of diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol. ECG findings need to be checked and anaemia considered as a precipitating factor.
underlyingDiagnosis: Acute myocardial infarction

title: Palpitations and dizziness in a 50-year-old man
patientProfile: 50-year-old man presenting to Emergency Department with palpitations and dizziness over the past three days. Has not seen a doctor for the past 10 years. Last assessment showed elevated blood pressure. Current blood pressure 150/96 mmHg. Symptoms still present. Lying down on a trolley.
candidateInfo: You are working in a hospital Emergency Department. You are asked to see a 50-year-old man complaining of palpitations and dizziness over the past three days. He has not seen a doctor for the past 10 years and at that last assessment he was told his blood pressure was elevated. His current blood pressure is 150/96 mmHg. The symptoms are still present when you see him to take his history. He is lying down on a trolley.
tasks: Take a concise, relevant and focused history
Present a summary of the patient's history to the examiner, who will then give you the findings on physical examination
Tell the examiner your differential diagnosis
Interpret the ECG to the examiner (the ECG will be given to you by the examiner about 7 minutes into the consultation)
openingStatement: Not explicitly stated in handbook
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to take a focused history in a patient with palpitations and dizziness, to formulate an appropriate differential diagnosis, and to interpret an ECG.
underlyingDiagnosis: <UNKNOWN>

title: Muscle weakness and urinary symptoms in a 60-year-old man
patientProfile: 60-year-old man (newsagent, age stated as 54 in examiner instructions)
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. Your next patient is a 60-year-old man who is complaining of tiredness, weakness and urinary symptoms.
tasks: Take a history from the patient.
Ask the examiner for the findings of a selective and focused physical examination you would perform.
State to the examiner any relevant investigations you would order.
Briefly explain to the patient what you believe to be the cause of his symptoms and the first step in management (you are not expected to discuss treatment in detail).
openingStatement: I'm feeling weak and tired which is not like me. I'm also having trouble with my waterworks.
historyWithoutPrompting: Symptoms have only developed over the last six months or so. Felt tired and noted weakness in muscles over the past few weeks, not constant and not severe, all muscles affected, worse towards end of day, attributed to tiredness and increasing age. Some cramps in calf muscles. Over the last six months increasingly strong urge to pass urine when standing up after sitting. Passing urine more frequently at night, some nights three or four times with trouble starting passage of urine. Commenced treatment for mild blood pressure about eight months ago with hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg each morning. On feet all day in newsagency. Still plays tennis on Sunday but power has gone. Always kept in good health. No marital, family or financial problems.
historyWithPrompting: Stream of urine is poor, hard to finish, annoying dribbling. No loss of control or soiling. Passing urine not painful. Urine does not smell abnormally. Sexual intercourse and ejaculation not affected except reduced frequency in recent years. Learned to empty bladder before going out or sitting for long periods. Reduction in beer intake from 4-5 glasses to 1-2 has helped. Other body systems normal. No cardiovascular or other neurological symptoms, no tremor or stiffness. No weight loss. Very puzzled by muscle weakness. Concerned about urinary symptoms and worried about prostate cancer. Thought symptoms might be caused by medication.
physicalExaminationFindings: General appearance: looks well. Pallor: absent. Pulse: 72/min regular. Blood pressure lying 154/92 mmHg and standing 148/90 mmHg. Heart: normal. Abdomen: normal. Neurological examination (limited) — power of limbs possibly slightly reduced, tone normal, reflexes normal, sensation normal. PR — prostate enlarged, moderate degree, both lobes, firm but not hard, no tenderness, smooth surface, no nodularity/induration. Urine office testing: normal on chemical testing.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to take a focused history regarding muscle weakness, symptoms of prostatism and the patient's concerns about their cause, while also being aware of the possibility of an adverse drug reaction. The candidate should know the essential components of a selective physical examination and essential investigations to confirm the diagnosis and exclude other conditions.
expectationsOfPerformance: Use of appropriate communication skills to define the salient points of the history.

Identification of muscular weakness, cramps, urinary frequency, urgency and dribbling and medication (for hypertension — use of thiazide diuretic should be elicited). Patient concern about cancer should be recognised.
Request for specific physical examination findings based on diagnostic possibilities suggested by the history.
Suggestion of appropriate investigations: serum potassium, haemoglobin and full blood examination, prostate specific antigen (PSA), microscopy and culture of midstream urine.

Explanation to patient that low potassium due to diuretic use is the cause of weakness (reversible), enlargement of prostate is the cause of urinary symptoms with reassurance regarding cancer, and cessation of Dithiazide with follow-up blood pressure checks.
keyIssues: History-taking to identify weakness, prostatism, fear of cancer, current medication.
Examination for pallor, pulse and BP, heart, abdomen, neurological (limited), rectal examination.
Investigations including serum electrolytes and creatinine, PSA, FBE, ECG, urine microscopy and culture and cytology.
Explanation to patient that the likely diagnosis is hypokalaemia (reversible) as the cause of muscle weakness together with benign prostatomegaly. Reassure the patient regarding cancer.
criticalErrors: Failure to do rectal examination.
Failure to suggest appropriate investigations.
commentary: Muscle weakness and fatigue are common symptoms with multiple aetiologies. The first dominant cue is the association of tiredness and weakness with urinary symptoms. These latter symptoms and the signs of benign prostatic enlargement suggest bladder neck obstruction requiring further investigation and referral. The other dominant cue is picked up by systems review giving the information that the patient has been on a thiazide diuretic for eight months, and his symptoms of muscle weakness began after starting this medication. The diuretic polyuria may have brought to light previously nonsymptomatic prostatic pathology, and caused potassium loss. Even so-called potassium-sparing thiazide diuretics can be associated with potassium depletion, which could be contributing to his muscle weakness and muscle cramps through hypokalaemia. Investigations of serum electrolytes (particularly potassium levels), renal function tests, urine cytology and culture, and full blood examination would be mandatory in a patient of this age with symptoms as described.
underlyingDiagnosis: Hypokalaemia due to thiazide diuretic use causing muscle weakness; benign prostatic enlargement causing urinary symptoms

title: Aches and pains in a 62-year-old man
patientProfile: 62-year-old retired office worker
chiefComplaint: Aches and pains, stiffness in back, neck, shoulders and hips
candidateInfo: You are working in a general practice. Your next patient is a retiree aged 62 years. He is consulting you about aches and pains, and you have just finished taking a history, which was as follows: Over the last six weeks, he has had a gradual onset of pain across the upper part of the back, neck and shoulders which feel stiff. In the past week or so his hips are also feeling stiff and sore. Since retirement two years ago he has been playing golf at least three times per week and thought that he may have been overdoing it. At first the pain was just an aching feeling, but is now more definite pain but hard to describe. It is continuous, worsened by movement and is keeping him awake at night. The aching and stiffness is worse early in the morning and he finds it difficult to get out of bed because of muscle weakness and pain which improves during the day. Pain is not relieved by aspirin or Brufen®, nor worsened by coughing. There is no radiation to the arms. Pain is felt in the muscles but not in the joints, although these feel stiff especially after inactivity. He has not played golf for a week. He has noted a little difficulty in lifting himself up from a chair. Muscles feel 'as if they are losing their strength'. He has felt much more tired than usual over the last few weeks, especially after golf. His appetite is not as good as usual. He thinks he may have lost a little weight and sometimes feels hot and slightly sweaty at night in bed.
tasks: Specify to the examiner the essential features you would like to know from a focused physical examination of this patient. The examiner will give you the results and ask you questions about your provisional diagnosis and further investigations.
Answer the questions put to you by the examiner.
Advise the patient of your diagnostic and management plans.
historyWithoutPrompting: Over the last six weeks, gradual onset of pain across the upper part of the back, neck and shoulders which feel stiff. In the past week or so hips are also feeling stiff and sore. Since retirement two years ago has been playing golf at least three times per week and thought may have been overdoing it. At first pain was just an aching feeling, but is now more definite pain but hard to describe. It is continuous, worsened by movement and is keeping him awake at night. Aching and stiffness worse early in the morning and finds it difficult to get out of bed because of muscle weakness and pain which improves during the day. Pain not relieved by aspirin or Brufen®, nor worsened by coughing. No radiation to the arms. Pain felt in the muscles but not in the joints, although these feel stiff especially after inactivity. Has not played golf for a week. Noted a little difficulty in lifting himself up from a chair. Muscles feel 'as if they are losing their strength'. Felt much more tired than usual over the last few weeks, especially after golf. Appetite not as good as usual. Thinks he may have lost a little weight and sometimes feels hot and slightly sweaty at night in bed.
physicalExaminationFindings: Temperature 37°C, normal. Pulse 70/min regular. Blood pressure 140/80 mmHg. Muscle groups of neck, trunk, upper and lower limbs should be examined. Active movement of neck, shoulder and trunk muscles causes discomfort. Normal power and tone and coordination of movements. Examination of joints, particularly hands, shoulders, neck, sacroiliac joints and hips show no abnormalities and a full range of movement. Examination of lymph nodes, abdomen, and respiratory systems normal. Rectal examination to check prostate normal.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's knowledge of the clinical presentation of polymyalgia rheumatica and the way in which this diagnosis is confirmed or excluded.
expectationsOfPerformance: Request for essential features of focused physical examination including temperature, pulse, blood pressure, examination of muscle groups and joints, examination of lymph nodes, abdomen, respiratory systems and rectal examination.
Identification of polymyalgia rheumatica as the most likely diagnosis from the history.
Suggestion of appropriate investigations: ESR and/or CRP.

Explanation to patient that if blood tests confirm suspected diagnosis of polymyalgia rheumatica, patient is likely to have good response to limited course of prednisolone which may need to be given for up to two years.
Advice to patient to report any severe headaches, visual disturbance or pain in the jaw when eating, since giant cell arteritis can occur together with polymyalgia rheumatica.
keyIssues: Focused physical examination which must include musculoskeletal system plus rectal examination.
Investigate with ESR and/or CRP.
Polymyalgia rheumatica as the most likely diagnosis.
criticalErrors: Failure to request ESR and/or C-reactive protein.
commentary: Polymyalgia rheumatica and giant cell arteritis are linked conditions of unknown aetiology. The incidence varies with ethnicity and these conditions are more common in people of Northern European descent. Polymyalgia rheumatica commonly presents in middle aged or elderly patients with diffuse symptoms of muscle pain particularly in the neck, shoulders and hip girdles. The myalgia is symmetric and often begins in the shoulders. Muscle strength is normal but can appear diminished because of pain. There is often a disparity between the severity of myalgia reported and the physical findings. There are often constitutional symptoms including weight loss, malaise and depression; spiking fevers are rare. The diagnosis in this instance would be confirmed by investigations, specifically ESR and C-reactive protein, and full blood examination (FBE). Treatment of polymyalgia rheumatica is with oral prednisolone, initially in high dosage. Differential diagnosis to be considered would include: Chronic fatigue syndrome (medically unexplained condition, usually seen in younger patients, may follow viral infection, dominant feature is incapacitating fatigue with other medical symptoms, ESR, CRP and FBE tests are normal); 'Fibromyalgia' (medically unexplained condition, characterised by aching pains across shoulders and upper back, skin tenderness, poor sleep pattern and often additional constitutional symptoms, ESR, CRP and FBE are normal); Polymyositis (uncommon inflammatory muscle disorder that may be associated with underlying neoplasm in older patients, onset of painful muscles and proximal muscle weakness, often commencing in neck, shoulder girdle and proximal limb muscles, associated with some atrophy with disproportionate weakness, ESR, CRP and FBE abnormalities may be indistinguishable from polymyalgia rheumatica but elevated creatine kinase and abnormal autoantibodies are characteristic, positive muscle biopsy is diagnostic); Underlying malignancy (prostate, breast in females, multiple myeloma, lung cancer).
underlyingDiagnosis: Polymyalgia rheumatica
differentialDiagnosis: Underlying malignancy (prostate, breast, multiple myeloma, lung cancer)

title: Lack of energy in a 56-year-old suntanned man
patientProfile: 56-year-old industrial chemist, recently returned from holidays in Queensland, appears quite suntanned
candidateInfo: You are consulting in a general practice setting in Melbourne, Victoria. Your next patient is a 56-year-old industrial chemist. He is complaining of tiredness, although he has just returned from holidays in Queensland and appears quite suntanned.
tasks: Take a concise, relevant and focused history.

After four minutes, tell the examiner what would be the most significant clinical signs you would search for on physical examination, including office laboratory tests. The examiner will respond with these findings for this patient.
Advise the patient of your opinion about possible causes for his tiredness, and how you intend to proceed to make a firm diagnosis.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's ability to take a focused history in a patient presenting with tiredness and to consider the differential diagnosis in the context of recent travel to a tropical region.
expectationsOfPerformance: Take a concise, relevant and focused history exploring the nature and duration of tiredness.
Identify significant clinical signs to search for on physical examination based on differential diagnosis.
Suggest appropriate office laboratory tests.
Consider tropical infections and other causes of tiredness in the context of recent travel to Queensland.
keyIssues: History of tiredness in context of recent travel to tropical region.
Differential diagnosis including tropical infections such as malaria, dengue fever, and other causes of fatigue.
Physical examination findings and office laboratory tests to confirm diagnosis.
underlyingDiagnosis: <UNKNOWN>

title: Recent haematemesis in a 50-year-old man
patientProfile: 50-year-old man presenting to Emergency Department with acute haematemesis
chiefComplaint: Vomited large quantity of blood two hours ago
candidateInfo: You have been told that the patient has vomited up a large quantity of blood two hours ago. You are not expected to take a further history. You have just finished taking a history. The patient is lying on the couch undressed to underclothes and wearing a hospital gown. The HMO who has taken the history is about to examine you.
tasks: Assess haemodynamic status (blood pressure, pulse, peripheral perfusion)
Perform rectal examination looking for melaena stool
Examine for evidence of chronic liver disease (hands, face, chest, abdomen)
Look for liver palms, leuconychia, spiders, clubbing, easy bruising, spider naevi
Examine for liver flap
Palpate abdomen for hepatic and splenic enlargement
Check for ascites by palpation for shifting dullness or fluid thrill
Percuss for evidence of liver and splenic enlargement
Auscultate abdomen for venous hum, bruit, bowel sounds
Provide logical description of examination findings
openingStatement: The candidates have been told that you have vomited up a large quantity of blood two hours ago and they have been instructed to undertake a relevant physical examination.
historyWithoutPrompting: Vomited large amount of fresh blood two hours ago (approximately 500 ml). Felt temporarily faint and broke into a sweat. Has not vomited since. Wife drove to hospital where HMO took history.
historyWithPrompting: Past history of previous admission six months ago with similar episode of vomiting blood which settled spontaneously. Had endoscopy showing dilated veins at lower end of oesophagus leading into stomach. Was warned about effects of continued drinking and has been trying to give up with limited success.
physicalExaminationFindings: Blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, pulse 90/min. Fresh melaena stool on rectal examination glove.
aimsOfStation: To assess the candidate's clinical perspective in examining a patient presenting to the Emergency Department with an acute haematemesis. To check abilities to examine for evidence of chronic liver disease.
expectationsOfPerformance: Look for evidence of haemodynamic compromise (hypotension, postural drop, pulse, peripheral perfusion)
Put patient at ease and correctly position supine with appropriate exposure
Make appropriate examination looking for evidence of chronic liver disease
Examine hands for liver palms, leuconychia, spiders, clubbing
Look for easy bruising, spider naevi elsewhere, gynaecomastia, parotid enlargement
Examine oral cavity and tongue
Check for ascites by palpation for shifting dullness or fluid thrill
Percuss for evidence of liver and splenic enlargement
Auscultate abdomen for venous hum, bruit, bowel sounds
Examine for liver flap
Palpate abdomen adequately for hepatic and splenic enlargement
Check for evidence of portal hypertension (dilated veins and splenomegaly)
Look for testicular atrophy
Provide logical description concerning the examination
Perform examination in logical sequence
keyIssues: Performing a satisfactory physical examination pertinent to an episode of acute haematemesis in a patient in whom evidence of chronic liver disease should be sought
Accuracy of examination will be a key issue for the mark sheet when a real patient is involved
Satisfactory commentary to examiner
criticalErrors: Failure to assess the haemodynamic state of the patient
Failure to look for evidence of liver failure and portal hypertension
